passages.g: The fool plans his life through the seasons and never once accounts for his own death.
passages.text: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.

passages.g: Death carries off even the man celebrated for his family and wealth, catching him unprepared and distracted.
passages.text: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

passages.g: No family member or relation can protect you once death has taken hold of you.
passages.text: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.

passages.g: A wise person who understands mortality should urgently clear the path to liberation.
passages.text: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana.

passages.g: It is wise to give up a small pleasure when doing so secures a much greater one.
passages.text: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.

passages.g: Whoever seeks their own pleasure by inflicting pain on others becomes trapped in hatred and never escapes it.
passages.text: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.

passages.g: Undisciplined, heedless people neglect what should be done and do what should not, and their cravings only multiply.
passages.text: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.

passages.g: Those who watch themselves closely, avoid what should not be done, and steadily do what should, see their cravings come to an end.
passages.text: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.

passages.g: One who has truly destroyed the inner forces that bind him — even ones as commanding as parents and kings — is beyond harm.
passages.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.

passages.g: One who has truly destroyed the inner forces that bind him — even ones as commanding as parents, kings, and the eminent — is beyond harm.
passages.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.

passages.g: Those who follow this path remain fully alert, keeping their attention fixed on the awakened teacher.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.

passages.g: Those who follow this path remain fully alert, keeping their attention fixed on the truth.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.

passages.g: Those who follow this path remain fully alert, keeping their attention fixed on the community.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.

passages.g: Those who follow this path remain fully alert, keeping their attention fixed on the body.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.

passages.g: Those who follow this path remain fully alert, and their minds delight continually in compassion.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.

passages.g: Those who follow this path remain fully alert, and their minds delight continually in meditation.
passages.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.

passages.g: Every way of living has its own hardship — renouncing the world, embracing it, communal life, household life, wandering — so choosing a hard road merely for its own sake is pointless.
passages.text: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.

passages.g: A person of faith, virtue, reputation, and means will be respected wherever they choose to live.
passages.text: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.

passages.g: The good are visible from a great distance, while the bad pass unnoticed, like arrows shot in the dark.
passages.text: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.

passages.g: Persistent solitude and self-mastery bring the joy of freedom from desire, wherever one happens to live.
passages.text: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest.

passages.g: Both the person who lies and the person who denies what they have done share the same fate; both are simply men with evil deeds.
passages.text: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.

passages.g: Many who wear the outward signs of holiness are undisciplined and corrupt, and their deeds bring them to ruin all the same.
passages.text: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.

passages.g: Living off other people's generosity while refusing any self-discipline is a worse fate than physical agony.
passages.text: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.

passages.g: Pursuing someone else's partner costs you your reputation, your peace of mind, and eventually real punishment.
passages.text: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife, — a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.

passages.g: Adultery trades a brief, anxious pleasure for lasting disgrace and punishment, so it is never worth wanting.
passages.text: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.

passages.g: Self-discipline practiced badly wounds the person practicing it, just as a tool handled wrong injures the hand that holds it.
passages.text: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.

passages.g: Careless effort, abandoned commitments, and half-hearted obedience produce little worth having.
passages.text: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.

passages.g: Whatever is worth doing is worth doing wholeheartedly; a half-committed effort only stirs up the desires it was meant to settle.
passages.text: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.

passages.g: Leave the harmful act undone, because you will regret it; do the good act, because you never will.
passages.text: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.

passages.g: Guard your own character as carefully as a fortress is guarded, and do not let the moment for doing so slip past.
passages.text: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.

passages.g: People who feel shame over harmless things and none over harmful ones have their values inverted, and that inversion leads them astray.
passages.text: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.

passages.g: People who fear what is harmless and ignore what is genuinely dangerous have their judgment inverted, and it leads them astray.
passages.text: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

passages.g: People who prohibit what does no harm and permit what does have their moral sense inverted, and it leads them astray.
passages.text: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

passages.g: Seeing clearly which things are actually harmful and which are not is what puts a person on the right path.
passages.text: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.

passages.g: Bear insults in silence, because hostility from others is simply a feature of the world.
passages.text: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.

passages.g: The person who can absorb abuse without retaliating is the most valuable kind of person there is.
passages.text: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.

passages.g: Discipline imposed on anything external is worth less than discipline a person imposes on themselves.
passages.text: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.

passages.g: No external vehicle carries you to freedom; only your own disciplined self can take you there.
passages.text: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.

passages.g: A creature held against its nature takes no pleasure in comfort and wants only to return to where it belongs.
passages.text: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.

passages.g: A life spent eating, sleeping, and indulging traps a person in the same dull cycle over and over.
passages.text: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.

passages.g: A mind that has always wandered wherever it pleased can be reined in by deliberate attention.
passages.text: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.

passages.g: Watch your own thoughts closely, and haul yourself out of destructive patterns by your own effort.
passages.text: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.

passages.g: If you find a wise and steady companion, travel with them, and you will get through hardship together.
passages.text: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.

passages.g: If no wise and steady companion is available, go alone rather than settle for poor company.
passages.text: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind, — like an elephant in the forest.

passages.g: Solitude is better than the company of a fool; walk alone, wanting little and harming no one.
passages.text: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.

passages.g: Friends are a pleasure when the occasion calls for them, having done good is a comfort when you are dying, and letting go of grief is a relief.
passages.text: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.

passages.g: There is real satisfaction in the ordinary roles of parenthood and in the disciplined roles of a spiritual life.
passages.text: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.

passages.g: Integrity that lasts into old age, settled conviction, hard-won understanding, and a life free of wrongdoing are each their own reward.
passages.text: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins.

passages.g: Craving in an inattentive person spreads unchecked, driving them restlessly from one object of desire to the next.
passages.text: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.

passages.g: Once fierce craving takes hold of someone, their suffering multiplies rapidly.
passages.text: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.

passages.g: For someone who has overcome craving, suffering no longer sticks to them.
passages.text: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.

passages.g: Pull up craving by its roots, or it will regrow and overwhelm you again and again.
passages.text: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'

passages.g: Cutting back a desire without removing what feeds it means the desire simply grows back, and the pain with it.
passages.text: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.

passages.g: When craving for pleasure runs strong through every channel of the senses, it sweeps a person away.
passages.text: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.

passages.g: Desire keeps sending out new shoots, so cut it at the root with clear understanding the moment you notice it.
passages.text: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.

passages.g: Chasing ever more elaborate pleasures keeps a person cycling endlessly through the same rising and decaying life.
passages.text: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.

passages.g: Craving traps people the way a snare traps an animal, and they stay caught in it for a long time.
passages.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.

passages.g: Because craving keeps people trapped, the way out is to deliberately cultivate freedom from wanting.
passages.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.

passages.g: Someone who escapes an addiction and then returns to it has chosen bondage while holding freedom in their hands.
passages.text: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.

passages.g: The strongest bonds are not physical restraints but attachment to wealth, status, and the people you love.
passages.text: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.

passages.g: The chains that matter are the ones that give a little when you pull, feel comfortable, and are nearly impossible to undo; cutting them is what frees a person.
passages.text: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.

passages.g: People enslaved by their desires are caught in a trap they spun themselves, and only cutting it releases them.
passages.text: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.

passages.g: Let go of past, future, and present alike; a mind holding nothing is a mind that cannot be dragged back into the cycle.
passages.text: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.

passages.g: Someone who is torn by doubt, ruled by strong desires, and always reaching for what feels good only deepens their craving and tightens their own bonds.
passages.text: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.

passages.g: Someone who works at resolving their doubts and steadily contemplates what is unappealing rather than what is tempting will break free of desire.
passages.text: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, etc.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.

passages.g: The person who has arrived, who is unshakeable, free of craving and of wrongdoing, has cleared away everything that could wound them.
passages.text: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.

passages.g: Someone free of craving and attachment who genuinely understands what things mean and how they fit together has reached the end of the road.
passages.text: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.

passages.g: Someone who has understood everything, released everything, and freed themselves from craving has nothing left to be taught.
passages.text: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'

passages.g: Teaching someone the truth is the most valuable thing you can give them, and the end of craving is the deepest relief from suffering.
passages.text: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.

passages.g: A person who chases pleasure without looking further ahead destroys himself, acting as his own worst enemy.
passages.text: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.

passages.g: Craving damages people the way weeds damage a crop, so investing in those who are free of it yields the most.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.

passages.g: Hatred damages people the way weeds damage a crop, so investing in those who are free of it yields the most.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.

passages.g: Vanity damages people the way weeds damage a crop, so investing in those who are free of it yields the most.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.

passages.g: Lust damages people the way weeds damage a crop, so investing in those who are free of it yields the most.
passages.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from lust brings great reward.

passages.g: It is good to govern what you let in through each of your senses.
passages.text: Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in the nose restraint is good, good is restraint in the tongue.

passages.g: Restraint in what you do, say, and think — restraint in everything — is what frees a person from suffering.
passages.text: In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in thought restraint is good, good is restraint in all things. A Bhikshu, restrained in all things, is freed from all pain.

passages.g: A genuinely disciplined person governs their actions and their speech, is inwardly content, and needs neither company nor more than they have.
passages.text: He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who controls his speech, he who is well controlled, he who delights inwardly, who is collected, who is solitary and content, him they call Bhikshu.

passages.g: Someone who guards their speech, talks calmly and thoughtfully, and explains things clearly is a pleasure to listen to.
passages.text: The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and calmly, who teaches the meaning and the law, his word is sweet.

passages.g: Someone who lives by a principle, enjoys it, thinks about it, and acts on it will not drift away from it.
passages.text: He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the law, follows the law, that Bhikshu will never fall away from the true law.

passages.g: Do not scorn what you have been given or envy what others have, because envy makes peace of mind impossible.
passages.text: Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others: a mendicant who envies others does not obtain peace of mind.

passages.g: Someone who receives little but neither resents it nor grows lazy, and lives honestly, earns the highest regard.
passages.text: A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what he has received, even the gods will praise him, if his life is pure, and if he is not slothful.

passages.g: The truly free person does not confuse themselves with their identity or appearance, and does not mourn what is already gone.
passages.text: He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not grieve over what is no more, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.

passages.g: Living kindly and staying settled in the teaching leads to peace, the end of craving, and happiness.
passages.text: The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha, will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.

passages.g: Unburden yourself of what weighs you down; once passion and hatred are gone, you move freely toward liberation.
passages.text: O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly; having cut off passion and hatred thou wilt go to Nirvana.

passages.g: Break with the senses, abandon attachment, and go beyond both; whoever slips free of the fetters that bind people is safe.
passages.text: Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five. A Bhikshu, who has escaped from the five fetters, he is called Oghatinna, `saved from the flood.'

passages.g: Stay attentive and don't chase pleasure, or carelessness will land you in suffering you'll wish you had avoided.
passages.text: Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy thought to what gives pleasure that thou mayest not for thy heedlessness have to swallow the iron ball (in hell), and that thou mayest not cry out when burning, `This is pain.'

passages.g: Knowledge and contemplation depend on each other; only someone with both approaches liberation.
passages.text: Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation there is no knowledge: he who has knowledge and meditation is near unto Nirvana.

passages.g: Solitude with a quiet mind produces a joy beyond ordinary pleasure when the truth becomes clear.
passages.text: A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is tranquil, feels a more than human delight when he sees the law clearly.

passages.g: Understanding how the components of a person arise and pass away brings the happiness known to those who have grasped the deathless.
passages.text: As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the elements (khandha) of the body, he finds happiness and joy which belong to those who know the immortal (Nirvana).

passages.g: Begin with guarding the senses, being content, keeping discipline, and choosing companions who are decent and diligent.
passages.text: And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness over the senses, contentedness, restraint under the law; keep noble friends whose life is pure, and who are not slothful.

passages.g: Live generously and do your work well, and delight will carry you past suffering.
passages.text: Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then in the fulness of delight he will make an end of suffering.

passages.g: Shed passion and hatred the way a plant drops its dead flowers.
passages.text: As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed passion and hatred, O ye Bhikshus!

passages.g: Whoever has stilled body, speech, and mind and given up the world's lures is genuinely at peace.
passages.text: The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is collected, and has rejected the baits of the world, he is called quiet.

passages.g: Motivate and examine yourself; self-guarded and alert, you will live well.
passages.text: Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus self-protected and attentive wilt thou live happily, O Bhikshu!

passages.g: You are your own master and your own refuge, so discipline yourself as carefully as a trader trains a fine horse.
passages.text: For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self; therefore curb thyself as the merchant curbs a good horse.

passages.g: Gladness combined with a settled grasp of the teaching leads to peace, the end of craving, and happiness.
passages.text: The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.

passages.g: Someone who takes up the teaching while still young lights up the world, like a moon with no clouds in front of it.
passages.text: He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine of Buddha, brightens up this world, like the moon when free from clouds.

passages.g: Cut off desire with resolve; once you understand that everything made is destroyed, you will understand the unmade.
passages.text: Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana! When you have understood the destruction of all that was made, you will understand that which was not made.

passages.g: When a person masters both self-restraint and contemplation, knowledge dissolves all their bonds.
passages.text: If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in restraint and contemplation), all bonds vanish from him who has obtained knowledge.

passages.g: The truly noble person has no near shore, far shore, or both — being fearless and unbound.
passages.text: He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him, the fearless and unshackled, I call indeed a Brahmana.

passages.g: Nobility lies in being thoughtful, blameless, steady, dutiful, free of passion, and arrived at the highest goal.
passages.text: He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without passions, and who has attained the highest end, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

passages.g: Each thing shines in its own element and time, but the awakened mind shines constantly.
passages.text: The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior is bright in his armour, the Brahmana is bright in his meditation; but Buddha, the Awakened, is bright with splendour day and night.

passages.g: Titles of holiness are earned by what a person actually does: abandoning evil, moving peacefully, and washing away one's own impurities.
passages.text: Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana; because he walks quietly, therefore he is called Samana; because he has sent away his own impurities, therefore he is called Pravragita (Pabbagita, a pilgrim).

passages.g: No one should assault a holy person, and a holy person assaulted should never strike back; the retaliation is the worse offense.
passages.text: No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked) should let himself fly at his aggressor! Woe to him who strikes a Brahmana, more woe to him who flies at his aggressor!

passages.g: Holding the mind back from pleasure is a real gain, and when the wish to harm disappears, pain ends.
passages.text: It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back from the pleasures of life; when all wish to injure has vanished, pain will cease.

passages.g: Nobility is causing no offense in body, speech, or thought, and being restrained in all three.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word, or thought, and is controlled on these three points.

passages.g: Once you understand the truth, tend it with the same care a priest gives the sacred fire.
passages.text: After a man has once understood the law as taught by the Well-awakened (Buddha), let him worship it carefully, as the Brahmana worships the sacrificial fire.

passages.g: No one becomes holy through hairstyle, lineage, or birth; truth and righteousness make a person holy.
passages.text: A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his family, or by birth; in whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brahmana.

passages.g: Ascetic costume is useless while you are still greedy inside; cleaning the outside changes nothing.
passages.text: What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of goat-skins? Within thee there is ravening, but the outside thou makest clean.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever lives simply and alone and contemplates, whatever their outward condition.
passages.text: The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered with veins, who lives alone in the forest, and meditates, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

passages.g: Birth and wealth confer nothing; the person with nothing who is free of attachment is the noble one.
passages.text: I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his mother. He is indeed arrogant, and he is wealthy: but the poor, who is free from all attachments, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has cut every bond, never trembles, and is independent and unbound.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never trembles, is independent and unshackled.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has broken every restraint that held them and has woken up.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the thong, the chain with all that pertains to it, who has burst the bar, and is awakened.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever endures undeserved abuse, with endurance as their only strength.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no offence, endures reproach, bonds, and stripes, who has endurance for his force, and strength for his army.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without craving, self-controlled, and at the end of rebirth.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without appetite, who is subdued, and has received his last body.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever lets pleasure slide off them without sticking.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures, like water on a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on the point of a needle.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever understands the end of their suffering here and now, has set down their burden, and is unbound.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of his suffering, has put down his burden, and is unshackled.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has deep knowledge and wisdom, can tell right from wrong, and has reached the highest goal.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who possesses wisdom, who knows the right way and the wrong, and has attained the highest end.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever keeps their distance from both householders and the religious, stays out of homes, and wants little.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen and from mendicants, who frequents no houses, and has but few desires.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever finds no fault with any creature, weak or strong, and neither kills nor causes killing.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other beings, whether feeble or strong, and does not kill nor cause slaughter.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever stays tolerant among the intolerant, gentle among critics, and calm among the passionate.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the intolerant, mild with fault-finders, and free from passion among the passionate.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever lets anger, hatred, pride, and envy fall away without ever sticking.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride and envy have dropt like a mustard seed from the point of a needle.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever speaks truthfully and instructively, without harshness, offending no one.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive and free from harshness, so that he offend no one.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever takes nothing that isn't offered, no matter its size or value.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that is not given him, be it long or short, small or large, good or bad.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever wants nothing from this world or the next, leans toward nothing, and is unbound.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this world or for the next, has no inclinations, and is unshackled.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has no stake in anything and, once they understand, no longer doubts, having reached the deathless.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he has understood (the truth), does not say How, how? and who has reached the depth of the Immortal.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has risen above good and evil alike and the bondage of both, free of grief, sin, and impurity.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and evil, above the bondage of both, free from grief from sin, and from impurity.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever is serene, pure, and undisturbed, with all frivolity burned out.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure, serene, undisturbed, and in whom all gaiety is extinct.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has made it through this difficult and empty world to the far side, and is thoughtful, honest, doubtless, unattached, and content.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road, the impassable world and its vanity, who has gone through, and reached the other shore, is thoughtful, guileless, free from doubts, free from attachment, and content.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever leaves all desires behind, lives without a home, and has extinguished lust.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all desires, travels about without a home, and in whom all concupiscence is extinct.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever abandons all longing, lives homeless, and has extinguished greed.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels about without a home, and in whom all covetousness is extinct.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has escaped attachment to people, then to gods, and is free of every bond.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to men, has risen above all bondage to the gods, and is free from all and every bondage.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever has left behind both pleasure and pain, gone cool, and conquered every world.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure and what gives pain, who is cold, and free from all germs (of renewed life), the hero who has conquered all the worlds.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever understands how beings die and are reborn everywhere, and is unbound, fortunate, and awake.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the return of beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, welfaring (Sugata), and awakened (Buddha).

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever follows a path no god, spirit, or human can trace, whose passions are gone.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know, nor spirits (Gandharvas), nor men, whose passions are extinct, and who is an Arhat (venerable).

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever claims nothing as their own in past, future, or present, and is poor and unattached to the world.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether it be before, behind, or between, who is poor, and free from the love of the world.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to the courageous, noble, heroic, wise, victorious, unshakable, complete, and awakened person.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero, the great sage, the conqueror, the impassible, the accomplished, the awakened.

passages.g: Nobility belongs to whoever knows their past lives, sees heaven and hell, has ended rebirth, and is complete in knowledge and perfection.
passages.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who sees heaven and hell, has reached the end of births, is perfect in knowledge, a sage, and whose perfections are all perfect.

passages.g: Character is inherited from the examples around us: gentleness and freedom from anger, modesty and courage, piety and generosity and restraint from even intending harm, contentment with little, avoidance of the excess wealth invites, and a willingness to spend freely on good teaching.
passages.text: Of my grandfather Verus I have learned to be gentle and meek, and to refrain from all anger and passion. From the fame and memory of him that begot me I have learned both shamefastness and manlike behaviour. Of my mother I have learned to be religious, and bountiful; and to forbear, not only to do, but to intend any evil; to content myself with a spare diet, and to fly all such excess as is incidental to great wealth. Of my great-grandfather, both to frequent public schools and auditories, and to get me good and able teachers at home; and that I ought not to think much, if upon such occasions, I were at excessive charges.

passages.g: It is worth learning not to take sides in popular partisan spectacles, and to endure hard work, need little, do your own tasks, avoid meddling in too many affairs, and refuse to believe slander easily.
passages.text: Of him that brought me up, not to be fondly addicted to either of the two great factions of the coursers in the circus, called Prasini, and Veneti: nor in the amphitheatre partially to favour any of the gladiators, or fencers, as either the Parmularii, or the Secutores. Moreover, to endure labour; nor to need many things; when I have anything to do, to do it myself rather than by others; not to meddle with many businesses; and not easily to admit of any slander.

passages.g: Do not waste attention on trivialities or credit the claims of miracle-workers, sorcerers, and charlatans; instead tolerate others' frank speech and devote yourself to philosophy.
passages.text: Of Diognetus, not to busy myself about vain things, and not easily to believe those things, which are commonly spoken, by such as take upon them to work wonders, and by sorcerers, or prestidigitators, and impostors; concerning the power of charms, and their driving out of demons, or evil spirits; and the like. Not to keep quails for the game; nor to be mad after such things. Not to be offended with other men's liberty of speech, and to apply myself unto philosophy. Him also I must thank, that ever I heard first Bacchius, then Tandasis and Marcianus, and that I did write dialogues in my youth;

passages.g: Learning to prefer the plain hard bed and simple possessions of the philosophic life is itself a piece of education.
passages.text: and that I took liking to the philosophers' little couch and skins, and such other things, which by the Grecian discipline are proper to those who profess philosophy.

passages.g: Improvement begins with recognizing that your own life needs correcting. Real philosophy is practiced quietly, not displayed through showy treatises, public exhortations, ornate style, or physical feats.
passages.text: To Rusticus I am beholding, that I first entered into the conceit that my life wanted some redress and cure. And then, that I did not fall into the ambition of ordinary sophists, either to write tracts concerning the common theorems, or to exhort men unto virtue and the study of philosophy by public orations; as also that I never by way of ostentation did affect to show myself an active able man, for any kind of bodily exercises. And that I gave over the study of rhetoric and poetry, and of elegant neat language. That I did not use to walk about the house in my long robe, nor to do any such things. Moreover I learned of him to write letters without any affectation, or curiosity;

passages.g: Be quick to reconcile with anyone who has wronged you the moment they approach you again. Read carefully rather than settling for superficial understanding, and don't accept a claim just because it is commonly repeated.
passages.text: such as that was, which by him was written to my mother from Sinuessa: and to be easy and ready to be reconciled, and well pleased again with them that had offended me, as soon as any of them would be content to seek unto me again. To read with diligence; not to rest satisfied with a light and superficial knowledge, nor quickly to assent to things commonly spoken of: whom also I must thank that ever I lighted upon Epictetus his Hypomnemata, or moral commentaries and common-factions: which also he gave me of his own.

passages.g: Freedom and steadiness mean regarding nothing except what is right and reasonable. The same person can be intense and relaxed at once, and can remain unchanged through severe pain, the loss of a child, or long illness.
passages.text: From Apollonius, true liberty, and unvariable steadfastness, and not to regard anything at all, though never so little, but right and reason: and always, whether in the sharpest pains, or after the loss of a child, or in long diseases, to be still the same man; who also was a present and visible example unto me, that it was possible for the same man to be both vehement and remiss: a man not subject to be vexed, and offended with the incapacity of his scholars and auditors in his lectures and expositions;

passages.g: The best people value their own greatest talents least. Accept favors from friends without becoming beholden or more compliant than you should be, but also without appearing insensible or ungrateful.
passages.text: and a true pattern of a man who of all his good gifts and faculties, least esteemed in himself, that his excellent skill and ability to teach and persuade others the common theorems and maxims of the Stoic philosophy. Of him also I learned how to receive favours and kindnesses (as commonly they are accounted:) from friends, so that I might not become obnoxious unto them, for them, nor more yielding upon occasion, than in right I ought; and yet so that I should not pass them neither, as an unsensible and unthankful man.

passages.g: Live according to nature and be serious without affectation, paying attention to what each friend is actually like. Adapt yourself to all kinds of company without flattering them, and don't be irritated by fools or lecture people out of their ordinary opinions when they haven't asked.
passages.text: Of Sextus, mildness and the pattern of a family governed with paternal affection; and a purpose to live according to nature: to be grave without affectation: to observe carefully the several dispositions of my friends, not to be offended with idiots, nor unseasonably to set upon those that are carried with the vulgar opinions, with the theorems, and tenets of philosophers: his conversation being an example how a man might accommodate himself to all men and companies; so that though his company were sweeter and more pleasing than any flatterer's cogging and fawning; yet was it at the same time most respected and reverenced:

passages.g: A person can be entirely free of anger and other passions while remaining deeply tender-hearted. Deep learning and good reputation need not be accompanied by any display.
passages.text: who also had a proper happiness and faculty, rationally and methodically to find out, and set in order all necessary determinations and instructions for a man's life. A man without ever the least appearance of anger, or any other passion; able at the same time most exactly to observe the Stoic Apathia, or unpassionateness, and yet to be most tender-hearted: ever of good credit; and yet almost without any noise, or rumour: very learned, and yet making little show.

passages.g: Correct other people's mistakes indirectly, by using the right word yourself in your reply, rather than by openly rebuking them.
passages.text: From Alexander the Grammarian, to be un-reprovable myself, and not reproachfully to reprehend any man for a barbarism, or a solecism, or any false pronunciation, but dextrously by way of answer, or testimony, or confirmation of the same matter (taking no notice of the word) to utter it as it should have been spoken; or by some other such close and indirect admonition, handsomely and civilly to tell him of it.

passages.g: Absolute power surrounds itself with envy, deceit, and hypocrisy, and those born into privilege are often incapable of ordinary human affection.
passages.text: Of Fronto, to how much envy and fraud and hypocrisy the state of a tyrannous king is subject unto, and how they who are commonly called εὐπατρίδαι, i.e. nobly born, are in some sort incapable, or void of natural affection.

passages.g: Don't use the excuse of being busy to keep postponing the obligations you owe to friends and family.
passages.text: Of Alexander the Platonic, not often nor without great necessity to say, or to write to any man in a letter, 'I am not at leisure'; nor in this manner still to put off those duties, which we owe to our friends and acquaintances (to every one in his kind) under pretence of urgent affairs.

passages.g: Take a friend's complaint seriously even when it is unjust, and work to restore him to his old feeling toward you. Speak generously of your teachers and love your children genuinely.
passages.text: Of Catulus, not to contemn any friend's expostulation, though unjust, but to strive to reduce him to his former disposition: freely and heartily to speak well of all my masters upon any occasion, as it is reported of Domitius, and Athenodotus: and to love my children with true affection.

passages.g: Aim at a society administered by justice and equality, where nothing counts for more than the welfare of the governed. Be steady in your study of philosophy, generous without limit, hopeful, and confident that your friends love you.
passages.text: From my brother Severus, to be kind and loving to all them of my house and family; by whom also I came to the knowledge of Thrasea and Helvidius, and Cato, and Dio, and Brutus. He it was also that did put me in the first conceit and desire of an equal commonwealth, administered by justice and equality; and of a kingdom wherein should be regarded nothing more than the good and welfare of the subjects. Of him also, to observe a constant tenor, (not interrupted, with any other cares and distractions,) in the study and esteem of philosophy: to be bountiful and liberal in the largest measure; always to hope the best; and to be confident that my friends love me.

passages.g: Be open and plain with the people you criticize, so that no one has to guess at what you want or think.
passages.text: In whom I moreover observed open dealing towards those whom he reproved at any time, and that his friends might without all doubt or much observation know what he would, or would not, so open and plain was he.

passages.g: Have command of yourself rather than being pulled about by events, stay cheerful in sudden misfortunes like illness, and do your work thoroughly without complaining. Never marvel at anything, never hurry, and never drag.
passages.text: From Claudius Maximus, in all things to endeavour to have power of myself, and in nothing to be carried about; to be cheerful and courageous in all sudden chances and accidents, as in sicknesses: to love mildness, and moderation, and gravity: and to do my business, whatsoever it be, thoroughly, and without querulousness. Whatsoever he said, all men believed him that as he spake, so he thought, and whatsoever he did, that he did it with a good intent. His manner was, never to wonder at anything; never to be in haste, and yet never slow: nor to be perplexed, or dejected, or at any time unseemly, or excessively to laugh:

passages.g: The admirable person is quick to forgive, truthful, and never makes anyone feel belittled, and seems to have been upright by nature rather than to have been straightened out.
passages.text: nor to be angry, or suspicious, but ever ready to do good, and to forgive, and to speak truth; and all this, as one that seemed rather of himself to have been straight and right, than ever to have been rectified or redressed; neither was there any man that ever thought himself undervalued by him, or that could find in his heart, to think himself a better man than he. He would also be very pleasant and gracious.

passages.g: Be immovable in decisions once carefully considered, indifferent to honors, tireless in work, and willing to hear anyone with something useful to say. Give each person exactly his due, and know when severity and when leniency is called for.
passages.text: In my father, I observed his meekness; his constancy without wavering in those things, which after a due examination and deliberation, he had determined. How free from all vanity he carried himself in matter of honour and dignity, (as they are esteemed:) his laboriousness and assiduity, his readiness to hear any man, that had aught to say tending to any common good: how generally and impartially he would give every man his due; his skill and knowledge, when rigour or extremity, or when remissness or moderation was in season; how he did abstain from all unchaste love of youths;

passages.g: Don't require friends to perform ceremony for you. Examine questions thoroughly rather than being satisfied with first impressions, and when an interrupted task is resumed, return to it as the same person you were.
passages.text: his moderate condescending to other men's occasions as an ordinary man, neither absolutely requiring of his friends, that they should wait upon him at his ordinary meals, nor that they should of necessity accompany him in his journeys; and that whensoever any business upon some necessary occasions was to be put off and omitted before it could be ended, he was ever found when he went about it again, the same man that he was before. His accurate examination of things in consultations, and patient hearing of others. He would not hastily give over the search of the matter, as one easy to be satisfied with sudden notions and apprehensions. His care to preserve his friends;

passages.g: Neither tire of your friends nor be obsessively attached to them. Look far ahead and attend to small matters without fuss, refuse flattery, and bear criticism for being strict.
passages.text: how neither at any time he would carry himself towards them with disdainful neglect, and grow weary of them; nor yet at any time be madly fond of them. His contented mind in all things, his cheerful countenance, his care to foresee things afar off, and to take order for the least, without any noise or clamour. Moreover how all acclamations and flattery were repressed by him: how carefully he observed all things necessary to the government, and kept an account of the common expenses, and how patiently he did abide that he was reprehended by some for this his strict and rigid kind of dealing.

passages.g: Be neither superstitious toward the gods nor a courter of popular applause, and avoid chasing novelty. Enjoy comforts freely and without pride when they are present, and feel no want of them when they are gone.
passages.text: How he was neither a superstitious worshipper of the gods, nor an ambitious pleaser of men, or studious of popular applause; but sober in all things, and everywhere observant of that which was fitting; no affecter of novelties: in those things which conduced to his ease and convenience, (plenty whereof his fortune did afford him,) without pride and bragging, yet with all freedom and liberty: so that as he did freely enjoy them without any anxiety or affectation when they were present; so when absent, he found no want of them. Moreover, that he was never commended by any man, as either a learned acute man, or an obsequious officious man, or a fine orator;

passages.g: The praise worth having is being called mature and sound, not learned or eloquent. Governing yourself comes before governing others.
passages.text: but as a ripe mature man, a perfect sound man; one that could not endure to be flattered; able to govern both himself and others. Moreover, how much he did honour all true philosophers, without upbraiding those that were not so; his sociableness, his gracious and delightful conversation, but never unto satiety; his care of his body within bounds and measure, not as one that desired to live long, or over-studious of neatness, and elegancy; and yet not as one that did not regard it: so that through his own care and providence, he seldom needed any inward physic, or outward applications:

passages.g: Yield readily to anyone with an expertise you lack, and work to see that each is recognized for what he does best. Follow the good practices of the past without wanting credit for doing so.
passages.text: but especially how ingeniously he would yield to any that had obtained any peculiar faculty, as either eloquence, or the knowledge of the laws, or of ancient customs, or the like; and how he concurred with them, in his best care and endeavour that every one of them might in his kind, for that wherein he excelled, be regarded and esteemed: and although he did all things carefully after the ancient customs of his forefathers, yet even of this was he not desirous that men should take notice, that he did imitate ancient customs. Again, how he was not easily moved and tossed up and down, but loved to be constant, both in the same places and businesses;

passages.g: Do public works out of regard for people as people and for the fairness of the thing itself, not for the glory that follows.
passages.text: and how after his great fits of headache he would return fresh and vigorous to his wonted affairs. Again, that secrets he neither had many, nor often, and such only as concerned public matters: his discretion and moderation, in exhibiting of the public sights and shows for the pleasure and pastime of the people: in public buildings. congiaries, and the like. In all these things, having a respect unto men only as men, and to the equity of the things themselves, and not unto the glory that might follow. Never wont to use the baths at unseasonable hours; no builder;

passages.g: Care nothing for external appearances — food, clothing, ornament. Do everything methodically and unhurriedly, so that no one could say you sweated over it.
passages.text: never curious, or solicitous, either about his meat, or about the workmanship, or colour of his clothes, or about anything that belonged to external beauty. In all his conversation, far from all inhumanity, all boldness, and incivility, all greediness and impetuosity; never doing anything with such earnestness, and intention, that a man could say of him, that he did sweat about it: but contrariwise, all things distinctly, as at leisure; without trouble; orderly, soundly, and agreeably. A man might have applied that to him, which is recorded of Socrates, that he knew how to want, and to enjoy those things, in the want whereof, most men show themselves weak; and in the fruition, intemperate:

passages.g: Real strength is being able both to do without things and to enjoy them, staying moderate in either condition, where most people are weak in want and intemperate in plenty.
passages.text: but to hold out firm and constant, and to keep within the compass of true moderation and sobriety in either estate, is proper to a man, who hath a perfect and invincible soul; such as he showed himself in the sickness of Maximus.

passages.g: Much of what we take credit for is luck: good family, good teachers, and above all the absence of the circumstances that would have brought out our worst tendencies.
passages.text: From the gods I received that I had good grandfathers, and parents, a good sister, good masters, good domestics, loving kinsmen, almost all that I have; and that I never through haste and rashness transgressed against any of them, notwithstanding that my disposition was such, as that such a thing (if occasion had been) might very well have been committed by me, but that It was the mercy of the gods, to prevent such a concurring of matters and occasions, as might make me to incur this blame. That I was not long brought up by the concubine of my father; that I preserved the flower of my youth. That I took not upon me to be a man before my time, but rather put it off longer than I needed.

passages.g: A person with great power can live nearly as simply as a private citizen without becoming any less effective in the duties that genuinely require authority.
passages.text: That I lived under the government of my lord and father, who would take away from me all pride and vainglory, and reduce me to that conceit and opinion that it was not impossible for a prince to live in the court without a troop of guards and followers, extraordinary apparel, such and such torches and statues, and other like particulars of state and magnificence; but that a man may reduce and contract himself almost to the state of a private man, and yet for all that not to become the more base and remiss in those public matters and affairs, wherein power and authority is requisite. That I have had such a brother, who by his own example might stir me up to think of myself;

passages.g: Failing to make progress at some pursuit can be a blessing, since it spares you from spending your life on it. Give people the positions they want while you can, rather than putting them off with promises for later.
passages.text: and by his respect and love, delight and please me. That I have got ingenuous children, and that they were not born distorted, nor with any other natural deformity. That I was no great proficient in the study of rhetoric and poetry, and of other faculties, which perchance I might have dwelt upon, if I had found myself to go on in them with success. That I did by times prefer those, by whom I was brought up, to such places and dignities, which they seemed unto me most to desire; and that I did not put them off with hope and expectation, that (since that they were yet but young) I would do the same hereafter. That I ever knew Apollonius and Rusticus, and Maximus.

passages.g: Nothing outside you prevents you from living according to nature; if you are not living that way, you are the only cause.
passages.text: That I have had occasion often and effectually to consider and meditate with myself, concerning that life which is according to nature, what the nature and manner of it is: so that as for the gods and such suggestions, helps and inspirations, as might be expected from them, nothing did hinder, but that I might have begun long before to live according to nature; or that even now that I was not yet partaker and in present possession of that life, that I myself (in that I did not observe those inward motions, and suggestions, yea and almost plain and apparent instructions and admonitions of the gods,) was the only cause of it. That my body in such a life, hath been able to hold out so long.

passages.g: Infatuations pass and can be recovered from, and displeasure with someone need not be acted on in a way you will later regret. Consider it good fortune that you were never told there was no money to help someone in need, and never had to ask such help yourself.
passages.text: That I never had to do with Benedicta and Theodotus, yea and afterwards when I fell into some fits of love, I was soon cured. That having been often displeased with Rusticus, I never did him anything for which afterwards I had occasion to repent. That it being so that my mother was to die young, yet she lived with me all her latter years. That as often as I had a purpose to help and succour any that either were poor, or fallen into some present necessity, I never was answered by my officers that there was not ready money enough to do it; and that I myself never had occasion to require the like succour from any other. That I have such a wife, so obedient, so loving, so ingenuous.

passages.g: Help sometimes arrives from sources we cannot explain. Beginning philosophy well means not falling among sophists, not burying yourself in commentary, and not spending your time on logical puzzles or speculation about natural curiosities.
passages.text: That I had choice of fit and able men, to whom I might commit the bringing up of my children. That by dreams I have received help, as for other things, so in particular, how I might stay my casting of blood, and cure my dizziness, as that also that happened to thee in Cajeta, as unto Chryses when he prayed by the seashore. And when I did first apply myself to philosophy, that I did not fall into the hands of some sophists, or spent my time either in reading the manifold volumes of ordinary philosophers, nor in practising myself in the solution of arguments and fallacies, nor dwelt upon the studies of the meteors, and other natural curiosities.

passages.g: Nothing we have is owed entirely to ourselves.
passages.text: All these things without the assistance of the gods, and fortune, could not have been.

passages.g: Begin each day expecting to meet people who are meddling, ungrateful, insolent, deceitful, envious, and unsociable — they are that way only because they cannot tell good from bad. You share reason with every one of them, which makes them your kin.
passages.text: In the country of the Quadi at Granua, these. Betimes in the morning say to thyself, This day I shalt have to do with an idle curious man, with an unthankful man, a railer, a crafty, false, or an envious man; an unsociable uncharitable man. All these ill qualities have happened unto them, through ignorance of that which is truly good and truly bad. But I that understand the nature of that which is good, that it only is to be desired, and of that which is bad, that it only is truly odious and shameful: who know moreover, that this transgressor, whosoever he be, is my kinsman, not by the same blood and seed, but by participation of the same reason, and of the same divine particle;

passages.g: No one else can harm you, because no one else can make you do anything genuinely shameful. Human beings are made to work together, like the two rows of teeth, so resenting one another is acting against our own nature.
passages.text: How can I either be hurt by any of those, since it is not in their power to make me incur anything that is truly reproachful? or angry, and ill affected towards him, who by nature is so near unto me? for we are all born to be fellow-workers, as the feet, the hands, and the eyelids; as the rows of the upper and under teeth: for such therefore to be in opposition, is against nature; and what is it to chafe at, and to be averse from, but to be in opposition?

passages.g: You consist of body, breath, and mind: the body is mere flesh and tissue, breath is only air drawn in and let out again, and only the ruling mind deserves your attention.
passages.text: Whatsoever I am, is either flesh, or life, or that which we commonly call the mistress and overruling part of man; reason. Away with thy books, suffer not thy mind any more to be distracted, and carried to and fro; for it will not be; but as even now ready to die, think little of thy flesh: blood, bones, and a skin; a pretty piece of knit and twisted work, consisting of nerves, veins and arteries; think no more of it, than so. And as for thy life, consider what it is; a wind; not one constant wind neither, but every moment of an hour let out, and sucked in again. The third, is thy ruling part; and here consider; Thou art an old man;

passages.g: Don't let your mind be jerked about like a puppet by antisocial impulses, or waste itself resenting what is present and dreading what is to come.
passages.text: suffer not that excellent part to be brought in subjection, and to become slavish: suffer it not to be drawn up and down with unreasonable and unsociable lusts and motions, as it were with wires and nerves; suffer it not any more, either to repine at anything now present, or to fear and fly anything to come, which the destiny hath appointed thee.

passages.g: Everything that happens flows from the same whole, including what we call chance. Whatever is necessary for the preservation of the whole must be good for you, since you are part of it.
passages.text: Whatsoever proceeds from the gods immediately, that any man will grant totally depends from their divine providence. As for those things that are commonly said to happen by fortune, even those must be conceived to have dependence from nature, or from that first and general connection, and concatenation of all those things, which more apparently by the divine providence are administered and brought to pass. All things flow from thence: and whatsoever it is that is, is both necessary, and conducing to the whole (part of which thou art), and whatsoever it is that is requisite and necessary for the preservation of the general, must of necessity for every particular nature, be good and behoveful.

passages.g: The world sustains itself through the endless transformation of one thing into another. Grasping this is enough to let you die grateful and content rather than complaining.
passages.text: And as for the whole, it is preserved, as by the perpetual mutation and conversion of the simple elements one into another, so also by the mutation, and alteration of things mixed and compounded. Let these things suffice thee; let them be always unto thee, as thy general rules and precepts. As for thy thirst after books, away with it with all speed, that thou die not murmuring and complaining, but truly meek and well satisfied, and from thy heart thankful unto the gods.

passages.g: You have only a fixed and limited stretch of time to calm the disorder in your own mind, and if you don't use it, it passes away with you and never returns.
passages.text: Remember how long thou hast already put off these things, and how often a certain day and hour as it were, having been set unto thee by the gods, thou hast neglected it. It is high time for thee to understand the true nature both of the world, whereof thou art a part; and of that Lord and Governor of the world, from whom, as a channel from the spring, thou thyself didst flow: and that there is but a certain limit of time appointed unto thee, which if thou shalt not make use of to calm and allay the many distempers of thy soul, it will pass away and thou with it, and never after return.

passages.g: Do whatever is in front of you with seriousness, affection, freedom, and justice, treating each action as if it were your last. This means acting without vanity, without hypocrisy, without straying from reason, and without resentment at what has happened to you.
passages.text: Let it be thy earnest and incessant care as a Roman and a man to perform whatsoever it is that thou art about, with true and unfeigned gravity, natural affection, freedom and justice: and as for all other cares, and imaginations, how thou mayest ease thy mind of them. Which thou shalt do; if thou shalt go about every action as thy last action, free from all vanity, all passionate and wilful aberration from reason, and from all hypocrisy, and self-love, and dislike of those things, which by the fates or appointment of God have happened unto thee.

passages.g: Very few things are actually required to live well.
passages.text: Thou seest that those things, which for a man to hold on in a prosperous course, and to live a divine life, are requisite and necessary, are not many, for the gods will require no more of any man, that shall but keep and observe these things.

passages.g: Your happiness depends entirely on you, yet you spend the little life you have left making it depend on other people's opinions.
passages.text: Do, soul, do; abuse and contemn thyself; yet a while and the time for thee to respect thyself, will be at an end. Every man's happiness depends from himself, but behold thy life is almost at an end, whiles affording thyself no respect, thou dost make thy happiness to consist in the souls, and conceits of other men.

passages.g: Don't let external events distract you, and beware of a second kind of aimlessness: working hard all your life without any settled purpose to direct your efforts. Unhappiness comes not from failing to watch other people's minds, but from failing to guide your own.
passages.text: Why should any of these things that happen externally, so much distract thee? Give thyself leisure to learn some good thing, and cease roving and wandering to and fro. Thou must also take heed of another kind of wandering, for they are idle in their actions, who toil and labour in this life, and have no certain scope to which to direct all their motions, and desires. V. For not observing the state of another man's soul, scarce was ever any man known to be unhappy. Tell whosoever they be that intend not, and guide not by reason and discretion the motions of their own souls, they must of necessity be unhappy.

passages.g: Keep constantly in mind what the universe is, what you are, and how the one relates to the other. No one can prevent you from doing and saying what accords with the nature you belong to.
passages.text: These things thou must always have in mind: What is the nature of the universe, and what is mine — in particular: This unto that what relation it hath: what kind of part, of what kind of universe it is: And that there is nobody that can hinder thee, but that thou mayest always both do and speak those things which are agreeable to that nature, whereof thou art a part.

passages.g: Wrongs done from desire are worse than wrongs done from anger, because the angry person turns from reason under a kind of pain, while the person overcome by pleasure shows a weaker and more unmanly character in the very act.
passages.text: Theophrastus, where he compares sin with sin (as after a vulgar sense such things I grant may be compared:) says well and like a philosopher, that those sins are greater which are committed through lust, than those which are committed through anger. For he that is angry seems with a kind of grief and close contraction of himself, to turn away from reason; but he that sins through lust, being overcome by pleasure, doth in his very sin bewray a more impotent, and unmanlike disposition. Well then and like a philosopher doth he say, that he of the two is the more to be condemned, that sins with pleasure, than he that sins with grief.

passages.g: The angry person was first injured and driven to it by that grief, whereas the person acting from desire chose the act entirely on his own.
passages.text: For indeed this latter may seem first to have been wronged, and so in some manner through grief thereof to have been forced to be angry, whereas he who through lust doth commit anything, did of himself merely resolve upon that action.

passages.g: Act on the assumption that you may leave this life at any moment. Death is nothing to fear: if the gods exist they will not harm you, and vice — the only genuine evil — they placed within your own power to avoid.
passages.text: Whatsoever thou dost affect, whatsoever thou dost project, so do, and so project all, as one who, for aught thou knowest, may at this very present depart out of this life. And as for death, if there be any gods, it is no grievous thing to leave the society of men. The gods will do thee no hurt, thou mayest be sure. But if it be so that there be no gods, or that they take no care of the world, why should I desire to live in a world void of gods, and of all divine providence? But gods there be certainly, and they take care for the world; and as for those things which be truly evil, as vice and wickedness, such things they have put in a man's own power, that he might avoid them if he would:

passages.g: Nothing that cannot make you a better or worse person can genuinely harm your life. It is absurd to suppose that the nature of the universe overlooked such things through ignorance or was powerless to arrange them otherwise.
passages.text: and had there been anything besides that had been truly bad and evil, they would have had a care of that also, that a man might have avoided it. But why should that be thought to hurt and prejudice a man's life in this world, which cannot any ways make man himself the better, or the worse in his own person? Neither must we think that the nature of the universe did either through ignorance pass these things, or if not as ignorant of them, yet as unable either to prevent, or better to order and dispose them.

passages.g: Life and death, honor and dishonor, pain and pleasure, wealth and poverty fall alike on good and bad people, which proves they are in themselves neither good nor bad.
passages.text: It cannot be that she through want either of power or skill, should have committed such a thing, so as to suffer all things both good and bad, equally and promiscuously, to happen unto all both good and bad. As for life therefore, and death, honour and dishonour, labour and pleasure, riches and poverty, all these things happen unto men indeed, both good and bad, equally; but as things which of themselves are neither good nor bad; because of themselves, neither shameful nor praiseworthy.

passages.g: Everything dissolves quickly — bodies back into matter, reputations into time. The things that tempt us, frighten us, or impress us turn out on inspection to be worthless and without substance.
passages.text: Consider how quickly all things are dissolved and resolved: the bodies and substances themselves, into the matter and substance of the world: and their memories into the general age and time of the world. Consider the nature of all worldly sensible things; of those especially, which either ensnare by pleasure, or for their irksomeness are dreadful, or for their outward lustre and show are in great esteem and request, how vile and contemptible, how base and corruptible, how destitute of all true life and being they are.

passages.g: Look closely at the people whose opinions confer honor, and you will see what honor is worth. Death, stripped of the associations we add to it, is just a natural process, and fearing nature's workings is childish.
passages.text: It is the part of a man endowed with a good understanding faculty, to consider what they themselves are in very deed, from whose bare conceits and voices, honour and credit do proceed: as also what it is to die, and how if a man shall consider this by itself alone, to die, and separate from it in his mind all those things which with it usually represent themselves unto us, he can conceive of it no otherwise, than as of a work of nature, and he that fears any work of nature, is a very child. Now death, it is not only a work of nature, but also conducing to nature.

passages.g: Nothing is more wretched than a mind that ranges over the whole world and probes at other people's thoughts, while failing to see that tending the spirit within itself would be enough.
passages.text: Consider with thyself how man, and by what part of his, is joined unto God, and how that part of man is affected, when it is said to be diffused. There is nothing more wretched than that soul, which in a kind of circuit compasseth all things, searching (as he saith) even the very depths of the earth; and by all signs and conjectures prying into the very thoughts of other men's souls; and yet of this, is not sensible, that it is sufficient for a man to apply himself wholly, and to confine all his thoughts and cares to the tendance of that spirit which is within him, and truly and really to serve him.

passages.g: To serve the spirit within you is to keep free of violent passion, rashness, vanity, and discontent with gods or people. What comes from others should be met with love, and — when it springs from their inability to tell good from bad — with pity.
