passages.g: A prudent person sees trouble coming and takes cover, while the naive walk straight into it and pay for it. Humility and reverence are what bring wealth, honor, and life.
passages.text: A prudent man foreseeth the evil, and hideth himself: but the simple pass on, and are punished. By humility and the fear of the LORD are riches, and honour, and life.

passages.g: The crooked path is full of thorns and traps, and guarding yourself keeps you far from them. Set a child's direction early and it will stay with them for life.
passages.text: Thorns and snares are in the way of the froward: he that doth keep his soul shall be far from them. Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.

passages.g: The rich rule the poor, and debt turns a borrower into a servant. Whoever sows injustice reaps nothing, and the power behind their anger finally fails.
passages.text: The rich ruleth over the poor, and the borrower is servant to the lender. He that soweth iniquity shall reap vanity: and the rod of his anger shall fail.

passages.g: The generous are blessed because they share their food with the poor. Get rid of the mocker and the quarreling, insults, and strife leave with him.
passages.text: He that hath a bountiful eye shall be blessed; for he giveth of his bread to the poor. Cast out the scorner, and contention shall go out; yea, strife and reproach shall cease.

passages.g: Sincerity of heart and gracious speech win the friendship of the powerful. Truth is preserved and deceit is overturned.
passages.text: He that loveth pureness of heart, for the grace of his lips the king shall be his friend. The eyes of the LORD preserve knowledge, and he overthroweth the words of the transgressor.

passages.g: The lazy invent dangers outside to justify staying in. Seductive talk is a deep pit, and those already ruined fall into it.
passages.text: The slothful man saith, There is a lion without, I shall be slain in the streets. The mouth of strange women is a deep pit: he that is abhorred of the LORD shall fall therein.

passages.g: Foolishness is bound up in a child's heart, and discipline drives it out. Exploiting the poor to get rich, or lavishing gifts on the rich, both end in poverty.
passages.text: Foolishness is bound in the heart of a child; but the rod of correction shall drive it far from him. He that oppresseth the poor to increase his riches, and he that giveth to the rich, shall surely come to want.

passages.g: Listen carefully to wise teaching and take it into yourself, because holding it inside is a pleasure and keeps it ready on your lips.
passages.text: Bow down thine ear, and hear the words of the wise, and apply thine heart unto my knowledge. For it is a pleasant thing if thou keep them within thee; they shall withal be fitted in thy lips.

passages.g: Excellent counsel and knowledge are set down and taught so that your confidence can rest on something trustworthy.
passages.text: That thy trust may be in the LORD, I have made known to thee this day, even to thee. Have not I written to thee excellent things in counsels and knowledge,

passages.g: Wisdom is taught so you can know what is reliably true and answer soundly to whoever asks. Don't exploit the poor merely because they are poor, or crush the afflicted in court.
passages.text: That I might make thee know the certainty of the words of truth; that thou mightest answer the words of truth to them that send unto thee? Rob not the poor, because he is poor: neither oppress the afflicted in the gate:

passages.g: Those who prey on the defenseless will be called to account and lose what they took. Don't make friends with an angry, violent man, or you will pick up his habits and trap yourself.
passages.text: For the LORD will plead their cause, and spoil the soul of those that spoiled them. Make no friendship with an angry man; and with a furious man thou shalt not go: Lest thou learn his ways, and get a snare to thy soul.

passages.g: Don't guarantee other people's debts, because if you can't pay, even the bed under you will be taken. Don't move the boundary markers your ancestors set.
passages.text: Be not thou one of them that strike hands, or of them that are sureties for debts. If thou hast nothing to pay, why should he take away thy bed from under thee? Remove not the ancient landmark, which thy fathers have set.

passages.g: Someone genuinely skilled and diligent at their work will end up serving the highest people, not obscure ones.
passages.text: Seest thou a man diligent in his business? he shall stand before kings; he shall not stand before mean men.

passages.g: When you eat at a powerful person's table, pay close attention and curb your appetite, because their lavish hospitality is bait rather than kindness.
passages.text: When thou sittest to eat with a ruler, consider diligently what is before thee: And put a knife to thy throat, if thou be a man given to appetite. Be not desirous of his dainties: for they are deceitful meat.

passages.g: Don't wear yourself out chasing wealth, and don't trust your own cleverness about it. Riches sprout wings and fly away.
passages.text: Labour not to be rich: cease from thine own wisdom. Wilt thou set thine eyes upon that which is not? for riches certainly make themselves wings; they fly away as an eagle toward heaven.

passages.g: Don't accept food from a grudging host, because a person is what they privately think, and his invitation is not what his heart is saying.
passages.text: Eat thou not the bread of him that hath an evil eye, neither desire thou his dainty meats: For as he thinketh in his heart, so is he: Eat and drink, saith he to thee; but his heart is not with thee.

passages.g: What a grudging host gives you will make you sick, and your polite words to him will be wasted. Don't speak wisdom to a fool, because he will only despise it.
passages.text: The morsel which thou hast eaten shalt thou vomit up, and lose thy sweet words. Speak not in the ears of a fool: for he will despise the wisdom of thy words.

passages.g: Don't move old boundaries or seize the property of the powerless, because they have a defender stronger than you who will take up their case.
passages.text: Remove not the old landmark; and enter not into the fields of the fatherless: For their redeemer is mighty; he shall plead their cause with thee.

passages.g: Give your full attention to instruction and knowledge. Don't withhold correction from a child; discipline will not destroy them.
passages.text: Apply thine heart unto instruction, and thine ears to the words of knowledge. Withhold not correction from the child: for if thou beatest him with the rod, he shall not die.

passages.g: Disciplining a child rescues them from ruin. A parent takes deep, visceral joy in a child who becomes wise and speaks rightly.
passages.text: Thou shalt beat him with the rod, and shalt deliver his soul from hell. My son, if thine heart be wise, my heart shall rejoice, even mine. Yea, my reins shall rejoice, when thy lips speak right things.

passages.g: Don't envy people who do wrong; live with steady reverence, because there is an outcome ahead and your hope will not be disappointed.
passages.text: Let not thine heart envy sinners: but be thou in the fear of the LORD all the day long. For surely there is an end; and thine expectation shall not be cut off.

passages.g: Listen to guidance and steer your own heart, and avoid the company of heavy drinkers and gluttons.
passages.text: Hear thou, my son, and be wise, and guide thine heart in the way. Be not among winebibbers; among riotous eaters of flesh:

passages.g: Excess in drink and food, and habitual drowsiness, end in poverty; honor your parents throughout their whole lives.
passages.text: For the drunkard and the glutton shall come to poverty: and drowsiness shall clothe a man with rags. Hearken unto thy father that begat thee, and despise not thy mother when she is old.

passages.g: Acquire truth, wisdom, and understanding at any cost and never trade them away; a wise child is a lasting joy to their parents.
passages.text: Buy the truth, and sell it not; also wisdom, and instruction, and understanding. The father of the righteous shall greatly rejoice: and he that begetteth a wise child shall have joy of him.

passages.g: Give your full attention to the way of wisdom, because illicit attachments are pits that are easy to fall into and nearly impossible to climb out of.
passages.text: Thy father and thy mother shall be glad, and she that bare thee shall rejoice. My son, give me thine heart, and let thine eyes observe my ways. For a whore is a deep ditch; and a strange woman is a narrow pit.

passages.g: Temptation lies in wait and multiplies wrongdoing; misery, conflict, pointless injury, and ruined health all trace back to a common source.
passages.text: She also lieth in wait as for a prey, and increaseth the transgressors among men. Who hath woe? who hath sorrow? who hath contentions? who hath babbling? who hath wounds without cause? who hath redness of eyes?

passages.g: It is those who linger over drink who suffer these things, so don't be seduced by alcohol's immediate appeal.
passages.text: They that tarry long at the wine; they that go to seek mixed wine. Look not thou upon the wine when it is red, when it giveth his colour in the cup, when it moveth itself aright.

passages.g: Alcohol's pleasure comes first and its damage after, distorting what you see and corrupting what you say.
passages.text: At the last it biteth like a serpent, and stingeth like an adder. Thine eyes shall behold strange women, and thine heart shall utter perverse things.

passages.g: Drunkenness leaves you disoriented and unsteady, as though you had lost all solid footing.
passages.text: Yea, thou shalt be as he that lieth down in the midst of the sea, or as he that lieth upon the top of a mast.

passages.g: Addiction numbs you to the harm it does and leaves you reaching for more the moment you come to.
passages.text: They have stricken me, shalt thou say, and I was not sick; they have beaten me, and I felt it not: when shall I awake? I will seek it yet again.

passages.g: Don't envy or seek out people bent on harm, since their minds run on destruction; it is wisdom and understanding that actually build a life.
passages.text: Be not thou envious against evil men, neither desire to be with them. For their heart studieth destruction, and their lips talk of mischief. Through wisdom is an house builded; and by understanding it is established:

passages.g: Knowledge fills a life with real value, and understanding is a genuine source of strength.
passages.text: And by knowledge shall the chambers be filled with all precious and pleasant riches. A wise man is strong; yea, a man of knowledge increaseth strength.

passages.g: You succeed in conflict through good counsel, and safety lies in having many advisers; wisdom is out of a fool's reach, and he has nothing to say where it counts.
passages.text: For by wise counsel thou shalt make thy war: and in multitude of counsellors there is safety. Wisdom is too high for a fool: he openeth not his mouth in the gate.

passages.g: Plotting harm earns you the name you deserve, and folly is itself a moral failure; if you collapse under pressure, your strength was never real.
passages.text: He that deviseth to do evil shall be called a mischievous person. The thought of foolishness is sin: and the scorner is an abomination to men. If thou faint in the day of adversity, thy strength is small.

passages.g: When you can rescue people headed toward destruction, you are responsible for doing it.
passages.text: If thou forbear to deliver them that are drawn unto death, and those that are ready to be slain;

passages.g: Claiming you didn't know is no defense, because your actual motives are visible and you will answer for what you did.
passages.text: If thou sayest, Behold, we knew it not; doth not he that pondereth the heart consider it? and he that keepeth thy soul, doth not he know it? and shall not he render to every man according to his works?

passages.g: Wisdom is worth pursuing for the same reason sweetness is: it is genuinely good.
passages.text: My son, eat thou honey, because it is good; and the honeycomb, which is sweet to thy taste:

passages.g: Wisdom rewards whoever finds it, and hope grounded in it will not be disappointed.
passages.text: So shall the knowledge of wisdom be unto thy soul: when thou hast found it, then there shall be a reward, and thy expectation shall not be cut off.

passages.g: Don't prey on decent people; the good person recovers from repeated setbacks, while the wicked are undone by their own.
passages.text: Lay not wait, O wicked man, against the dwelling of the righteous; spoil not his resting place: For a just man falleth seven times, and riseth up again: but the wicked shall fall into mischief.

passages.g: Don't take pleasure in an enemy's downfall.
passages.text: Rejoice not when thine enemy falleth, and let not thine heart be glad when he stumbleth: Lest the LORD see it, and it displease him, and he turn away his wrath from him.

passages.g: Don't be agitated or envious over the success of bad people, because it doesn't last.
passages.text: Fret not thyself because of evil men, neither be thou envious at the wicked: For there shall be no reward to the evil man; the candle of the wicked shall be put out.

passages.g: Respect legitimate authority and steer clear of those addicted to upheaval, since ruin comes on them without warning.
passages.text: My son, fear thou the LORD and the king: and meddle not with them that are given to change: For their calamity shall rise suddenly; and who knoweth the ruin of them both?

passages.g: Favoritism in judgment is wrong, and whoever declares the guilty innocent earns universal contempt.
passages.text: These things also belong to the wise. It is not good to have respect of persons in judgment. He that saith unto the wicked, Thou are righteous; him shall the people curse, nations shall abhor him:

passages.g: Those who confront wrongdoing are valued and rewarded, and an honest answer is received like an act of affection.
passages.text: But to them that rebuke him shall be delight, and a good blessing shall come upon them. Every man shall kiss his lips that giveth a right answer.

passages.g: Put the work that sustains you in order before you build your comforts; and never testify against someone without cause.
passages.text: Prepare thy work without, and make it fit for thyself in the field; and afterwards build thine house. Be not a witness against thy neighbour without cause; and deceive not with thy lips.

passages.g: Don't repay harm with harm. Neglect shows itself plainly in what a person allows to fall apart.
passages.text: Say not, I will do so to him as he hath done to me: I will render to the man according to his work. I went by the field of the slothful, and by the vineyard of the man void of understanding;

passages.g: A field overgrown and its wall collapsed is decay produced by inattention, and watching it happen is itself an education.
passages.text: And, lo, it was all grown over with thorns, and nettles had covered the face thereof, and the stone wall thereof was broken down. Then I saw, and considered it well: I looked upon it, and received instruction.

passages.g: Small, repeated indulgences of rest accumulate until poverty arrives suddenly and cannot be resisted.
passages.text: Yet a little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to sleep: So shall thy poverty come as one that travelleth; and thy want as an armed man.

passages.g: Some truths stay hidden, and the honor of a serious person lies in searching them out.
passages.text: These are also proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out. It is the glory of God to conceal a thing: but the honour of kings is to search out a matter.

passages.g: Some things are past finding out. Removing what is impure is exactly what makes the rest valuable.
passages.text: The heaven for height, and the earth for depth, and the heart of kings is unsearchable. Take away the dross from the silver, and there shall come forth a vessel for the finer.

passages.g: Remove corruption from power and power becomes stable; and don't push yourself forward among important people.
passages.text: Take away the wicked from before the king, and his throne shall be established in righteousness. Put not forth thyself in the presence of the king, and stand not in the place of great men:

passages.g: It is better to be invited up than to be publicly moved down.
passages.text: For better it is that it be said unto thee, Come up hither; than that thou shouldest be put lower in the presence of the prince whom thine eyes have seen.

passages.g: Don't rush into a fight you don't know how to finish; settle a dispute with the person directly and don't expose what was told you in confidence.
passages.text: Go not forth hastily to strive, lest thou know not what to do in the end thereof, when thy neighbour hath put thee to shame. Debate thy cause with thy neighbour himself; and discover not a secret to another:

passages.g: Betraying a confidence brings shame that never quite lifts. A word spoken at exactly the right moment is beautiful and valuable.
passages.text: Lest he that heareth it put thee to shame, and thine infamy turn not away. A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in pictures of silver.

passages.g: Wise correction landing on someone willing to hear it is a rare and precious thing.
passages.text: As an earring of gold, and an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise reprover upon an obedient ear.

passages.g: A reliable messenger restores the people who sent him, while someone who boasts about gifts he never gives is all promise and no delivery.
passages.text: As the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to them that send him: for he refresheth the soul of his masters. Whoso boasteth himself of a false gift is like clouds and wind without rain.

passages.g: Patience and gentle speech wear down hard resistance. Even genuinely good things become harmful in excess.
passages.text: By long forbearing is a prince persuaded, and a soft tongue breaketh the bone. Hast thou found honey? eat so much as is sufficient for thee, lest thou be filled therewith, and vomit it.

passages.g: Don't wear out your welcome, or affection turns to resentment. Lying about someone is a weapon that inflicts real injury.
passages.text: Withdraw thy foot from thy neighbour’s house; lest he be weary of thee, and so hate thee. A man that beareth false witness against his neighbour is a maul, and a sword, and a sharp arrow.

passages.g: Relying on an untrustworthy person in a crisis fails you precisely at the moment you put weight on it.
passages.text: Confidence in an unfaithful man in time of trouble is like a broken tooth, and a foot out of joint.

passages.g: Forced cheerfulness aimed at someone in grief is a cruelty, not a kindness. Meet even an enemy's hunger and thirst with generosity.
passages.text: As he that taketh away a garment in cold weather, and as vinegar upon nitre, so is he that singeth songs to an heavy heart. If thine enemy be hungry, give him bread to eat; and if he be thirsty, give him water to drink:

passages.g: Kindness shown to an enemy shames him and returns to you. Visible disapproval silences gossip the way a change in the wind clears away rain.
passages.text: For thou shalt heap coals of fire upon his head, and the LORD shall reward thee. The north wind driveth away rain: so doth an angry countenance a backbiting tongue.

passages.g: Solitude in a cramped place beats comfort shared with constant conflict; good news from far away revives you like water when you're parched.
passages.text: It is better to dwell in the corner of the housetop, than with a brawling woman and in a wide house. As cold waters to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country.

passages.g: A good person giving way before the wicked is a fouled water source everyone depended on. Chasing your own glory is not glory.
passages.text: A righteous man falling down before the wicked is as a troubled fountain, and a corrupt spring. It is not good to eat much honey: so for men to search their own glory is not glory.

passages.g: A person with no control over their own impulses has no defenses at all.
passages.text: He that hath no rule over his own spirit is like a city that is broken down, and without walls.

passages.g: Honor sits wrongly on a fool, like weather out of season. A curse with no cause behind it never lands.
passages.text: As snow in summer, and as rain in harvest, so honour is not seemly for a fool. As the bird by wandering, as the swallow by flying, so the curse causeless shall not come.

passages.g: Fools respond only to blunt correction. Don't answer foolishness on its own terms, or you become what you're arguing with.
passages.text: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool’s back. Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest thou also be like unto him.

passages.g: Sometimes you must answer foolishness on its own terms so it doesn't go unchallenged and confirm itself. Trusting a fool with your message cripples your own purposes.
passages.text: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own conceit. He that sendeth a message by the hand of a fool cutteth off the feet, and drinketh damage.

passages.g: Wisdom in a fool's mouth limps and carries no weight. Giving honor to a fool defeats the purpose of honor.
passages.text: The legs of the lame are not equal: so is a parable in the mouth of fools. As he that bindeth a stone in a sling, so is he that giveth honour to a fool.

passages.g: A wise saying in the wrong hands does damage rather than good. Folly and wrongdoing both receive what they've earned.
passages.text: As a thorn goeth up into the hand of a drunkard, so is a parable in the mouths of fools. The great God that formed all things both rewardeth the fool, and rewardeth transgressors.

passages.g: A fool returns to the same folly again and again; but the person convinced of their own wisdom is further beyond help than a fool.
passages.text: As a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly. Seest thou a man wise in his own conceit? there is more hope of a fool than of him.

passages.g: The lazy invent dangers to justify not moving, and turn endlessly in place without going anywhere.
passages.text: The slothful man saith, There is a lion in the way; a lion is in the streets. As the door turneth upon his hinges, so doth the slothful upon his bed.

passages.g: Laziness makes even the smallest effort feel impossible, yet the lazy person believes he sees more clearly than many people who can actually think.
passages.text: The slothful hideth his hand in his bosom; it grieveth him to bring it again to his mouth. The sluggard is wiser in his own conceit than seven men that can render a reason.

passages.g: Inserting yourself into a quarrel that isn't yours invites injury, just as recklessness does.
passages.text: He that passeth by, and meddleth with strife belonging not to him, is like one that taketh a dog by the ears. As a mad man who casteth firebrands, arrows, and death,

passages.g: Deceiving someone and calling it a joke is genuine harm. Conflict burns only as long as gossip feeds it; remove the gossip and it goes out.
passages.text: So is the man that deceiveth his neighbour, and saith, Am not I in sport? Where no wood is, there the fire goeth out: so where there is no talebearer, the strife ceaseth.

passages.g: A quarrelsome person exists to keep conflict burning. Gossip's words lodge deep inside a person and stay there as wounds.
passages.text: As coals are to burning coals, and wood to fire; so is a contentious man to kindle strife. The words of a talebearer are as wounds, and they go down into the innermost parts of the belly.

passages.g: Warm words over a malicious heart are cheap plating on cheap material. Hatred disguises itself in pleasant speech and stores up deceit.
passages.text: Burning lips and a wicked heart are like a potsherd covered with silver dross. He that hateth dissembleth with his lips, and layeth up deceit within him;

passages.g: Don't believe flattering words from someone who hates you, however convincing; concealed malice is eventually exposed to everyone.
passages.text: When he speaketh fair, believe him not: for there are seven abominations in his heart. Whose hatred is covered by deceit, his wickedness shall be shewed before the whole congregation.

passages.g: The harm you engineer for others comes back onto you. Lies and flattery destroy the very people they touch.
passages.text: Whoso diggeth a pit shall fall therein: and he that rolleth a stone, it will return upon him. A lying tongue hateth those that are afflicted by it; and a flattering mouth worketh ruin.

passages.g: Don't boast about a future you cannot predict. Let praise come from someone else's mouth rather than your own.
passages.text: Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth. Let another man praise thee, and not thine own mouth; a stranger, and not thine own lips.

passages.g: A fool's rage is a heavier thing to carry than stone or sand. Anger is cruel and rage overwhelming, but envy is the hardest of all to stand against; and open criticism is better than affection that never says anything.
passages.text: A stone is heavy, and the sand weighty; but a fool’s wrath is heavier than them both. Wrath is cruel, and anger is outrageous; but who is able to stand before envy? Open rebuke is better than secret love.

passages.g: Honest criticism from someone who cares about you is more valuable than affectionate flattery from someone who does not. What you value depends on your need: when you are satisfied even good things seem worthless, and when you are desperate even bad things seem acceptable.
passages.text: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful. The full soul loatheth an honeycomb; but to the hungry soul every bitter thing is sweet.

passages.g: A person who abandons the place where they belong loses their bearings. Sincere advice from a friend is a genuine comfort.
passages.text: As a bird that wandereth from her nest, so is a man that wandereth from his place. Ointment and perfume rejoice the heart: so doth the sweetness of a man’s friend by hearty counsel.

passages.g: Maintain long-standing friendships rather than trading them for new ones, because in a crisis someone close at hand helps more than family who are far away.
passages.text: Thine own friend, and thy father’s friend, forsake not; neither go into thy brother’s house in the day of thy calamity: for better is a neighbour that is near than a brother far off.

passages.g: A child who lives wisely vindicates the parent against critics. Prudent people see trouble coming and take cover, while the naive walk into it and suffer for it.
passages.text: My son, be wise, and make my heart glad, that I may answer him that reproacheth me. A prudent man foreseeth the evil, and hideth himself; but the simple pass on, and are punished.

passages.g: Demand security from anyone who guarantees a stranger's debt. Praise that is too loud and too eager is heard as mockery rather than as a compliment.
passages.text: Take his garment that is surety for a stranger, and take a pledge of him for a strange woman. He that blesseth his friend with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, it shall be counted a curse to him.

passages.g: Constant complaining wears a person down the way steady dripping does, and it cannot be contained or concealed any more than wind can be held.
passages.text: A continual dropping in a very rainy day and a contentious woman are alike. Whosoever hideth her hideth the wind, and the ointment of his right hand, which bewrayeth itself.

passages.g: People sharpen and improve one another through direct contact. Whoever tends something faithfully receives the benefit of it.
passages.text: Iron sharpeneth iron; so a man sharpeneth the countenance of his friend. Whoso keepeth the fig tree shall eat the fruit thereof: so he that waiteth on his master shall be honoured.

passages.g: People recognize themselves in one another because human hearts work alike. Human desire has no natural limit and is never satisfied.
passages.text: As in water face answereth to face, so the heart of man to man. Hell and destruction are never full; so the eyes of man are never satisfied.

passages.g: A person's character is revealed by how they handle praise, the way heat reveals the purity of metal. Foolishness is not cured by punishment, however severe.
passages.text: As the fining pot for silver, and the furnace for gold; so is a man to his praise. Though thou shouldest bray a fool in a mortar among wheat with a pestle, yet will not his foolishness depart from him.

passages.g: Know the actual condition of what you own and manage it attentively, because wealth and position are never permanent.
passages.text: Be thou diligent to know the state of thy flocks, and look well to thy herds. For riches are not for ever: and doth the crown endure to every generation?

passages.g: Resources come in seasons and must be gathered when they appear. What you raise and tend is what clothes you and buys what you need.
passages.text: The hay appeareth, and the tender grass sheweth itself, and herbs of the mountains are gathered. The lambs are for thy clothing, and the goats are the price of the field.

passages.g: Careful management of what you have provides enough to sustain everyone who depends on you.
passages.text: And thou shalt have goats’ milk enough for thy food, for the food of thy household, and for the maintenance for thy maidens.

passages.g: Guilt makes people fearful without cause, while integrity makes them confident. A corrupt society churns through many rulers, but one capable leader can give it stability.
passages.text: The wicked flee when no man pursueth: but the righteous are bold as a lion. For the transgression of a land many are the princes thereof: but by a man of understanding and knowledge the state thereof shall be prolonged.

passages.g: A person with little power who exploits others still ruins them completely. Those who abandon principle end up defending wrongdoing, and those who keep it end up resisting it.
passages.text: A poor man that oppresseth the poor is like a sweeping rain which leaveth no food. They that forsake the law praise the wicked: but such as keep the law contend with them.

passages.g: People committed to wrongdoing cannot understand justice, while those seeking what is right come to understand it fully. Poverty with integrity is better than wealth with corruption.
passages.text: Evil men understand not judgment: but they that seek the LORD understand all things. Better is the poor that walketh in his uprightness, than he that is perverse in his ways, though he be rich.

passages.g: Living by principle honors your family, while running with dissipated company shames it. Wealth built on exploitation is eventually inherited by someone who uses it generously.
passages.text: Whoso keepeth the law is a wise son: but he that is a companion of riotous men shameth his father. He that by usury and unjust gain increaseth his substance, he shall gather it for him that will pity the poor.

passages.g: Someone who refuses to listen to moral instruction cannot expect their own appeals to be taken seriously.
passages.text: He that turneth away his ear from hearing the law, even his prayer shall be abomination.

passages.g: Anyone who leads good people into wrongdoing falls into the trap they set, while the upright keep what is genuinely good.
passages.text: Whoso causeth the righteous to go astray in an evil way, he shall fall himself into his own pit: but the upright shall have good things in possession.

passages.g: Wealth convinces people they are wise, but a discerning person without money sees through them. When decent people hold power a society flourishes openly, and when corrupt people hold power people go into hiding.
passages.text: The rich man is wise in his own conceit; but the poor that hath understanding searcheth him out. When righteous men do rejoice, there is great glory: but when the wicked rise, a man is hidden.

passages.g: Concealing your wrongdoing keeps you stuck, while admitting it and abandoning it makes you forgivable. Staying alert to your own capacity for error protects you, and hardening yourself against correction destroys you.
passages.text: He that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy. Happy is the man that feareth alway: but he that hardeneth his heart shall fall into mischief.

passages.g: A corrupt ruler over powerless people is simply a predator. Leaders without understanding become oppressors, while those who refuse to be bought last.
passages.text: As a roaring lion, and a ranging bear; so is a wicked ruler over the poor people. The prince that wanteth understanding is also a great oppressor: but he that hateth covetousness shall prolong his days.

passages.g: Someone guilty of killing will be a fugitive for life. Integrity carries a person through, while crookedness brings sudden collapse.
passages.text: A man that doeth violence to the blood of any person shall flee to the pit; let no man stay him. Whoso walketh uprightly shall be saved: but he that is perverse in his ways shall fall at once.

passages.g: Working steadily at your own real work produces enough to live on, while chasing empty pursuits produces nothing but poverty.
passages.text: He that tilleth his land shall have plenty of bread: but he that followeth after vain persons shall have poverty enough.

passages.g: Reliability accumulates real advantages, while rushing to get rich requires cutting corners. Showing favoritism corrupts judgment, since a person who does it will betray fairness for almost nothing.
passages.text: A faithful man shall abound with blessings: but he that maketh haste to be rich shall not be innocent. To have respect of persons is not good: for for a piece of bread that man will transgress.

passages.g: The urgency to get rich blinds a person to the ruin they are walking toward. Honest correction eventually earns more gratitude than flattery does.
passages.text: He that hasteth to be rich hath an evil eye, and considereth not that poverty shall come upon him. He that rebuketh a man afterwards shall find more favour than he that flattereth with the tongue.

passages.g: Someone who takes from their own parents and tells themselves it is not really wrong is no different from an outright vandal.
passages.text: Whoso robbeth his father or his mother, and saith, It is no transgression; the same is the companion of a destroyer.

passages.g: Arrogance generates conflict, while trusting something beyond yourself brings abundance. Relying entirely on your own judgment is foolish, and living wisely is what saves you.
passages.text: He that is of a proud heart stirreth up strife: but he that putteth his trust in the LORD shall be made fat. He that trusteth in his own heart is a fool: but whoso walketh wisely, he shall be delivered.

passages.g: Giving to those in need does not impoverish you, while deliberately looking away earns you widespread resentment. People go into hiding when the corrupt gain power and flourish again when they fall.
passages.text: He that giveth unto the poor shall not lack: but he that hideth his eyes shall have many a curse. When the wicked rise, men hide themselves: but when they perish, the righteous increase.

passages.g: Someone corrected again and again who refuses to change will break suddenly and beyond repair. People thrive under good leadership and suffer under bad.
passages.text: He, that being often reproved hardeneth his neck, shall suddenly be destroyed, and that without remedy. When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice: but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.

passages.g: Loving wisdom brings your family joy, while dissipation drains your resources. A ruler builds a country through fair judgment and destroys it by taking bribes.
passages.text: Whoso loveth wisdom rejoiceth his father: but he that keepeth company with harlots spendeth his substance. The king by judgment establisheth the land: but he that receiveth gifts overthroweth it.

passages.g: Flattering someone sets a trap for them. Wrongdoing ensnares the person who commits it, while those who live honestly are free to enjoy their lives.
passages.text: A man that flattereth his neighbour spreadeth a net for his feet. In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare: but the righteous doth sing and rejoice.

passages.g: Decent people care whether the powerless get justice, while the corrupt make sure not to find out. Contemptuous people inflame a community, and wise people defuse its anger.
passages.text: The righteous considereth the cause of the poor: but the wicked regardeth not to know it. Scornful men bring a city into a snare: but wise men turn away wrath.

passages.g: Arguing with a fool never reaches resolution, whether he responds with rage or with laughter. Violent people hate those who live honestly, while the just protect them.
passages.text: If a wise man contendeth with a foolish man, whether he rage or laugh, there is no rest. The bloodthirsty hate the upright: but the just seek his soul.

passages.g: A fool says everything that comes into his head, while a wise person holds it back until the right moment. A leader who listens to lies ends up surrounded by liars. The poor and the deceitful share the same nature and the same origin.
passages.text: A fool uttereth all his mind: but a wise man keepeth it in till afterwards. If a ruler hearken to lies, all his servants are wicked. The poor and the deceitful man meet together: the LORD lighteneth both their eyes.

passages.g: A ruler who deals fairly with the powerless secures their own position. Discipline and correction produce wisdom, while a child left entirely to themselves brings the family shame.
passages.text: The king that faithfully judgeth the poor, his throne shall be established for ever. The rod and reproof give wisdom: but a child left to himself bringeth his mother to shame.

passages.g: The more wrongdoers there are the more wrongdoing spreads, but it does not last. Correcting your child is what eventually gives you peace.
passages.text: When the wicked are multiplied, transgression increaseth: but the righteous shall see their fall. Correct thy son, and he shall give thee rest; yea, he shall give delight unto thy soul.

passages.g: Without a guiding vision people come apart, while those who live by a shared standard do well. Some people will not be changed by words alone, even when they understand perfectly.
passages.text: Where there is no vision, the people perish: but he that keepeth the law, happy is he. A servant will not be corrected by words: for though he understand he will not answer.

passages.g: A person who speaks without thinking is a worse prospect than an outright fool. Indulging a subordinate from the beginning ends with them claiming they are your equal.
passages.text: Seest thou a man that is hasty in his words? there is more hope of a fool than of him. He that delicately bringeth up his servant from a child shall have him become his son at the length.

passages.g: Anger breeds conflict and multiplies the harm a person does. Pride brings a person down, and humility is what earns them real standing.
passages.text: An angry man stirreth up strife, and a furious man aboundeth in transgression. A man’s pride shall bring him low: but honour shall uphold the humble in spirit.

passages.g: Going in with a thief destroys you, because you hear the truth and stay silent about it. Fear of what people think is a trap, while trusting something larger keeps you safe.
passages.text: Whoso is partner with a thief hateth his own soul: he heareth cursing, and bewrayeth it not. The fear of man bringeth a snare: but whoso putteth his trust in the LORD shall be safe.

passages.g: Many people court those in power, but what a person actually deserves is determined elsewhere. The just and the unjust each find the other repellent.
passages.text: Many seek the ruler’s favour; but every man’s judgment cometh from the LORD. An unjust man is an abomination to the just: and he that is upright in the way is abomination to the wicked.

passages.g: A person can begin by admitting they understand less than anyone else.
passages.text: The words of Agur the son of Jakeh, even the prophecy: the man spake unto Ithiel, even unto Ithiel and Ucal, Surely I am more brutish than any man, and have not the understanding of a man.

passages.g: Admitting you have neither learned wisdom nor grasped the sacred is itself a starting point.
passages.text: I neither learned wisdom, nor have the knowledge of the holy.

passages.g: No human being has done or could do what made the world, and whatever did is beyond human naming or comprehension.
passages.text: Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended? who hath gathered the wind in his fists? who hath bound the waters in a garment? who hath established all the ends of the earth? what is his name, and what is his son’s name, if thou canst tell?

passages.g: What is genuinely true needs no additions, and anyone who adds to it makes themselves a liar.
passages.text: Every word of God is pure: he is a shield unto them that put their trust in him. Add thou not unto his words, lest he reprove thee, and thou be found a liar.

passages.g: The two things worth asking for are freedom from falsehood and enough to live on, neither destitution nor excess.
passages.text: Two things have I required of thee; deny me them not before I die: Remove far from me vanity and lies: give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with food convenient for me:

passages.g: Too much makes people forget what sustains them, and too little drives them to steal. Do not accuse a subordinate to their superior, because the accusation will land on you.
passages.text: Lest I be full, and deny thee, and say, Who is the LORD? or lest I be poor, and steal, and take the name of my God in vain. Accuse not a servant unto his master, lest he curse thee, and thou be found guilty.

passages.g: Some people despise the parents who raised them, and some consider themselves pure while remaining filthy.
passages.text: There is a generation that curseth their father, and doth not bless their mother. There is a generation that are pure in their own eyes, and yet is not washed from their filthiness.

passages.g: Some people are consumed by their own arrogance.
passages.text: There is a generation, O how lofty are their eyes! and their eyelids are lifted up.

passages.g: Some people live by devouring the poor and the defenseless.
passages.text: There is a generation, whose teeth are as swords, and their jaw teeth as knives, to devour the poor from off the earth, and the needy from among men.

passages.g: Greed only ever asks for more. Some appetites can never be filled.
passages.text: The horseleach hath two daughters, crying, Give, give. There are three things that are never satisfied, yea, four things say not, It is enough:

passages.g: Death, barrenness, parched ground, and fire share the same nature: they consume without ever reaching enough.
passages.text: The grave; and the barren womb; the earth that is not filled with water; and the fire that saith not, It is enough.

passages.g: Contempt for the parents who raised you ends badly for the one who holds it.
passages.text: The eye that mocketh at his father, and despiseth to obey his mother, the ravens of the valley shall pick it out, and the young eagles shall eat it.

passages.g: Some things remain genuinely beyond understanding, however closely you look at them.
passages.text: There be three things which are too wonderful for me, yea, four which I know not:

passages.g: The most remarkable movements leave no trace of how they happened, and attraction between people is one of them.
passages.text: The way of an eagle in the air; the way of a serpent upon a rock; the way of a ship in the midst of the sea; and the way of a man with a maid.

passages.g: People can do real harm and sincerely believe they have done nothing wrong. Some situations a society simply cannot absorb.
passages.text: Such is the way of an adulterous woman; she eateth, and wipeth her mouth, and saith, I have done no wickedness. For three things the earth is disquieted, and for four which it cannot bear:

passages.g: People raised suddenly above what they can handle become intolerable, whether through power, plenty, marriage, or inheritance.
passages.text: For a servant when he reigneth; and a fool when he is filled with meat; For an odious woman when she is married; and an handmaid that is heir to her mistress.

passages.g: Wisdom does not require strength; small creatures show it by preparing in advance for what is coming.
passages.text: There be four things which are little upon the earth, but they are exceeding wise: The ants are a people not strong, yet they prepare their meat in the summer;

passages.g: Weakness is compensated by shelter, by coordinated action without a leader, and by persistence that reaches the highest places.
passages.text: The conies are but a feeble folk, yet make they their houses in the rocks; The locusts have no king, yet go they forth all of them by bands; The spider taketh hold with her hands, and is in kings’ palaces.

passages.g: Some things carry themselves with a bearing that does not flinch or give way.
passages.text: There be three things which go well, yea, four are comely in going: A lion which is strongest among beasts, and turneth not away for any; A greyhound; an he goat also; and a king, against whom there is no rising up.

passages.g: If you have acted arrogantly or entertained malicious thoughts, stop talking and stay silent.
passages.text: If thou hast done foolishly in lifting up thyself, or if thou hast thought evil, lay thine hand upon thy mouth.

passages.g: Pressure applied to anything produces a predictable result, and stirring up anger reliably produces conflict.
passages.text: Surely the churning of milk bringeth forth butter, and the wringing of the nose bringeth forth blood: so the forcing of wrath bringeth forth strife.

passages.g: Do not spend your vitality on sexual pursuit or adopt the habits that ruin people in power.
passages.text: The words of king Lemuel, the prophecy that his mother taught him. What, my son? and what, the son of my womb? and what, the son of my vows? Give not thy strength unto women, nor thy ways to that which destroyeth kings.

passages.g: Rulers should not drink, because intoxication makes them forget the law and deny justice to the vulnerable.
passages.text: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine; nor for princes strong drink: Lest they drink, and forget the law, and pervert the judgment of any of the afflicted.

passages.g: Alcohol is better suited to those in genuine misery, for whom it offers temporary relief from suffering.
passages.text: Give strong drink unto him that is ready to perish, and wine unto those that be of heavy hearts. Let him drink, and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.

passages.g: Speak up on behalf of those who cannot defend themselves, and judge fairly for the poor and needy.
passages.text: Open thy mouth for the dumb in the cause of all such as are appointed to destruction. Open thy mouth, judge righteously, and plead the cause of the poor and needy.

passages.g: A person of real character is rare and worth more than wealth, and earns their partner's complete trust by consistently acting for their good.
passages.text: Who can find a virtuous woman? for her price is far above rubies. The heart of her husband doth safely trust in her, so that he shall have no need of spoil. She will do him good and not evil all the days of her life.

passages.g: Excellence shows in working willingly and in going far afield to provide for one's household.
passages.text: She seeketh wool, and flax, and worketh willingly with her hands. She is like the merchants’ ships; she bringeth her food from afar.

passages.g: Excellence shows in rising early to provide for others, and in shrewdly investing one's earnings in productive assets.
passages.text: She riseth also while it is yet night, and giveth meat to her household, and a portion to her maidens. She considereth a field, and buyeth it: with the fruit of her hands she planteth a vineyard.

passages.g: Excellence shows in physical strength, in confidence that one's work is good, and in persistent labor late into the night.
passages.text: She girdeth her loins with strength, and strengtheneth her arms. She perceiveth that her merchandise is good: her candle goeth not out by night. She layeth her hands to the spindle, and her hands hold the distaff.

passages.g: Excellence shows in generosity to the poor, and in preparing so thoroughly for hardship that one has no fear of it.
passages.text: She stretcheth out her hand to the poor; yea, she reacheth forth her hands to the needy. She is not afraid of the snow for her household: for all her household are clothed with scarlet.

passages.g: Excellence produces both material comfort at home and standing for one's family in public life.
passages.text: She maketh herself coverings of tapestry; her clothing is silk and purple. Her husband is known in the gates, when he sitteth among the elders of the land.

passages.g: Excellence shows in producing goods worth selling, and gives a person strength, dignity, and confidence about the future.
passages.text: She maketh fine linen, and selleth it; and delivereth girdles unto the merchant. Strength and honour are her clothing; and she shall rejoice in time to come.

passages.g: Excellence shows in speaking wisely and kindly, in managing one's household attentively, and in refusing idleness.
passages.text: She openeth her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue is the law of kindness. She looketh well to the ways of her household, and eateth not the bread of idleness.

passages.g: A life of real character earns the praise of those closest to you, who recognize it as surpassing all others.
passages.text: Her children arise up, and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praiseth her. Many daughters have done virtuously, but thou excellest them all.

passages.g: Charm and beauty are unreliable measures of worth; reverence and one's actual accomplishments are what deserve recognition.
passages.text: Favour is deceitful, and beauty is vain: but a woman that feareth the LORD, she shall be praised. Give her of the fruit of her hands; and let her own works praise her in the gates.

passages.g: Everything is fleeting and insubstantial, and human labor yields no lasting gain, since generations pass away while the world endures unchanged.
passages.text: The words of the Preacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem. Vanity of vanities, saith the Preacher, vanity of vanities; all is vanity. What profit hath a man of all his labour which he taketh under the sun? One generation passeth away, and another generation cometh: but the earth abideth for ever.

passages.g: Nature moves in endless cycles, returning always to where it began.
passages.text: The sun also ariseth, and the sun goeth down, and hasteth to his place where he arose. The wind goeth toward the south, and turneth about unto the north; it whirleth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits.

passages.g: The world's processes run endlessly without ever reaching completion, and human desire is likewise never satisfied by what it takes in.
passages.text: All the rivers run into the sea; yet the sea is not full; unto the place from whence the rivers come, thither they return again. All things are full of labour; man cannot utter it: the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.

passages.g: Nothing is genuinely new; whatever appears novel has already occurred before.
passages.text: The thing that hath been, it is that which shall be; and that which is done is that which shall be done: and there is no new thing under the sun. Is there any thing whereof it may be said, See, this is new? it hath been already of old time, which was before us.

passages.g: The past is forgotten, and the present will be forgotten too by those who come after.
passages.text: There is no remembrance of former things; neither shall there be any remembrance of things that are to come with those that shall come after. I the Preacher was king over Israel in Jerusalem.

passages.g: Dedicating oneself to understanding everything that happens is a wearying task, and the conclusion is that it all amounts to nothing.
passages.text: And I gave my heart to seek and search out by wisdom concerning all things that are done under heaven: this sore travail hath God given to the sons of man to be exercised therewith. I have seen all the works that are done under the sun; and, behold, all is vanity and vexation of spirit.

passages.g: Some things cannot be fixed or accounted for, and even the greatest accumulation of wisdom and experience does not change this.
passages.text: That which is crooked cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbered. I communed with mine own heart, saying, Lo, I am come to great estate, and have gotten more wisdom than all they that have been before me in Jerusalem: yea, my heart had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.

passages.g: Pursuing knowledge of wisdom and folly alike proves futile, because greater understanding brings greater grief.
passages.text: And I gave my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly: I perceived that this also is vexation of spirit. For in much wisdom is much grief: and he that increaseth knowledge increaseth sorrow.

passages.g: Testing life through pleasure reveals it to be empty, and laughter and amusement accomplish nothing.
passages.text: I said in mine heart, Go to now, I will prove thee with mirth, therefore enjoy pleasure: and, behold, this also is vanity. I said of laughter, It is mad: and of mirth, What doeth it?

passages.g: Seeking what is genuinely good for people, one may try indulgence and folly deliberately, and undertake vast building projects.
passages.text: I sought in mine heart to give myself unto wine, yet acquainting mine heart with wisdom; and to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was that good for the sons of men, which they should do under the heaven all the days of their life. I made me great works; I builded me houses; I planted me vineyards:

passages.g: One may acquire gardens, estates, servants, and herds beyond anyone before.
passages.text: I made me gardens and orchards, and I planted trees in them of all kind of fruits: I made me pools of water, to water therewith the wood that bringeth forth trees: I got me servants and maidens, and had servants born in my house; also I had great possessions of great and small cattle above all that were in Jerusalem before me:

passages.g: One may amass wealth, entertainment, and every human delight, becoming greater than all predecessors while retaining full clarity of mind.
passages.text: I gathered me also silver and gold, and the peculiar treasure of kings and of the provinces: I gat me men singers and women singers, and the delights of the sons of men, as musical instruments, and that of all sorts. So I was great, and increased more than all that were before me in Jerusalem: also my wisdom remained with me.

passages.g: Withholding no pleasure from oneself and taking joy in one's work is the only reward one actually gets from that work.
passages.text: And whatsoever mine eyes desired I kept not from them, I withheld not my heart from any joy; for my heart rejoiced in all my labour: and this was my portion of all my labour.

passages.g: Surveying everything one has built and achieved, it proves to be empty and to yield no lasting gain.
passages.text: Then I looked on all the works that my hands had wrought, and on the labour that I had laboured to do: and, behold, all was vanity and vexation of spirit, and there was no profit under the sun. And I turned myself to behold wisdom, and madness, and folly: for what can the man do that cometh after the king? even that which hath been already done.

passages.g: Wisdom is genuinely better than folly, as light is better than darkness, yet the same fate overtakes the wise and the foolish alike.
passages.text: Then I saw that wisdom excelleth folly, as far as light excelleth darkness. The wise man’s eyes are in his head; but the fool walketh in darkness: and I myself perceived also that one event happeneth to them all.

passages.g: If the wise and the foolish share the same end, wisdom's advantage is hollow, since both are equally forgotten and both die.
passages.text: Then said I in my heart, As it happeneth to the fool, so it happeneth even to me; and why was I then more wise? Then I said in my heart, that this also is vanity. For there is no remembrance of the wise more than of the fool for ever; seeing that which now is in the days to come shall all be forgotten. And how dieth the wise man? as the fool.

passages.g: Life and labor become hateful when one realizes the work will pass to someone else after death.
passages.text: Therefore I hated life; because the work that is wrought under the sun is grievous unto me: for all is vanity and vexation of spirit. Yea, I hated all my labour which I had taken under the sun: because I should leave it unto the man that shall be after me.

passages.g: Everything you build passes to a successor whose wisdom or folly you cannot know, which is cause for despair about all effort.
passages.text: And who knoweth whether he shall be a wise man or a fool? yet shall he have rule over all my labour wherein I have laboured, and wherein I have shewed myself wise under the sun. This is also vanity. Therefore I went about to cause my heart to despair of all the labour which I took under the sun.

passages.g: That a person's skilled and just labor is inherited by someone who did none of it is a great injustice, leaving the worker with nothing to show.
passages.text: For there is a man whose labour is in wisdom, and in knowledge, and in equity; yet to a man that hath not laboured therein shall he leave it for his portion. This also is vanity and a great evil. For what hath man of all his labour, and of the vexation of his heart, wherein he hath laboured under the sun?

passages.g: A life of anxious toil brings sorrow and sleeplessness; the best available course is to eat, drink, and find enjoyment in one's work, which is itself a gift.
passages.text: For all his days are sorrows, and his travail grief; yea, his heart taketh not rest in the night. This is also vanity. There is nothing better for a man, than that he should eat and drink, and that he should make his soul enjoy good in his labour. This also I saw, that it was from the hand of God.

passages.g: Wisdom, knowledge, and joy are given to some while others merely accumulate for someone else's benefit, and this too is empty.
passages.text: For who can eat, or who else can hasten hereunto, more than I? For God giveth to a man that is good in his sight wisdom, and knowledge, and joy: but to the sinner he giveth travail, to gather and to heap up, that he may give to him that is good before God. This also is vanity and vexation of spirit.

passages.g: Everything has its appropriate moment: birth and death, planting and uprooting, killing and healing, destroying and building, weeping and laughing, mourning and dancing.
passages.text: To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven: A time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up that which is planted; A time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up; A time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;

passages.g: Every action has its opposite and its proper season: scattering and gathering, embracing and abstaining, keeping and discarding, silence and speech, love and hate, war and peace.
passages.text: A time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing; A time to get, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away; A time to rend, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak; A time to love, and a time to hate; a time of war, and a time of peace.

passages.g: Labor yields no real profit; everything is beautiful in its own time, and humans sense eternity yet cannot grasp the whole design of existence.
passages.text: What profit hath he that worketh in that wherein he laboureth? I have seen the travail, which God hath given to the sons of men to be exercised in it. He hath made every thing beautiful in his time: also he hath set the world in their heart, so that no man can find out the work that God maketh from the beginning to the end.

passages.g: The best a person can do is to enjoy life and do good, and to eat, drink, and take pleasure in one's work as a gift.
passages.text: I know that there is no good in them, but for a man to rejoice, and to do good in his life. And also that every man should eat and drink, and enjoy the good of all his labour, it is the gift of God.

passages.g: What is divinely established is permanent and cannot be added to or subtracted from, and the past recurs perpetually.
passages.text: I know that, whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever: nothing can be put to it, nor any thing taken from it: and God doeth it, that men should fear before him. That which hath been is now; and that which is to be hath already been; and God requireth that which is past.

passages.g: Even courts and holy places are corrupted by wickedness, yet there will come a reckoning for every action in its time.
passages.text: And moreover I saw under the sun the place of judgment, that wickedness was there; and the place of righteousness, that iniquity was there. I said in mine heart, God shall judge the righteous and the wicked: for there is a time there for every purpose and for every work.

passages.g: Human beings are tested so that they may recognize they are animals.
passages.text: I said in mine heart concerning the estate of the sons of men, that God might manifest them, and that they might see that they themselves are beasts.

passages.g: Humans and animals share the same fate and the same breath, so humans have no real superiority; all come from dust and return to it.
passages.text: For that which befalleth the sons of men befalleth beasts; even one thing befalleth them: as the one dieth, so dieth the other; yea, they have all one breath; so that a man hath no preeminence above a beast: for all is vanity. All go unto one place; all are of the dust, and all turn to dust again.

passages.g: No one knows what becomes of anyone after death, so the best course is to find satisfaction in one's own work, which is all one truly has.
passages.text: Who knoweth the spirit of man that goeth upward, and the spirit of the beast that goeth downward to the earth? Wherefore I perceive that there is nothing better, than that a man should rejoice in his own works; for that is his portion: for who shall bring him to see what shall be after him?

passages.g: The oppressed suffer without comfort while power lies with their oppressors, which makes the dead seem better off than the living.
passages.text: So I returned, and considered all the oppressions that are done under the sun: and behold the tears of such as were oppressed, and they had no comforter; and on the side of their oppressors there was power; but they had no comforter. Wherefore I praised the dead which are already dead more than the living which are yet alive.

passages.g: Best of all is never to have existed and witnessed such evil; meanwhile skilled work merely provokes envy, and the idle destroy themselves.
passages.text: Yea, better is he than both they, which hath not yet been, who hath not seen the evil work that is done under the sun. Again, I considered all travail, and every right work, that for this a man is envied of his neighbour. This is also vanity and vexation of spirit. The fool foldeth his hands together, and eateth his own flesh.

passages.g: A little held in peace is better than much gained through exhausting struggle.
passages.text: Better is an handful with quietness, than both the hands full with travail and vexation of spirit. Then I returned, and I saw vanity under the sun.

passages.g: Working endlessly to accumulate wealth with no one to inherit it, never asking who it is for, is a pointless and painful way to live.
passages.text: There is one alone, and there is not a second; yea, he hath neither child nor brother: yet is there no end of all his labour; neither is his eye satisfied with riches; neither saith he, For whom do I labour, and bereave my soul of good? This is also vanity, yea, it is a sore travail.

passages.g: Two people are better than one because they help each other when one falls and give each other warmth and support.
passages.text: Two are better than one; because they have a good reward for their labour. For if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow: but woe to him that is alone when he falleth; for he hath not another to help him up. Again, if two lie together, then they have heat: but how can one be warm alone?

passages.g: Multiple people together resist attack far better than one alone, and a poor young person who listens to counsel is better than an old ruler who will not.
passages.text: And if one prevail against him, two shall withstand him; and a threefold cord is not quickly broken. Better is a poor and a wise child than an old and foolish king, who will no more be admonished. For out of prison he cometh to reign; whereas also he that is born in his kingdom becometh poor.

passages.g: Popular favor does not last; even a successor whom crowds follow will not be celebrated by later generations.
passages.text: I considered all the living which walk under the sun, with the second child that shall stand up in his stead. There is no end of all the people, even of all that have been before them: they also that come after shall not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and vexation of spirit.

passages.g: Approach the sacred carefully: listen rather than perform empty ritual, and speak little rather than blurting out words before what is far greater than you.
passages.text: Keep thy foot when thou goest to the house of God, and be more ready to hear, than to give the sacrifice of fools: for they consider not that they do evil. Be not rash with thy mouth, and let not thine heart be hasty to utter any thing before God: for God is in heaven, and thou upon earth: therefore let thy words be few.

passages.g: Too many words mark a fool, and if you make a promise you should keep it promptly — better not to promise at all than to promise and fail to deliver.
passages.text: For a dream cometh through the multitude of business; and a fool’s voice is known by multitude of words. When thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it; for he hath no pleasure in fools: pay that which thou hast vowed. Better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay.

passages.g: Do not let careless speech lead you into wrongdoing and then excuse it as a mistake; amid many empty words and fantasies, hold to reverence.
passages.text: Suffer not thy mouth to cause thy flesh to sin; neither say thou before the angel, that it was an error: wherefore should God be angry at thy voice, and destroy the work of thine hands? For in the multitude of dreams and many words there are also divers vanities: but fear thou God.

passages.g: Do not be shocked to see the poor oppressed and justice perverted, because officials protect one another up the hierarchy, yet everyone ultimately depends on the land.
passages.text: If thou seest the oppression of the poor, and violent perverting of judgment and justice in a province, marvel not at the matter: for he that is higher than the highest regardeth; and there be higher than they. Moreover the profit of the earth is for all: the king himself is served by the field.

passages.g: Whoever loves money is never satisfied by having more of it, and as wealth grows so do the people consuming it, leaving the owner only the sight of it.
passages.text: He that loveth silver shall not be satisfied with silver; nor he that loveth abundance with increase: this is also vanity. When goods increase, they are increased that eat them: and what good is there to the owners thereof, saving the beholding of them with their eyes?

passages.g: A laborer sleeps well regardless of how much he eats, while the rich man's abundance keeps him awake; hoarded wealth harms its owner and can be lost entirely, leaving nothing for his heirs.
passages.text: The sleep of a labouring man is sweet, whether he eat little or much: but the abundance of the rich will not suffer him to sleep. There is a sore evil which I have seen under the sun, namely, riches kept for the owners thereof to their hurt. But those riches perish by evil travail: and he begetteth a son, and there is nothing in his hand.

passages.g: We leave the world as naked as we entered it, carrying none of what we earned, so laboring only to accumulate is laboring for nothing.
passages.text: As he came forth of his mother’s womb, naked shall he return to go as he came, and shall take nothing of his labour, which he may carry away in his hand. And this also is a sore evil, that in all points as he came, so shall he go: and what profit hath he that hath laboured for the wind?

passages.g: A life consumed by bitterness wastes itself; the good available to a person is to eat, drink, and take pleasure in the fruits of their own work during their brief life.
passages.text: All his days also he eateth in darkness, and he hath much sorrow and wrath with his sickness. Behold that which I have seen: it is good and comely for one to eat and to drink, and to enjoy the good of all his labour that he taketh under the sun all the days of his life, which God giveth him: for it is his portion.

passages.g: Being able to actually enjoy what one has earned is itself a piece of good fortune, and a person absorbed in that enjoyment scarcely notices life passing.
passages.text: Every man also to whom God hath given riches and wealth, and hath given him power to eat thereof, and to take his portion, and to rejoice in his labour; this is the gift of God. For he shall not much remember the days of his life; because God answereth him in the joy of his heart.

passages.g: It is a common misfortune for someone to acquire everything they wanted and yet lack the capacity to enjoy any of it, so a stranger consumes it instead.
passages.text: There is an evil which I have seen under the sun, and it is common among men: A man to whom God hath given riches, wealth, and honour, so that he wanteth nothing for his soul of all that he desireth, yet God giveth him not power to eat thereof, but a stranger eateth it: this is vanity, and it is an evil disease.

passages.g: A long life full of children and years is worth nothing if the person never finds satisfaction in it; one who was never born is better off.
passages.text: If a man beget an hundred children, and live many years, so that the days of his years be many, and his soul be not filled with good, and also that he have no burial; I say, that an untimely birth is better than he. For he cometh in with vanity, and departeth in darkness, and his name shall be covered with darkness.

passages.g: Someone who never existed has more peace than a person who lives thousands of years without ever enjoying anything, since everyone ends in the same place; all our labor feeds an appetite that is never filled.
passages.text: Moreover he hath not seen the sun, nor known any thing: this hath more rest than the other. Yea, though he live a thousand years twice told, yet hath he seen no good: do not all go to one place? All the labour of man is for his mouth, and yet the appetite is not filled.

passages.g: The wise hold no lasting advantage over the fool, and it is better to enjoy what is in front of you than to chase what you merely imagine. Nothing about human nature is new, and no one can contend against what is stronger than they are.
passages.text: For what hath the wise more than the fool? what hath the poor, that knoweth to walk before the living? Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the desire: this is also vanity and vexation of spirit. That which hath been is named already, and it is known that it is man: neither may he contend with him that is mightier than he.

passages.g: The more we accumulate, the less it helps; no one knows what is genuinely good in a life this brief, nor what will come after they are gone.
passages.text: Seeing there be many things that increase vanity, what is man the better? For who knoweth what is good for man in this life, all the days of his vain life which he spendeth as a shadow? for who can tell a man what shall be after him under the sun?

passages.g: A good reputation outlasts luxury, and grief teaches more than celebration because it forces the living to confront their own end. Sorrow can improve a person in a way amusement cannot.
passages.text: A good name is better than precious ointment; and the day of death than the day of one’s birth. It is better to go to the house of mourning, than to go to the house of feasting: for that is the end of all men; and the living will lay it to his heart. Sorrow is better than laughter: for by the sadness of the countenance the heart is made better.

passages.g: The wise are at home with mourning while fools seek constant amusement; honest criticism is worth more than flattering entertainment, which burns loud and fast and leaves nothing behind.
passages.text: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning; but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth. It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise, than for a man to hear the song of fools. For as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fool: this also is vanity.

passages.g: Oppression can drive even a sensible person to madness, and a bribe corrupts judgment. Patience is better than pride, and quick anger is the mark of a fool.
passages.text: Surely oppression maketh a wise man mad; and a gift destroyeth the heart. Better is the end of a thing than the beginning thereof: and the patient in spirit is better than the proud in spirit. Be not hasty in thy spirit to be angry: for anger resteth in the bosom of fools.

passages.g: Asking why the past was better than the present is not a wise question. Wisdom protects a person the way money does, but unlike money it also gives them life.
passages.text: Say not thou, What is the cause that the former days were better than these? for thou dost not enquire wisely concerning this. Wisdom is good with an inheritance: and by it there is profit to them that see the sun. For wisdom is a defence, and money is a defence: but the excellency of knowledge is, that wisdom giveth life to them that have it.

passages.g: No one can straighten what reality has bent; enjoy good times and reflect during hard ones, since both are set against each other so that no one can predict what comes next.
passages.text: Consider the work of God: for who can make that straight, which he hath made crooked? In the day of prosperity be joyful, but in the day of adversity consider: God also hath set the one over against the other, to the end that man should find nothing after him.

passages.g: Good people sometimes perish early while the corrupt live long; do not be excessively righteous or excessively clever, since it can ruin you.
passages.text: All things have I seen in the days of my vanity: there is a just man that perisheth in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man that prolongeth his life in his wickedness. Be not righteous over much; neither make thyself over wise: why shouldest thou destroy thyself?

passages.g: Do not be excessively wicked or foolish either, since that shortens your life; grasp both warnings and let go of neither. Wisdom defends a person more than a city's warriors do.
passages.text: Be not over much wicked, neither be thou foolish: why shouldest thou die before thy time? It is good that thou shouldest take hold of this; yea, also from this withdraw not thine hand: for he that feareth God shall come forth of them all. Wisdom strengtheneth the wise more than ten mighty men which are in the city.

passages.g: No one on earth does good without also doing wrong. Do not listen closely to everything said about you, since you know you have said the same things about others.
passages.text: For there is not a just man upon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not. Also take no heed unto all words that are spoken; lest thou hear thy servant curse thee: For oftentimes also thine own heart knoweth that thou thyself likewise hast cursed others. All this have I proved by wisdom: I said, I will be wise; but it was far from me.

passages.g: The deepest truths cannot be found out, no matter how hard a person searches for wisdom and for an understanding of folly and madness.
passages.text: That which is far off, and exceeding deep, who can find it out? I applied mine heart to know, and to search, and to seek out wisdom, and the reason of things, and to know the wickedness of folly, even of foolishness and madness:

passages.g: A seduction whose affection is a trap and whose embrace is a snare is more bitter than death, and only the disciplined escape it.
passages.text: And I find more bitter than death the woman, whose heart is snares and nets, and her hands as bands: whoso pleaseth God shall escape from her; but the sinner shall be taken by her. Behold, this have I found, saith the preacher, counting one by one, to find out the account:

passages.g: A truly upright person is almost impossible to find; human beings were made straightforward, but they have complicated themselves with endless schemes.
passages.text: Which yet my soul seeketh, but I find not: one man among a thousand have I found; but a woman among all those have I not found. Lo, this only have I found, that God hath made man upright; but they have sought out many inventions.

passages.g: Wisdom transforms a person's very bearing. Keep your commitments to those in power, and do not rush out of favor or dig in on a bad position, since they will do as they please.
passages.text: Who is as the wise man? and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing? a man’s wisdom maketh his face to shine, and the boldness of his face shall be changed. I counsel thee to keep the king’s commandment, and that in regard of the oath of God. Be not hasty to go out of his sight: stand not in an evil thing; for he doeth whatsoever pleaseth him.

passages.g: A ruler's word carries unquestionable force, and the wise sense the right time and manner for everything. Because timing decides so much and we control it so little, people suffer.
passages.text: Where the word of a king is, there is power: and who may say unto him, What doest thou?
