Whoso keepeth the commandment shall feel no evil thing: and a wise man’s heart discerneth both time and judgment. Because to every purpose there is time and judgment, therefore the misery of man is great upon him.

passages.g: No one knows the future or when it will arrive. No one controls their own breath or the day of their death; death is the one war with no discharge, and no amount of scheming buys escape from it.
passages.text: For he knoweth not that which shall be: for who can tell him when it shall be? There is no man that hath power over the spirit to retain the spirit; neither hath he power in the day of death: and there is no discharge in that war; neither shall wickedness deliver those that are given to it.

passages.g: People sometimes hold power over others only to their own harm, and the corrupt are buried with honor and quickly forgotten by the very city they wronged.
passages.text: All this have I seen, and applied my heart unto every work that is done under the sun: there is a time wherein one man ruleth over another to his own hurt. And so I saw the wicked buried, who had come and gone from the place of the holy, and they were forgotten in the city where they had so done: this is also vanity.

passages.g: Because punishment for wrongdoing is delayed, people grow bolder in doing wrong; even so, in the long run it goes well for those who live with reverence.
passages.text: Because sentence against an evil work is not executed speedily, therefore the heart of the sons of men is fully set in them to do evil. Though a sinner do evil an hundred times, and his days be prolonged, yet surely I know that it shall be well with them that fear God, which fear before him:

passages.g: It will not finally go well for the person who lives without reverence, and their days pass like a shadow.
passages.text: But it shall not be well with the wicked, neither shall he prolong his days, which are as a shadow; because he feareth not before God.

passages.g: Good people often meet what the wicked deserve, and the wicked meet what the good deserve, and this is absurd.
passages.text: There is a vanity which is done upon the earth; that there be just men, unto whom it happeneth according to the work of the wicked; again, there be wicked men, to whom it happeneth according to the work of the righteous: I said that this also is vanity.

passages.g: Since nothing better is on offer, the sensible response is to eat, drink, and be glad, because that is what stays with a person through the work of their life.
passages.text: Then I commended mirth, because a man hath no better thing under the sun, than to eat, and to drink, and to be merry: for that shall abide with him of his labour the days of his life, which God giveth him under the sun.

passages.g: Trying to grasp everything that happens on earth costs a person their sleep and yields nothing.
passages.text: When I applied mine heart to know wisdom, and to see the business that is done upon the earth: (for also there is that neither day nor night seeth sleep with his eyes:)

passages.g: No one, however hard they work or however wise they are, can comprehend the whole of what happens in the world.
passages.text: Then I beheld all the work of God, that a man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun: because though a man labour to seek it out, yet he shall not find it; yea farther; though a wise man think to know it, yet shall he not be able to find it.

passages.g: The fates of the good and the wise are out of their own hands, and no one can tell from what befalls them whether they are loved or hated.
passages.text: For all this I considered in my heart even to declare all this, that the righteous, and the wise, and their works, are in the hand of God: no man knoweth either love or hatred by all that is before them.

passages.g: The same fate arrives for everyone — the decent and the corrupt, the devout and the indifferent alike.
passages.text: All things come alike to all: there is one event to the righteous, and to the wicked; to the good and to the clean, and to the unclean; to him that sacrificeth, and to him that sacrificeth not: as is the good, so is the sinner; and he that sweareth, as he that feareth an oath.

passages.g: That one fate comes to everyone is the great wrong, and it fills people with madness while they live. Still, being alive carries hope, and the lowliest living creature is better off than the greatest dead one.
passages.text: This is an evil among all things that are done under the sun, that there is one event unto all: yea, also the heart of the sons of men is full of evil, and madness is in their heart while they live, and after that they go to the dead. For to him that is joined to all the living there is hope: for a living dog is better than a dead lion.

passages.g: The living at least know they will die; the dead know nothing, are forgotten, and their loves, hatreds, and envies have vanished from the world entirely.
passages.text: For the living know that they shall die: but the dead know not any thing, neither have they any more a reward; for the memory of them is forgotten. Also their love, and their hatred, and their envy, is now perished; neither have they any more a portion for ever in any thing that is done under the sun.

passages.g: Eat your food gladly, drink with a light heart, and take pleasure in small comforts, for this way of living is accepted.
passages.text: Go thy way, eat thy bread with joy, and drink thy wine with a merry heart; for God now accepteth thy works. Let thy garments be always white; and let thy head lack no ointment.

passages.g: Live joyfully with the person you love through your brief and fleeting days, for that companionship is the share allotted to you in return for all your work.
passages.text: Live joyfully with the wife whom thou lovest all the days of the life of thy vanity, which he hath given thee under the sun, all the days of thy vanity: for that is thy portion in this life, and in thy labour which thou takest under the sun.

passages.g: Do whatever is in front of you with your full strength, because after death there is no work, no planning, no knowledge, and no wisdom.
passages.text: Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might; for there is no work, nor device, nor knowledge, nor wisdom, in the grave, whither thou goest.

passages.g: The fastest do not win the race, the strongest do not win the battle, the wise do not eat best, and the skilled are not favored; timing and chance decide it all.
passages.text: I returned, and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, neither yet bread to the wise, nor yet riches to men of understanding, nor yet favour to men of skill; but time and chance happeneth to them all.

passages.g: People do not know when their moment will come, and disaster catches them suddenly, the way a net catches fish or a snare catches birds.
passages.text: For man also knoweth not his time: as the fishes that are taken in an evil net, and as the birds that are caught in the snare; so are the sons of men snared in an evil time, when it falleth suddenly upon them. This wisdom have I seen also under the sun, and it seemed great unto me:

passages.g: A poor but wise man once saved a besieged city by his intelligence, and afterward no one remembered him.
passages.text: There was a little city, and few men within it; and there came a great king against it, and besieged it, and built great bulwarks against it: Now there was found in it a poor wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city; yet no man remembered that same poor man.

passages.g: Wisdom is stronger than force or weapons, yet the wisdom of a poor person is dismissed and goes unheard, while quiet words of the wise carry further than a ruler shouting among fools. One wrongdoer can destroy a great deal of good.
passages.text: Then said I, Wisdom is better than strength: nevertheless the poor man’s wisdom is despised, and his words are not heard. The words of wise men are heard in quiet more than the cry of him that ruleth among fools. Wisdom is better than weapons of war: but one sinner destroyeth much good.

passages.g: A little foolishness spoils an otherwise honored reputation, the way a few dead flies ruin perfume; a fool announces his foolishness with every step he takes.
passages.text: Dead flies cause the ointment of the apothecary to send forth a stinking savour: so doth a little folly him that is in reputation for wisdom and honour. A wise man’s heart is at his right hand; but a fool’s heart at his left. Yea also, when he that is a fool walketh by the way, his wisdom faileth him, and he saith to every one that he is a fool.

passages.g: When someone in power turns against you, stay composed rather than react, since composure defuses even serious offense. It is a visible wrong when fools are elevated and capable people are placed beneath them.
passages.text: If the spirit of the ruler rise up against thee, leave not thy place; for yielding pacifieth great offences. There is an evil which I have seen under the sun, as an error which proceedeth from the ruler: Folly is set in great dignity, and the rich sit in low place. I have seen servants upon horses, and princes walking as servants upon the earth.

passages.g: Every action carries its own risk back to the one who takes it, and working with a dull tool costs more force. Foresight is what makes effort effective.
passages.text: He that diggeth a pit shall fall into it; and whoso breaketh an hedge, a serpent shall bite him. Whoso removeth stones shall be hurt therewith; and he that cleaveth wood shall be endangered thereby. If the iron be blunt, and he do not whet the edge, then must he put to more strength: but wisdom is profitable to direct.

passages.g: A snake bites before it can be charmed, and the compulsive talker is no better. A wise person's words earn goodwill, while a fool's words devour him, beginning in nonsense and ending in destructive madness.
passages.text: Surely the serpent will bite without enchantment; and a babbler is no better. The words of a wise man’s mouth are gracious; but the lips of a fool will swallow up himself. The beginning of the words of his mouth is foolishness: and the end of his talk is mischievous madness.

passages.g: A fool talks endlessly, though no one can know what comes next. Foolish labor exhausts the fool because he does not know the simplest way to get where he is going.
passages.text: A fool also is full of words: a man cannot tell what shall be; and what shall be after him, who can tell him? The labour of the foolish wearieth every one of them, because he knoweth not how to go to the city. Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child, and thy princes eat in the morning!

passages.g: A society suffers under immature, self-indulgent leaders and thrives under disciplined ones who take what they need for strength rather than pleasure. Neglect quietly collapses whatever it touches, and while feasting brings laughter, money is what actually gets things done.
passages.text: Blessed art thou, O land, when thy king is the son of nobles, and thy princes eat in due season, for strength, and not for drunkenness! By much slothfulness the building decayeth; and through idleness of the hands the house droppeth through. A feast is made for laughter, and wine maketh merry: but money answereth all things.

passages.g: Do not speak against the powerful, even privately or in your own thoughts, because word travels in ways you cannot trace.
passages.text: Curse not the king, no not in thy thought; and curse not the rich in thy bedchamber: for a bird of the air shall carry the voice, and that which hath wings shall tell the matter.

passages.g: Give away what you have without expecting immediate return, and spread it widely, because you cannot know what disaster is coming.
passages.text: Cast thy bread upon the waters: for thou shalt find it after many days. Give a portion to seven, and also to eight; for thou knowest not what evil shall be upon the earth.

passages.g: Nature takes its own course regardless of us, and the person who waits for perfect conditions never plants and never harvests.
passages.text: If the clouds be full of rain, they empty themselves upon the earth: and if the tree fall toward the south, or toward the north, in the place where the tree falleth, there it shall be. He that observeth the wind shall not sow; and he that regardeth the clouds shall not reap.

passages.g: Since you cannot explain how life takes shape in the womb, you cannot expect to understand how the whole of the world works.
passages.text: As thou knowest not what is the way of the spirit, nor how the bones do grow in the womb of her that is with child: even so thou knowest not the works of God who maketh all.

passages.g: Work in the morning and again in the evening, because you cannot know which effort will succeed, or whether both will.
passages.text: In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening withhold not thine hand: for thou knowest not whether shall prosper, either this or that, or whether they both shall be alike good. Truly the light is sweet, and a pleasant thing it is for the eyes to behold the sun:

passages.g: However many years you enjoy, remember that the dark days will be many; everything that comes is fleeting.
passages.text: But if a man live many years, and rejoice in them all; yet let him remember the days of darkness; for they shall be many. All that cometh is vanity.

passages.g: Enjoy your youth and follow your heart and your eyes, but know that you will be held to account for all of it. Put grief and harm away from you, because youth passes quickly.
passages.text: Rejoice, O young man, in thy youth; and let thy heart cheer thee in the days of thy youth, and walk in the ways of thine heart, and in the sight of thine eyes: but know thou, that for all these things God will bring thee into judgment. Therefore remove sorrow from thy heart, and put away evil from thy flesh: for childhood and youth are vanity.

passages.g: Turn toward what matters most while you are young, before the hard years arrive and pleasure drains out of everything.
passages.text: Remember now thy Creator in the days of thy youth, while the evil days come not, nor the years draw nigh, when thou shalt say, I have no pleasure in them; While the sun, or the light, or the moon, or the stars, be not darkened, nor the clouds return after the rain:

passages.g: Old age arrives as the body fails: the hands tremble, the legs weaken, the teeth are lost, and the eyes go dim.
passages.text: In the day when the keepers of the house shall tremble, and the strong men shall bow themselves, and the grinders cease because they are few, and those that look out of the windows be darkened,

passages.g: In old age a person withdraws from the world, sleeps lightly, wakes at the first bird, and can no longer hear music well.
passages.text: And the doors shall be shut in the streets, when the sound of the grinding is low, and he shall rise up at the voice of the bird, and all the daughters of musick shall be brought low;

passages.g: The old grow afraid of heights and of the road, hair turns white, small burdens become heavy, and desire fades — the person is moving toward death while the mourners gather.
passages.text: Also when they shall be afraid of that which is high, and fears shall be in the way, and the almond tree shall flourish, and the grasshopper shall be a burden, and desire shall fail: because man goeth to his long home, and the mourners go about the streets:

passages.g: Eventually the body's fragile mechanism breaks: dust returns to the earth and breath returns to its source. Everything is fleeting.
passages.text: Or ever the silver cord be loosed, or the golden bowl be broken, or the pitcher be broken at the fountain, or the wheel broken at the cistern. Then shall the dust return to the earth as it was: and the spirit shall return unto God who gave it. Vanity of vanities, saith the preacher; all is vanity.

passages.g: A wise teacher went on teaching, weighed and arranged many sayings, and searched for words that were both pleasing and true.
passages.text: And moreover, because the preacher was wise, he still taught the people knowledge; yea, he gave good heed, and sought out, and set in order many proverbs. The preacher sought to find out acceptable words: and that which was written was upright, even words of truth.

passages.g: The words of the wise prod like a goad and hold like driven nails, all coming from one source; beyond them, books multiply without end, and too much study wears out the body.
passages.text: The words of the wise are as goads, and as nails fastened by the masters of assemblies, which are given from one shepherd. And further, by these, my son, be admonished: of making many books there is no end; and much study is a weariness of the flesh.

passages.g: The sum of it all is to live in reverence and keep your obligations, since that is the whole of a person's task, and every act, including the hidden ones, will be brought to account.
passages.text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil.

passages.g: Those who are spiritually empty and those who grieve are not cursed but favored, and will be filled and comforted.
passages.text: And seeing the multitudes, he went up into a mountain: and when he was set, his disciples came unto him: And he opened his mouth, and taught them, saying, Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted.

passages.g: Gentleness, a deep craving for what is right, mercy, and inner purity are each answered in kind.
passages.text: Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled. Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God.

passages.g: People who make peace, and people who suffer for doing right, hold the highest standing.
passages.text: Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God. Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness’ sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.

passages.g: Being slandered and attacked for your convictions places you among those who came before you, so treat it as cause for gladness rather than despair.
passages.text: Blessed are ye, when men shall revile you, and persecute you, and shall say all manner of evil against you falsely, for my sake. Rejoice, and be exceeding glad: for great is your reward in heaven: for so persecuted they the prophets which were before you.

passages.g: A thing that loses the quality that made it valuable is worthless; and anyone who genuinely has that quality cannot stay hidden.
passages.text: Ye are the salt of the earth: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be salted? it is thenceforth good for nothing, but to be cast out, and to be trodden under foot of men. Ye are the light of the world. A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid.

passages.g: Do good openly, so that others see it and credit what it points to rather than the person doing it.
passages.text: Neither do men light a candle, and put it under a bushel, but on a candlestick; and it giveth light unto all that are in the house. Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.

passages.g: A new teaching completes an old moral law rather than abolishing it, and not the smallest part of that law lapses before its purpose is served.
passages.text: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil. For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled.

passages.g: How you treat even the smallest obligations, and what you teach others about them, determines your worth.
passages.text: Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called the least in the kingdom of heaven: but whosoever shall do and teach them, the same shall be called great in the kingdom of heaven.

passages.g: Merely meeting the letter of a rule, as the professionally religious do, is not enough; the old prohibition punished killing itself.
passages.text: For I say unto you, That except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven. Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not kill; and whosoever shall kill shall be in danger of the judgment:

passages.g: Contempt and unwarranted anger toward another person carry the same moral weight as violence against them.
passages.text: But I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment: and whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council: but whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire.

passages.g: Repair a broken relationship before you attempt any act of devotion.
passages.text: Therefore if thou bring thy gift to the altar, and there rememberest that thy brother hath ought against thee; Leave there thy gift before the altar, and go thy way; first be reconciled to thy brother, and then come and offer thy gift.

passages.g: Settle a dispute early, while you still can, before it escalates past your control.
passages.text: Agree with thine adversary quickly, whiles thou art in the way with him; lest at any time the adversary deliver thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and thou be cast into prison.

passages.g: A debt is exacted to the last coin; and the old prohibition forbade adultery.
passages.text: Verily I say unto thee, Thou shalt by no means come out thence, till thou hast paid the uttermost farthing. Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not commit adultery:

passages.g: Wanting someone in your mind is already the betrayal, whatever your body does.
passages.text: But I say unto you, That whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart.

passages.g: Cut away whatever part of your life leads you to ruin, since losing a part is better than losing the whole.
passages.text: And if thy right eye offend thee, pluck it out, and cast it from thee: for it is profitable for thee that one of thy members should perish, and not that thy whole body should be cast into hell.

passages.g: Sacrifice whatever destroys you, however useful it seems; the old custom allowed a man to dismiss his wife with a certificate.
passages.text: And if thy right hand offend thee, cut it off, and cast it from thee: for it is profitable for thee that one of thy members should perish, and not that thy whole body should be cast into hell. It hath been said, Whosoever shall put away his wife, let him give her a writing of divorcement:

passages.g: Discarding a spouse for any reason but infidelity implicates both parties in the wrong that follows.
passages.text: But I say unto you, That whosoever shall put away his wife, saving for the cause of fornication, causeth her to commit adultery: and whosoever shall marry her that is divorced committeth adultery.

passages.g: Swearing by sacred things adds nothing, because none of them are yours to pledge.
passages.text: Again, ye have heard that it hath been said by them of old time, Thou shalt not forswear thyself, but shalt perform unto the Lord thine oaths: But I say unto you, Swear not at all; neither by heaven; for it is God’s throne:

passages.g: You cannot control even the color of your own hair, so let a plain yes or no be your whole word; anything beyond it comes from bad faith.
passages.text: Nor by the earth; for it is his footstool: neither by Jerusalem; for it is the city of the great King. Neither shalt thou swear by thy head, because thou canst not make one hair white or black. But let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay: for whatsoever is more than these cometh of evil.

passages.g: Do not answer injury with injury; when someone strikes you or takes from you, offer more rather than retaliate.
passages.text: Ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth: But I say unto you, That ye resist not evil: but whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also. And if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloak also.

passages.g: Exceed what is demanded of you and give to whoever asks; the old rule said to love your own and hate your enemies.
passages.text: And whosoever shall compel thee to go a mile, go with him twain. Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn not thou away. Ye have heard that it hath been said, Thou shalt love thy neighbour, and hate thine enemy.

passages.g: Love your enemies, treat well the people who hate you, and wish good on those who mistreat you.
passages.text: But I say unto you, Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you;

passages.g: Nature's gifts fall on good and bad alike, and loving only those who already love you is no achievement at all.
passages.text: That ye may be the children of your Father which is in heaven: for he maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust. For if ye love them which love you, what reward have ye? do not even the publicans the same?

passages.g: Kindness to your own circle is unremarkable; the standard is a goodness as complete and impartial as nature's.
passages.text: And if ye salute your brethren only, what do ye more than others? do not even the publicans so? Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect.

passages.g: Doing good in order to be seen doing it cancels its worth.
passages.text: Take heed that ye do not your alms before men, to be seen of them: otherwise ye have no reward of your Father which is in heaven.

passages.g: Advertised generosity buys only the admiration it seeks; give so quietly that you barely register it yourself.
passages.text: Therefore when thou doest thine alms, do not sound a trumpet before thee, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, that they may have glory of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward. But when thou doest alms, let not thy left hand know what thy right hand doeth:

passages.g: Let your giving be hidden, and what sees in secret will answer it.
passages.text: That thine alms may be in secret: and thy Father which seeth in secret himself shall reward thee openly.

passages.g: Piety performed for onlookers is paid in full by their attention and nothing more.
passages.text: And when thou prayest, thou shalt not be as the hypocrites are: for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and in the corners of the streets, that they may be seen of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

passages.g: Go alone, shut the door, and speak in private to what is itself unseen.
passages.text: But thou, when thou prayest, enter into thy closet, and when thou hast shut thy door, pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly.

passages.g: Piling up words does not make you heard, since what you need is already known before you ask.
passages.text: But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking. Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him.

passages.g: Ask simply: that a greater will be done, that today's needs be met, and that you be forgiven exactly as far as you forgive others.
passages.text: After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come, Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.

passages.g: Ask to be spared from harm; and know that forgiving others is what opens forgiveness to you.
passages.text: And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil: For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen. For if ye forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you:

passages.g: Refuse to forgive and you close off forgiveness for yourself; and self-denial paraded before others is repaid only by their notice.
passages.text: But if ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses. Moreover when ye fast, be not, as the hypocrites, of a sad countenance: for they disfigure their faces, that they may appear unto men to fast. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

passages.g: Conceal your self-denial behind an ordinary face, keeping it between you and what you do it for.
passages.text: But thou, when thou fastest, anoint thine head, and wash thy face; That thou appear not unto men to fast, but unto thy Father which is in secret: and thy Father, which seeth in secret, shall reward thee openly.

passages.g: Invest in what cannot decay or be stolen rather than in what can.
passages.text: Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal: But lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal:

passages.g: Your heart follows whatever you treasure, and a clear, undivided way of seeing fills your whole life with light.
passages.text: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also. The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light.

passages.g: A corrupted way of seeing darkens everything, and when the very faculty meant to give you light is dark, the darkness is total.
passages.text: But if thine eye be evil, thy whole body shall be full of darkness. If therefore the light that is in thee be darkness, how great is that darkness!

passages.g: You cannot devote yourself to two masters with opposed claims; wealth and a higher good will not both hold you.
passages.text: No man can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.

passages.g: Stop anxiously calculating about food and clothing, because life is worth more than the things that maintain it.
passages.text: Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink; nor yet for your body, what ye shall put on. Is not the life more than meat, and the body than raiment?

passages.g: Wild creatures are sustained without storing anything up, and no amount of worry adds a single inch to you.
passages.text: Behold the fowls of the air: for they sow not, neither do they reap, nor gather into barns; yet your heavenly Father feedeth them. Are ye not much better than they? Which of you by taking thought can add one cubit unto his stature?

passages.g: Wildflowers, which do no work at all, outshine the finest king in his robes.
passages.text: And why take ye thought for raiment? Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither do they spin: And yet I say unto you, That even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.

passages.g: If even grass that burns tomorrow is clothed beautifully, then anxious questions about food and clothing betray a failure of trust.
passages.text: Wherefore, if God so clothe the grass of the field, which to day is, and to morrow is cast into the oven, shall he not much more clothe you, O ye of little faith? Therefore take no thought, saying, What shall we eat? or, What shall we drink? or, Wherewithal shall we be clothed?

passages.g: Frantic pursuit of material needs is what people without trust do; aim first at what matters most and the rest follows.
passages.text: (For after all these things do the Gentiles seek:) for your heavenly Father knoweth that ye have need of all these things. But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.

passages.g: Don't carry tomorrow's worries today; each day brings enough trouble of its own.
passages.text: Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof.

passages.g: The standard you use to judge others is the one that will be applied to you, and you fixate on their small faults while ignoring your own enormous ones.
passages.text: Judge not, that ye be not judged. For with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again. And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but considerest not the beam that is in thine own eye?

passages.g: Clear your own far greater faults first; only then can you see well enough to help anyone else with theirs.
passages.text: Or how wilt thou say to thy brother, Let me pull out the mote out of thine eye; and, behold, a beam is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, first cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother’s eye.

passages.g: Don't offer what is precious to those who will only trample it and turn on you; but ask, search, and knock, and you will be answered.
passages.text: Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine, lest they trample them under their feet, and turn again and rend you. Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you:

passages.g: Everyone who persists in asking receives, and no decent parent hands a hungry child something harmful instead of food.
passages.text: For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened. Or what man is there of you, whom if his son ask bread, will he give him a stone? Or if he ask a fish, will he give him a serpent?

passages.g: If flawed people know how to give their children good things, the source of everything gives far more readily; so treat others exactly as you would want to be treated, since this sums up all the rest.
passages.text: If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask him? Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets.

passages.g: The way that leads to ruin is broad, easy, and crowded, while the way that leads to life is narrow and few ever find it.
passages.text: Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and many there be which go in thereat: Because strait is the gate, and narrow is the way, which leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it.

passages.g: Deceivers arrive disguised as what they prey on, so judge them by what they actually produce, since a thing's nature determines its output.
passages.text: Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep’s clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves. Ye shall know them by their fruits. Do men gather grapes of thorns, or figs of thistles? Even so every good tree bringeth forth good fruit; but a corrupt tree bringeth forth evil fruit.

passages.g: Nothing can produce fruit contrary to its own nature, and what yields nothing good is eventually discarded, so results reveal character.
passages.text: A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit, neither can a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit. Every tree that bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire. Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them.

passages.g: Professing allegiance counts for nothing; only actually doing what is asked does.
passages.text: Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom of heaven; but he that doeth the will of my Father which is in heaven.

passages.g: Impressive works performed in a cause's name mean nothing if the person doing them lives corruptly, and such people were never truly known by it.
passages.text: Many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in thy name? and in thy name have cast out devils? and in thy name done many wonderful works? And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity.

passages.g: Whoever hears these teachings and acts on them builds a life on rock, which stands when the storm hits.
passages.text: Therefore whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, I will liken him unto a wise man, which built his house upon a rock: And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell not: for it was founded upon a rock.

passages.g: Whoever hears these teachings and fails to act on them builds a life on sand, and its collapse under pressure is total.
passages.text: And every one that heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a foolish man, which built his house upon the sand: And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell: and great was the fall of it.

passages.g: People were astonished because the teaching carried its own authority rather than borrowing it from established experts.
passages.text: And it came to pass, when Jesus had ended these sayings, the people were astonished at his doctrine: For he taught them as one having authority, and not as the scribes.

passages.g: Truth is taught through ordinary stories, and it is scattered broadly like seed, without regard for where it will land.
passages.text: The same day went Jesus out of the house, and sat by the sea side. And great multitudes were gathered together unto him, so that he went into a ship, and sat; and the whole multitude stood on the shore. And he spake many things unto them in parables, saying, Behold, a sower went forth to sow;

passages.g: Some of what is sown is snatched away at once, and some lands where there is no depth to sustain it and springs up immediately.
passages.text: And when he sowed, some seeds fell by the way side, and the fowls came and devoured them up: Some fell upon stony places, where they had not much earth: and forthwith they sprung up, because they had no deepness of earth:

passages.g: What has no root withers under the first heat, and what is crowded by competing growth is choked, while what falls on good ground yields many times what was sown.
passages.text: And when the sun was up, they were scorched; and because they had no root, they withered away. And some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprung up, and choked them: But other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some sixtyfold, some thirtyfold.

passages.g: Deep teachings are conveyed indirectly, and only those already prepared to grasp them will understand.
passages.text: Who hath ears to hear, let him hear. And the disciples came, and said unto him, Why speakest thou unto them in parables? He answered and said unto them, Because it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given.

passages.g: Understanding compounds: those who already have insight gain more, while those without lose even what little they had.
passages.text: For whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and he shall have more abundance: but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath. Therefore speak I to them in parables: because they seeing see not; and hearing they hear not, neither do they understand.

passages.g: People can hear and see something without ever perceiving its meaning.
passages.text: And in them is fulfilled the prophecy of Esaias, which saith, By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and shall not perceive:

passages.g: People close themselves off from understanding because grasping the truth would require them to change.
passages.text: For this people’s heart is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes they have closed; lest at any time they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears, and should understand with their heart, and should be converted, and I should heal them.

passages.g: Those who actually perceive what is in front of them are fortunate, since many have longed for such insight and never received it.
passages.text: But blessed are your eyes, for they see: and your ears, for they hear. For verily I say unto you, That many prophets and righteous men have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them.

passages.g: A truth heard without being understood is quickly lost.
passages.text: Hear ye therefore the parable of the sower. When any one heareth the word of the kingdom, and understandeth it not, then cometh the wicked one, and catcheth away that which was sown in his heart. This is he which received seed by the way side.

passages.g: Something accepted with enthusiasm but without deep roots collapses at the first hardship.
passages.text: But he that received the seed into stony places, the same is he that heareth the word, and anon with joy receiveth it; Yet hath he not root in himself, but dureth for a while: for when tribulation or persecution ariseth because of the word, by and by he is offended.

passages.g: Anxiety and the lure of wealth crowd out what you have learned and leave it unproductive.
passages.text: He also that received seed among the thorns is he that heareth the word; and the care of this world, and the deceitfulness of riches, choke the word, and he becometh unfruitful.

passages.g: A truth that is both heard and understood becomes fruitful, sometimes many times over.
passages.text: But he that received seed into the good ground is he that heareth the word, and understandeth it; which also beareth fruit, and bringeth forth, some an hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.

passages.g: Corruption is quietly introduced alongside the good while no one is paying attention.
passages.text: Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field: But while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way.

passages.g: The difference between the genuine and the counterfeit only becomes visible once both have matured.
passages.text: But when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also. So the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? from whence then hath it tares?

passages.g: Trying to eliminate what is bad too early destroys the good along with it.
passages.text: He said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said unto him, Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up? But he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them.

passages.g: Let good and bad develop side by side; separation is possible only at the end, when the distinction is clear.
passages.text: Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn.

passages.g: What is ultimately vast can begin as something almost negligible.
passages.text: Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is like to a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and sowed in his field:

passages.g: The smallest beginning can grow into something large enough to shelter others.
passages.text: Which indeed is the least of all seeds: but when it is grown, it is the greatest among herbs, and becometh a tree, so that the birds of the air come and lodge in the branches thereof.

passages.g: A small hidden influence gradually transforms the entire mass it is placed in.
passages.text: Another parable spake he unto them; The kingdom of heaven is like unto leaven, which a woman took, and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened. All these things spake Jesus unto the multitude in parables; and without a parable spake he not unto them:

passages.g: Indirect speech can reveal things that have never been said openly.
passages.text: That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, saying, I will open my mouth in parables; I will utter things which have been kept secret from the foundation of the world.

passages.g: What is genuinely good in the world originates from a good source.
passages.text: Then Jesus sent the multitude away, and went into the house: and his disciples came unto him, saying, Declare unto us the parable of the tares of the field. He answered and said unto them, He that soweth the good seed is the Son of man;

passages.g: The world contains both good and corrupt elements, sown from opposite sources, and they will be sorted only at the end.
passages.text: The field is the world; the good seed are the children of the kingdom; but the tares are the children of the wicked one; The enemy that sowed them is the devil; the harvest is the end of the world; and the reapers are the angels.

passages.g: At the end, everything harmful and every wrongdoer will be gathered out and destroyed.
passages.text: As therefore the tares are gathered and burned in the fire; so shall it be in the end of this world. The Son of man shall send forth his angels, and they shall gather out of his kingdom all things that offend, and them which do iniquity;

passages.g: Wrongdoing ends in ruin and anguish, while the good finally shine plainly for what they are.
passages.text: And shall cast them into a furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth. Then shall the righteous shine forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father. Who hath ears to hear, let him hear.

passages.g: When you discover something of supreme value, giving up everything else to obtain it is worth it, and a genuine seeker recognizes it when found.
passages.text: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto treasure hid in a field; the which when a man hath found, he hideth, and for joy thereof goeth and selleth all that he hath, and buyeth that field. Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a merchant man, seeking goodly pearls:

passages.g: The one thing of ultimate worth is worth everything you own, and life gathers all kinds of people indiscriminately.
passages.text: Who, when he had found one pearl of great price, went and sold all that he had, and bought it. Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a net, that was cast into the sea, and gathered of every kind:

passages.g: In the end what is worthwhile will be kept and what is not will be discarded, and the good will be separated from the wicked.
passages.text: Which, when it was full, they drew to shore, and sat down, and gathered the good into vessels, but cast the bad away. So shall it be at the end of the world: the angels shall come forth, and sever the wicked from among the just,

passages.g: Wrongdoing ends in destruction and misery, and people often claim to understand teachings they have only just heard.
passages.text: And shall cast them into the furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth. Jesus saith unto them, Have ye understood all these things? They say unto him, Yea, Lord.

passages.g: Someone truly educated draws on both old and new knowledge rather than only one.
passages.text: Then said he unto them, Therefore every scribe which is instructed unto the kingdom of heaven is like unto a man that is an householder, which bringeth forth out of his treasure things new and old. And it came to pass, that when Jesus had finished these parables, he departed thence.

passages.g: People are astonished when wisdom and power appear in someone they thought they knew.
passages.text: And when he was come into his own country, he taught them in their synagogue, insomuch that they were astonished, and said, Whence hath this man this wisdom, and these mighty works?

passages.g: Familiarity with a person's ordinary origins makes people refuse to credit their extraordinary qualities.
passages.text: Is not this the carpenter’s son? is not his mother called Mary? and his brethren, James, and Joses, and Simon, and Judas? And his sisters, are they not all with us? Whence then hath this man all these things?

passages.g: A person is least honored where they are best known, and disbelief in them limits what they can accomplish there.
passages.text: And they were offended in him. But Jesus said unto them, A prophet is not without honour, save in his own country, and in his own house. And he did not many mighty works there because of their unbelief.

passages.g: Greatness is not what the ambitious imagine it to be.
passages.text: At the same time came the disciples unto Jesus, saying, Who is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven? And Jesus called a little child unto him, and set him in the midst of them,

passages.g: Whoever makes themselves lowest, with a child's lack of status, is in truth the greatest.
passages.text: And said, Verily I say unto you, Except ye be converted, and become as little children, ye shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven. Whosoever therefore shall humble himself as this little child, the same is greatest in the kingdom of heaven.

passages.g: Welcoming the powerless honors what is highest, and harming them is the gravest of wrongs.
passages.text: And whoso shall receive one such little child in my name receiveth me. But whoso shall offend one of these little ones which believe in me, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea.

passages.g: Corrupting influences are inevitable in the world, but the person who causes them still bears the guilt.
passages.text: Woe unto the world because of offences! for it must needs be that offences come; but woe to that man by whom the offence cometh!

passages.g: It is better to lose something valuable to you than to let it lead you into ruin.
passages.text: Wherefore if thy hand or thy foot offend thee, cut them off, and cast them from thee: it is better for thee to enter into life halt or maimed, rather than having two hands or two feet to be cast into everlasting fire.

passages.g: Sacrifice even what you most cherish rather than let it destroy you.
passages.text: And if thine eye offend thee, pluck it out, and cast it from thee: it is better for thee to enter into life with one eye, rather than having two eyes to be cast into hell fire.

passages.g: Never treat the insignificant with contempt; they matter supremely, and rescuing the lost is the whole point.
passages.text: Take heed that ye despise not one of these little ones; for I say unto you, That in heaven their angels do always behold the face of my Father which is in heaven. For the Son of man is come to save that which was lost.

passages.g: The one who is lost deserves more attention than the many who are safe.
passages.text: How think ye? if a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray, doth he not leave the ninety and nine, and goeth into the mountains, and seeketh that which is gone astray?

passages.g: Recovering what was lost brings more joy than what was never in danger, and no one should be written off.
passages.text: And if so be that he find it, verily I say unto you, he rejoiceth more of that sheep, than of the ninety and nine which went not astray. Even so it is not the will of your Father which is in heaven, that one of these little ones should perish.

passages.g: Confront someone who wronged you privately first, and widen the circle only if they refuse to listen.
passages.text: Moreover if thy brother shall trespass against thee, go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone: if he shall hear thee, thou hast gained thy brother. But if he will not hear thee, then take with thee one or two more, that in the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established.

passages.g: Someone who refuses correction from everyone eventually places themselves outside the community.
passages.text: And if he shall neglect to hear them, tell it unto the church: but if he neglect to hear the church, let him be unto thee as an heathen man and a publican.

passages.g: Decisions made by a genuine community carry weight beyond themselves.
passages.text: Verily I say unto you, Whatsoever ye shall bind on earth shall be bound in heaven: and whatsoever ye shall loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven.

passages.g: Genuine agreement among even a few people has real power, and what is sacred is present wherever they gather.
passages.text: Again I say unto you, That if two of you shall agree on earth as touching any thing that they shall ask, it shall be done for them of my Father which is in heaven. For where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them.

passages.g: Forgiveness should have no limit or countable end.
passages.text: Then came Peter to him, and said, Lord, how oft shall my brother sin against me, and I forgive him? till seven times? Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times: but, Until seventy times seven.

passages.g: Everyone stands under an obligation far larger than they can ever repay.
passages.text: Therefore is the kingdom of heaven likened unto a certain king, which would take account of his servants. And when he had begun to reckon, one was brought unto him, which owed him ten thousand talents.

passages.g: Facing ruin over an unpayable debt, a person can only beg for mercy they cannot earn.
passages.text: But forasmuch as he had not to pay, his lord commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and children, and all that he had, and payment to be made. The servant therefore fell down, and worshipped him, saying, Lord, have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.

passages.g: A person who has been forgiven an enormous debt may still ruthlessly demand a trivial one from someone else.
passages.text: Then the lord of that servant was moved with compassion, and loosed him, and forgave him the debt. But the same servant went out, and found one of his fellowservants, which owed him an hundred pence: and he laid hands on him, and took him by the throat, saying, Pay me that thou owest.

passages.g: People refuse others the very patience they themselves were shown.
passages.text: And his fellowservant fell down at his feet, and besought him, saying, Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all. And he would not: but went and cast him into prison, till he should pay the debt.

passages.g: Refusing mercy after receiving it is a wickedness that others see and condemn.
passages.text: So when his fellowservants saw what was done, they were very sorry, and came and told unto their lord all that was done. Then his lord, after that he had called him, said unto him, O thou wicked servant, I forgave thee all that debt, because thou desiredst me:

passages.g: Having been shown compassion obliges you to show it, and refusing forfeits the mercy you were given.
passages.text: Shouldest not thou also have had compassion on thy fellowservant, even as I had pity on thee? And his lord was wroth, and delivered him to the tormentors, till he should pay all that was due unto him.

passages.g: Mercy withheld from others is mercy forfeited for yourself, and forgiveness must be sincere rather than formal.
passages.text: So likewise shall my heavenly Father do also unto you, if ye from your hearts forgive not every one his brother their trespasses.

passages.g: Wisdom means preparing for a delay you cannot predict; foolishness means bringing only what suffices for the present moment.
passages.text: Then shall the kingdom of heaven be likened unto ten virgins, which took their lamps, and went forth to meet the bridegroom. And five of them were wise, and five were foolish. They that were foolish took their lamps, and took no oil with them: But the wise took oil in their vessels with their lamps.

passages.g: When the long-awaited moment finally arrives without warning, the unprepared discover their resources are already spent.
passages.text: While the bridegroom tarried, they all slumbered and slept. And at midnight there was a cry made, Behold, the bridegroom cometh; go ye out to meet him. Then all those virgins arose, and trimmed their lamps. And the foolish said unto the wise, Give us of your oil; for our lamps are gone out.

passages.g: Some preparation cannot be borrowed or acquired at the last moment, and by the time you seek it the opportunity has closed.
passages.text: But the wise answered, saying, Not so; lest there be not enough for us and you: but go ye rather to them that sell, and buy for yourselves. And while they went to buy, the bridegroom came; and they that were ready went in with him to the marriage: and the door was shut.

passages.g: An opportunity missed cannot be reclaimed by pleading, so stay ready, since you never know when the decisive moment comes.
passages.text: Afterward came also the other virgins, saying, Lord, Lord, open to us. But he answered and said, Verily I say unto you, I know you not. Watch therefore, for ye know neither the day nor the hour wherein the Son of man cometh.

passages.g: People are entrusted with resources in differing amounts, each according to their own capacity.
passages.text: For the kingdom of heaven is as a man travelling into a far country, who called his own servants, and delivered unto them his goods. And unto one he gave five talents, to another two, and to another one; to every man according to his several ability; and straightway took his journey.

passages.g: Those who use what they are given increase it; those who fear losing it hide it away and produce nothing.
passages.text: Then he that had received the five talents went and traded with the same, and made them other five talents. And likewise he that had received two, he also gained other two. But he that had received one went and digged in the earth, and hid his lord’s money.

passages.g: Eventually everyone is asked to account for what they did with what they were given.
passages.text: After a long time the lord of those servants cometh, and reckoneth with them. And so he that had received five talents came and brought other five talents, saying, Lord, thou deliveredst unto me five talents: behold, I have gained beside them five talents more.

passages.g: Proving trustworthy in small responsibilities earns larger ones and genuine reward.
passages.text: His lord said unto him, Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things: enter thou into the joy of thy lord.

passages.g: Someone given less can still double what they were given.
passages.text: He also that had received two talents came and said, Lord, thou deliveredst unto me two talents: behold, I have gained two other talents beside them.

passages.g: Faithfulness is measured against what you were given, not against what others were given, so a smaller yield earns the same praise.
passages.text: His lord said unto him, Well done, good and faithful servant; thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things: enter thou into the joy of thy lord.

passages.g: Fear of a harsh judge makes people protect what they were given rather than risk it, so they produce nothing.
passages.text: Then he which had received the one talent came and said, Lord, I knew thee that thou art an hard man, reaping where thou hast not sown, and gathering where thou hast not strawed: And I was afraid, and went and hid thy talent in the earth: lo, there thou hast that is thine.

passages.g: Fearing the person you answer to is no excuse for inaction; if you truly believed the stakes were high, you would have made at least some effort.
passages.text: His lord answered and said unto him, Thou wicked and slothful servant, thou knewest that I reap where I sowed not, and gather where I have not strawed: Thou oughtest therefore to have put my money to the exchangers, and then at my coming I should have received mine own with usury.

passages.g: Advantage compounds: those who already have gain more, while those with little lose even what they hold.
passages.text: Take therefore the talent from him, and give it unto him which hath ten talents. For unto every one that hath shall be given, and he shall have abundance: but from him that hath not shall be taken away even that which he hath.

passages.g: Those who make nothing of what they were entrusted with are cast out, and a reckoning eventually arrives for everyone.
passages.text: And cast ye the unprofitable servant into outer darkness: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth. When the Son of man shall come in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then shall he sit upon the throne of his glory:

passages.g: In the end people are sorted into two kinds, and the division is made by someone who can see what they actually are.
passages.text: And before him shall be gathered all nations: and he shall separate them one from another, as a shepherd divideth his sheep from the goats: And he shall set the sheep on his right hand, but the goats on the left.

passages.g: Those who fed the hungry, gave drink to the thirsty, and welcomed strangers are the ones who inherit what was prepared for them.
passages.text: Then shall the King say unto them on his right hand, Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world: For I was an hungred, and ye gave me meat: I was thirsty, and ye gave me drink: I was a stranger, and ye took me in:

passages.g: People who genuinely do good do it without realizing they are serving anything greater than the person in front of them.
passages.text: Naked, and ye clothed me: I was sick, and ye visited me: I was in prison, and ye came unto me. Then shall the righteous answer him, saying, Lord, when saw we thee an hungred, and fed thee? or thirsty, and gave thee drink? When saw we thee a stranger, and took thee in? or naked, and clothed thee?

passages.g: How you treat the lowest and least powerful person is how you treat what is most sacred.
passages.text: Or when saw we thee sick, or in prison, and came unto thee? And the King shall answer and say unto them, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these my brethren, ye have done it unto me.

passages.g: Those who saw need and gave nothing bring their own condemnation on themselves.
passages.text: Then shall he say also unto them on the left hand, Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the devil and his angels: For I was an hungred, and ye gave me no meat: I was thirsty, and ye gave me no drink:

passages.g: People who withhold help do not notice they are withholding it; the neglect feels like nothing at the time.
passages.text: I was a stranger, and ye took me not in: naked, and ye clothed me not: sick, and in prison, and ye visited me not. Then shall they also answer him, saying, Lord, when saw we thee an hungred, or athirst, or a stranger, or naked, or sick, or in prison, and did not minister unto thee?

passages.g: Failing the least person is failing the highest, and the consequences of that failure are lasting.
passages.text: Then shall he answer them, saying, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye did it not to one of the least of these, ye did it not to me. And these shall go away into everlasting punishment: but the righteous into life eternal.

passages.g: Rule-keepers object when hungry people meet their basic needs in a way that breaks the letter of a sacred law.
passages.text: And it came to pass on the second sabbath after the first, that he went through the corn fields; and his disciples plucked the ears of corn, and did eat, rubbing them in their hands. And certain of the Pharisees said unto them, Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath days?

passages.g: Even revered figures broke ritual rules when they were hungry, and the tradition honors them anyway.
passages.text: And Jesus answering them said, Have ye not read so much as this, what David did, when himself was an hungred, and they which were with him;

passages.g: Sacred rules exist to serve human need, not the reverse, and a person may stand above the rule.
passages.text: How he went into the house of God, and did take and eat the shewbread, and gave also to them that were with him; which it is not lawful to eat but for the priests alone? And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.

passages.g: Legalists watch a good person closely, hoping to catch him doing good at a forbidden moment so they can accuse him.
passages.text: And it came to pass also on another sabbath, that he entered into the synagogue and taught: and there was a man whose right hand was withered. And the scribes and Pharisees watched him, whether he would heal on the sabbath day; that they might find an accusation against him.

passages.g: If a rule forbids you from doing good or saving a life, you have misunderstood the rule.
passages.text: But he knew their thoughts, and said to the man which had the withered hand, Rise up, and stand forth in the midst. And he arose and stood forth. Then said Jesus unto them, I will ask you one thing; Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?

passages.g: Doing obvious good in defiance of rigid convention enrages the people who enforce it.
passages.text: And looking round about upon them all, he said unto the man, Stretch forth thy hand. And he did so: and his hand was restored whole as the other. And they were filled with madness; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus.

passages.g: Withdraw and reflect at length before choosing the people who will carry your work.
passages.text: And it came to pass in those days, that he went out into a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God. And when it was day, he called unto him his disciples: and of them he chose twelve, whom also he named apostles;

passages.g: Any chosen circle, however carefully selected, will contain someone who betrays it.
passages.text: Simon, (whom he also named Peter,) and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew, Matthew and Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes, And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also was the traitor.

passages.g: People travel from far outside their own regions to hear a teacher and to be healed.
passages.text: And he came down with them, and stood in the plain, and the company of his disciples, and a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him, and to be healed of their diseases;

passages.g: Something goes out of a person who heals others; and the poor, not the comfortable, are the ones who possess what matters.
passages.text: And they that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed. And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all. And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said, Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God.

passages.g: Those who go hungry and grieve now will be filled and will laugh, and being hated and slandered for a worthy cause places you in good company.
passages.text: Blessed are ye that hunger now: for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh. Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you, and when they shall separate you from their company, and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man’s sake.

passages.g: Rejoice when you are persecuted, because the reward is deferred; the rich, by contrast, have already collected theirs.
passages.text: Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy: for, behold, your reward is great in heaven: for in the like manner did their fathers unto the prophets. But woe unto you that are rich! for ye have received your consolation.

passages.g: Present fullness, laughter, and universal approval are all borrowed against a future reversal; and the right response to enemies is love and active kindness.
passages.text: Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now! for ye shall mourn and weep. Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets. But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you,

passages.g: Answer hostility with blessing, and when someone strikes or takes from you, do not retaliate or resist.
passages.text: Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you. And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloak forbid not to take thy coat also.

passages.g: Give to whoever asks and let go of what is taken; treat others exactly as you would want to be treated.
passages.text: Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again. And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise. For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? for sinners also love those that love them.

passages.g: There is no moral credit in loving, helping, or lending only to those who will return the favor, since even the worst people do that.
passages.text: And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? for sinners also do even the same. And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? for sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again.

passages.g: Give and lend expecting nothing back, imitating a generosity that extends even to the ungrateful and the wicked.
passages.text: But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil. Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful.

passages.g: Refrain from judging and condemning, and forgive, because you will be treated as you treat others.
passages.text: Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven:

passages.g: Give generously and generosity overflows back to you; the standard you apply is the standard applied to you.
passages.text: Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again.

passages.g: Someone who cannot see cannot guide others, and a student can at best only equal the teacher, never exceed him.
passages.text: And he spake a parable unto them, Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch? The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master.

passages.g: People notice trivial flaws in others while remaining blind to enormous ones in themselves.
passages.text: And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?

passages.g: You cannot help correct someone else's fault until you have removed the larger one obstructing your own vision.
passages.text: Either how canst thou say to thy brother, Brother, let me pull out the mote that is in thine eye, when thou thyself beholdest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly to pull out the mote that is in thy brother’s eye.

passages.g: Character reliably produces its own kind, so a person is known by what they actually produce, not by what they claim.
passages.text: For a good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit; neither doth a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit. For every tree is known by his own fruit. For of thorns men do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes.

passages.g: What a person has stored inside them is what comes out in their speech and actions, so professing loyalty without acting on it is empty.
passages.text: A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil: for of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh. And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?

passages.g: The person who both hears and acts on what they hear is fundamentally different from one who only hears.
passages.text: Whosoever cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them, I will shew you to whom he is like:

passages.g: A life built slowly on solid ground holds when crisis strikes it.
passages.text: He is like a man which built an house, and digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock: and when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently upon that house, and could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock.

passages.g: Hearing without acting builds on nothing, and when crisis comes the collapse is total.
passages.text: But he that heareth, and doeth not, is like a man that without a foundation built an house upon the earth; against which the stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell; and the ruin of that house was great.

passages.g: Hypocrisy works like yeast, spreading invisibly through a whole group, so guard against it from the start.
passages.text: In the mean time, when there were gathered together an innumerable multitude of people, insomuch that they trode one upon another, he began to say unto his disciples first of all, Beware ye of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.

passages.g: Nothing stays concealed; whatever is whispered in private will eventually be announced publicly.
passages.text: For there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed; neither hid, that shall not be known. Therefore whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light; and that which ye have spoken in the ear in closets shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.

passages.g: Do not fear those who can only destroy your body, since their power ends there; fear only what can destroy what is deeper.
passages.text: And I say unto you my friends, Be not afraid of them that kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do. But I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear: Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him.

passages.g: Nothing, however small, goes unnoticed or forgotten, so you have no reason to be afraid.
passages.text: Are not five sparrows sold for two farthings, and not one of them is forgotten before God? But even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not therefore: ye are of more value than many sparrows.

passages.g: Publicly standing by what you believe, or publicly disowning it, determines how you are finally recognized.
passages.text: Also I say unto you, Whosoever shall confess me before men, him shall the Son of man also confess before the angels of God: But he that denieth me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.

passages.g: Most offenses can be forgiven, but rejecting the very source of truth cannot be, because it removes the means of forgiveness.
passages.text: And whosoever shall speak a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that blasphemeth against the Holy Ghost it shall not be forgiven.

passages.g: When you are hauled before authorities to defend yourself, do not rehearse; the words you need will come when the moment arrives.
passages.text: And when they bring you unto the synagogues, and unto magistrates, and powers, take ye no thought how or what thing ye shall answer, or what ye shall say: For the Holy Ghost shall teach you in the same hour what ye ought to say.

passages.g: Refuse to be pulled into other people's disputes over money and inheritance; it is not your role to arbitrate them.
passages.text: And one of the company said unto him, Master, speak to my brother, that he divide the inheritance with me. And he said unto him, Man, who made me a judge or a divider over you?

passages.g: Guard against greed, because a person's life does not consist of the abundance of what they own.
passages.text: And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man’s life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth. And he spake a parable unto them, saying, The ground of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully:

passages.g: A man whose success outgrows his storage responds by building bigger storage.
passages.text: And he thought within himself, saying, What shall I do, because I have no room where to bestow my fruits? And he said, This will I do: I will pull down my barns, and build greater; and there will I bestow all my fruits and my goods.

passages.g: You can stockpile enough for many years and lose your life the same night, leaving everything you accumulated to someone else.
passages.text: And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry. But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee: then whose shall those things be, which thou hast provided?

passages.g: Hoarding for yourself while remaining spiritually empty is folly; life is more than food and the body is more than clothing.
passages.text: So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God. And he said unto his disciples, Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on. The life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment.

passages.g: Wild birds neither sow nor store yet are fed, and no amount of worry can add a single inch to your height.
passages.text: Consider the ravens: for they neither sow nor reap; which neither have storehouse nor barn; and God feedeth them: how much more are ye better than the fowls? And which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?

passages.g: If anxiety cannot accomplish the smallest thing, it is pointless to apply it to the rest; wildflowers outshine kings without laboring at all.
passages.text: If ye then be not able to do that thing which is least, why take ye thought for the rest? Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.

passages.g: If even grass that lasts a day is clothed beautifully, anxiety about your own provision reveals a failure of trust.
