passages.text: If then God so clothe the grass, which is to day in the field, and to morrow is cast into the oven; how much more will he clothe you, O ye of little faith? And seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink, neither be ye of doubtful mind.

passages.g: Everyone chases food and drink, but what you actually need is already known; pursue what is highest and the rest follows.
passages.text: For all these things do the nations of the world seek after: and your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things. But rather seek ye the kingdom of God; and all these things shall be added unto you. Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father’s good pleasure to give you the kingdom.

passages.g: Sell what you own and give it away, storing value where it cannot be stolen or decay, because your heart inevitably follows whatever you treasure.
passages.text: Sell that ye have, and give alms; provide yourselves bags which wax not old, a treasure in the heavens that faileth not, where no thief approacheth, neither moth corrupteth. For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also. Let your loins be girded about, and your lights burning;

passages.g: Live like someone expecting a return at any moment, ready to respond the instant it comes.
passages.text: And ye yourselves like unto men that wait for their lord, when he will return from the wedding; that when he cometh and knocketh, they may open unto him immediately.

passages.g: Those found alert when the reckoning arrives are rewarded, and the one they waited for serves them in turn.
passages.text: Blessed are those servants, whom the lord when he cometh shall find watching: verily I say unto you, that he shall gird himself, and make them to sit down to meat, and will come forth and serve them.

passages.g: Since no one knows the hour, readiness cannot be timed; if the householder had known when the thief would come, he would have stayed awake.
passages.text: And if he shall come in the second watch, or come in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants. And this know, that if the goodman of the house had known what hour the thief would come, he would have watched, and not have suffered his house to be broken through.

passages.g: Be ready always, because the decisive moment comes precisely when you do not expect it.
passages.text: Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not. Then Peter said unto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable unto us, or even to all?

passages.g: The trustworthy person is the one found doing their assigned work faithfully at whatever moment they happen to be checked.
passages.text: And the Lord said, Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall make ruler over his household, to give them their portion of meat in due season? Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing.

passages.g: People entrusted with authority who believe no one is coming to check on them begin to abuse those under them and indulge themselves.
passages.text: Of a truth I say unto you, that he will make him ruler over all that he hath. But and if that servant say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming; and shall begin to beat the menservants and maidens, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken;

passages.g: A reckoning arrives at the moment one least expects it, and those who betrayed their trust end up sharing the fate of those who never had any.
passages.text: The lord of that servant will come in a day when he looketh not for him, and at an hour when he is not aware, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the unbelievers.

passages.g: Someone who understood what was required of them and did not do it deserves harsher punishment than someone who did not understand.
passages.text: And that servant, which knew his lord’s will, and prepared not himself, neither did according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

passages.g: Ignorance reduces culpability, so those who did wrong without knowing better are punished less. The more a person has been given or entrusted with, the more is rightly demanded of them.
passages.text: But he that knew not, and did commit things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more.

passages.g: A message that demands real change sets the world on fire rather than calming it, and it brings division rather than peace. The one who carries such a message lives under strain until it is finished.
passages.text: I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled? But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished! Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division:

passages.g: A message that demands real change splits even a single household into opposing camps.
passages.text: For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three.

passages.g: Deep conviction divides people along the lines of their closest family ties, setting parent against child.
passages.text: The father shall be divided against the son, and the son against the father; the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother; the mother in law against her daughter in law, and the daughter in law against her mother in law.

passages.g: People become skilled at reading the signs of ordinary, practical things and predicting what will follow.
passages.text: And he said also to the people, When ye see a cloud rise out of the west, straightway ye say, There cometh a shower; and so it is. And when ye see the south wind blow, ye say, There will be heat; and it cometh to pass.

passages.g: It is inconsistent to interpret everyday signs expertly while refusing to interpret the moral significance of your own moment, when you are perfectly capable of judging for yourself what is right.
passages.text: Ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky and of the earth; but how is it that ye do not discern this time? Yea, and why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?

passages.g: Settle a dispute with your opponent early, while you still can, rather than letting it escalate into a formal judgment you cannot control.
passages.text: When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison.

passages.g: Once a judgment is handed down, the full price must be paid; there is no partial escape.
passages.text: I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite.

passages.g: Those looking for a reason to condemn you watch closely for the moment a human need collides with a rule.
passages.text: And it came to pass, as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharisees to eat bread on the sabbath day, that they watched him. And, behold, there was a certain man before him which had the dropsy.

passages.g: When asked directly whether mercy is permitted, those who enforce rules against it fall silent, because they cannot defend the position out loud.
passages.text: And Jesus answering spake unto the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day? And they held their peace. And he took him, and healed him, and let him go;

passages.g: People suspend their own strict rules the moment their own interests are at stake, which shows the rules were never applied consistently.
passages.text: And answered them, saying, Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day? And they could not answer him again to these things.

passages.g: Do not claim the most prominent position for yourself, because someone more deserving may arrive.
passages.text: And he put forth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked how they chose out the chief rooms; saying unto them, When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not down in the highest room; lest a more honourable man than thou be bidden of him;

passages.g: Being publicly demoted from a place you claimed for yourself is more humiliating than never claiming it.
passages.text: And he that bade thee and him come and say to thee, Give this man place; and thou begin with shame to take the lowest room.

passages.g: Take the lowest place, and any honor you receive will come from others rather than from yourself, which is the only kind that carries weight.
passages.text: But when thou art bidden, go and sit down in the lowest room; that when he that bade thee cometh, he may say unto thee, Friend, go up higher: then shalt thou have worship in the presence of them that sit at meat with thee.

passages.g: Those who raise themselves up get brought down, and those who lower themselves get raised.
passages.text: For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.

passages.g: Giving to people who can pay you back is a transaction, not generosity, because you have already collected your reward.
passages.text: Then said he also to him that bade him, When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, neither thy kinsmen, nor thy rich neighbours; lest they also bid thee again, and a recompence be made thee.

passages.g: Real generosity is directed at those who have no way of repaying you.
passages.text: But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind: And thou shalt be blessed; for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just.

passages.g: A genuine invitation is extended widely, to far more people than will accept it.
passages.text: And when one of them that sat at meat with him heard these things, he said unto him, Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God. Then said he unto him, A certain man made a great supper, and bade many:

passages.g: People decline what matters most by pleading obligations to their property.
passages.text: And sent his servant at supper time to say to them that were bidden, Come; for all things are now ready. And they all with one consent began to make excuse. The first said unto him, I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it: I pray thee have me excused.

passages.g: People decline what matters most by pleading obligations to their work and their families.
passages.text: And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee have me excused. And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.

passages.g: When those with standing refuse an invitation, it passes to those who had none.
passages.text: So that servant came, and shewed his lord these things. Then the master of the house being angry said to his servant, Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind.

passages.g: Generosity keeps reaching further outward until nothing is left unfilled.
passages.text: And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room. And the lord said unto the servant, Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.

passages.g: Those who make excuses forfeit the thing they were offered, and it is not held open for them.
passages.text: For I say unto you, That none of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper. And there went great multitudes with him: and he turned, and said unto them,

passages.g: A commitment that comes second to your family ties and your own life is not a real commitment. Anyone unwilling to carry the cost personally has not actually taken it on.
passages.text: If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple. And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come after me, cannot be my disciple.

passages.g: Before starting something, calculate whether you have the resources to finish it.
passages.text: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it? Lest haply, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it begin to mock him,

passages.g: An abandoned project invites ridicule, so any serious undertaking begins with an honest assessment of whether your strength matches what you are up against.
passages.text: Saying, This man began to build, and was not able to finish. Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?

passages.g: If you cannot meet the cost, negotiate rather than pretend, and if you are unwilling to give up everything the thing requires, you cannot have it. Something that has lost the quality that made it valuable cannot get that quality back from outside.
passages.text: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sendeth an ambassage, and desireth conditions of peace. So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple. Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?

passages.g: A thing that has lost its essential quality is useless for every purpose and gets thrown away.
passages.text: It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill; but men cast it out. He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.

passages.g: Welcoming disreputable people as equals reliably scandalizes the respectable.
passages.text: Then drew near unto him all the publicans and sinners for to hear him. And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them. And he spake this parable unto them, saying,

passages.g: A single lost member of a group matters enough to justify leaving the secure majority and going after it.
passages.text: What man of you, having an hundred sheep, if he lose one of them, doth not leave the ninety and nine in the wilderness, and go after that which is lost, until he find it? And when he hath found it, he layeth it on his shoulders, rejoicing.

passages.g: Recovering what was lost is a joy meant to be shared rather than kept private.
passages.text: And when he cometh home, he calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying unto them, Rejoice with me; for I have found my sheep which was lost.

passages.g: Recovering one person who went wrong produces more joy than the continued presence of many who never went wrong.
passages.text: I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance.

passages.g: What is genuinely valuable to you is worth an exhaustive search, however small it is.
passages.text: Either what woman having ten pieces of silver, if she lose one piece, doth not light a candle, and sweep the house, and seek diligently till she find it?

passages.g: Finding what was lost calls for celebration, and the recovery of one person who went wrong is met with joy.
passages.text: And when she hath found it, she calleth her friends and her neighbours together, saying, Rejoice with me; for I have found the piece which I had lost. Likewise, I say unto you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth. And he said, A certain man had two sons:

passages.g: The young take what they are owed, leave, and squander it.
passages.text: And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he divided unto them his living. And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living.

passages.g: Squandered resources leave a person exposed when circumstances turn hard, and dependent on strangers for degrading work.
passages.text: And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land; and he began to be in want. And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.

passages.g: Hitting bottom is often what makes a person see their situation clearly and recognize what they left behind.
passages.text: And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat: and no man gave unto him. And when he came to himself, he said, How many hired servants of my father’s have bread enough and to spare, and I perish with hunger!

passages.g: Genuine repentance means returning, admitting the wrong plainly, and claiming no entitlement to be restored.
passages.text: I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee, And am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants.

passages.g: Real forgiveness runs to meet the returning person before the apology is finished, and does not wait to be asked.
passages.text: And he arose, and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. And the son said unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son.

passages.g: Forgiveness restores a person to their full former standing rather than granting them some lesser, probationary status.
passages.text: But the father said to his servants, Bring forth the best robe, and put it on him; and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet: And bring hither the fatted calf, and kill it; and let us eat, and be merry:

passages.g: The return of someone thought lost is a genuine resurrection worth celebrating, though those who stayed behind may not understand what the celebration is for.
passages.text: For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to be merry. Now his elder son was in the field: and as he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard musick and dancing. And he called one of the servants, and asked what these things meant.

passages.g: The dutiful resent mercy shown to those who did not earn it, and refuse to join the celebration.
passages.text: And he said unto him, Thy brother is come; and thy father hath killed the fatted calf, because he hath received him safe and sound. And he was angry, and would not go in: therefore came his father out, and intreated him.

passages.g: Long obedience can curdle into a sense of entitlement and a grievance that faithfulness was never rewarded the way failure now is.
passages.text: And he answering said to his father, Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment: and yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends:

passages.g: Those who resent another's forgiveness overlook that they possessed all along, freely, everything they thought they were owed.
passages.text: But as soon as this thy son was come, which hath devoured thy living with harlots, thou hast killed for him the fatted calf. And he said unto him, Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine.

passages.g: Restoring what was lost is worth celebrating even when it seems unfair to those who never left.
passages.text: It was meet that we should make merry, and be glad: for this thy brother was dead, and is alive again; and was lost, and is found.

passages.g: Anyone managing what belongs to another will eventually be required to account for how they handled it.
passages.text: And he said also unto his disciples, There was a certain rich man, which had a steward; and the same was accused unto him that he had wasted his goods. And he called him, and said unto him, How is it that I hear this of thee? give an account of thy stewardship; for thou mayest be no longer steward.

passages.g: Faced with losing his position, a person plans shrewdly for what comes after, using what little leverage he still has.
passages.text: Then the steward said within himself, What shall I do? for my lord taketh away from me the stewardship: I cannot dig; to beg I am ashamed. I am resolved what to do, that, when I am put out of the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.

passages.g: A person about to lose power uses it, while it lasts, to put others in his debt.
passages.text: So he called every one of his lord’s debtors unto him, and said unto the first, How much owest thou unto my lord? And he said, An hundred measures of oil. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and sit down quickly, and write fifty.

passages.g: Reducing what others owe you buys goodwill that outlasts the money.
passages.text: Then said he to another, And how much owest thou? And he said, An hundred measures of wheat. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and write fourscore.

passages.g: People who pursue worldly ends are often more clear-eyed and resourceful about achieving them than good people are about theirs.
passages.text: And the lord commended the unjust steward, because he had done wisely: for the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light.

passages.g: Use money to build relationships rather than to accumulate, since it will not last. How a person handles small matters reveals exactly how they will handle large ones.
passages.text: And I say unto you, Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations. He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much.

passages.g: Someone who cannot be trusted with money, or with what belongs to someone else, will never be given anything genuinely valuable or genuinely their own.
passages.text: If therefore ye have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches? And if ye have not been faithful in that which is another man’s, who shall give you that which is your own?

passages.g: No one can serve two ultimate loyalties, because devotion to one becomes contempt for the other, and the pursuit of wealth competes directly with the pursuit of goodness.
passages.text: No servant can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon. And the Pharisees also, who were covetous, heard all these things: and they derided him.

passages.g: People construct justifications for themselves in public while their actual motives remain visible to anyone who can see inward. What a society admires most is often what is worth least.
passages.text: And he said unto them, Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God.

passages.g: An old order gives way to a new one that people press eagerly into, yet the underlying moral law outlasts the physical world.
passages.text: The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached, and every man presseth into it. And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail.

passages.g: Discarding a spouse to marry another is a betrayal, and so is marrying someone who was discarded. A person can live in daily luxury while remaining entirely unmoved by what is outside his door.
passages.text: Whosoever putteth away his wife, and marrieth another, committeth adultery: and whosoever marrieth her that is put away from her husband committeth adultery. There was a certain rich man, which was clothed in purple and fine linen, and fared sumptuously every day:

passages.g: Extreme suffering can sit at the very threshold of extreme comfort and go completely unnoticed.
passages.text: And there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, which was laid at his gate, full of sores, And desiring to be fed with the crumbs which fell from the rich man’s table: moreover the dogs came and licked his sores.

passages.g: Fortunes reverse after death: the neglected poor are comforted and the comfortable rich are not.
passages.text: And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham’s bosom: the rich man also died, and was buried; And in hell he lift up his eyes, being in torments, and seeth Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom.

passages.g: In torment, the once-powerful must beg relief from the very people they ignored.
passages.text: And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame.

passages.g: Those who take all their comfort in this life have no further claim on it afterward.
passages.text: But Abraham said, Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented.

passages.g: After death a person's condition is fixed, and no one can cross from one side to the other.
passages.text: And beside all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed: so that they which would pass from hence to you cannot; neither can they pass to us, that would come from thence. Then he said, I pray thee therefore, father, that thou wouldest send him to my father’s house:

passages.g: The guidance needed to live rightly is already available to anyone who will listen to it.
passages.text: For I have five brethren; that he may testify unto them, lest they also come into this place of torment. Abraham saith unto him, They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them. And he said, Nay, father Abraham: but if one went unto them from the dead, they will repent.

passages.g: People who ignore ordinary evidence will not be persuaded by miraculous evidence either, because refusal to believe is a matter of will rather than proof.
passages.text: And he said unto him, If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead.

passages.g: Real love holds steady to the end, even when you know the end is coming.
passages.text: Now before the feast of the passover, when Jesus knew that his hour was come that he should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved his own which were in the world, he loved them unto the end.

passages.g: Betrayal takes root quietly inside the closest circle, and knowing your own standing lets you meet it without flinching.
passages.text: And supper being ended, the devil having now put into the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, to betray him; Jesus knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he was come from God, and went to God;

passages.g: The person with the highest standing is the one who can afford to take on the lowest task.
passages.text: He riseth from supper, and laid aside his garments; and took a towel, and girded himself. After that he poureth water into a bason, and began to wash the disciples’ feet, and to wipe them with the towel wherewith he was girded.

passages.g: An act of service can be baffling while it is happening and understood only afterward.
passages.text: Then cometh he to Simon Peter: and Peter saith unto him, Lord, dost thou wash my feet? Jesus answered and said unto him, What I do thou knowest not now; but thou shalt know hereafter.

passages.g: Refusing to let someone serve you is pride, and it shuts you out of the relationship.
passages.text: Peter saith unto him, Thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus answered him, If I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me. Simon Peter saith unto him, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.

passages.g: Someone already fundamentally sound needs only small ongoing correction, not repeated wholesale renewal.
passages.text: Jesus saith to him, He that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet, but is clean every whit: and ye are clean, but not all. For he knew who should betray him; therefore said he, Ye are not all clean.

passages.g: If the person at the top serves, no one below can treat service as beneath them.
passages.text: So after he had washed their feet, and had taken his garments, and was set down again, he said unto them, Know ye what I have done to you? Ye call me Master and Lord: and ye say well; for so I am. If I then, your Lord and Master, have washed your feet; ye also ought to wash one another’s feet.

passages.g: An example counts only when it is copied; knowing what is right does you no good until you do it.
passages.text: For I have given you an example, that ye should do as I have done to you. Verily, verily, I say unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord; neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him. If ye know these things, happy are ye if ye do them.

passages.g: Betrayal by an intimate is foreseeable, and saying so in advance turns the shock into confirmation.
passages.text: I speak not of you all: I know whom I have chosen: but that the scripture may be fulfilled, He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me. Now I tell you before it come, that, when it is come to pass, ye may believe that I am he.

passages.g: How you treat someone's representative is how you treat that person.
passages.text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that receiveth whomsoever I send receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me. When Jesus had thus said, he was troubled in spirit, and testified, and said, Verily, verily, I say unto you, that one of you shall betray me.

passages.g: Within a close group, a betrayer is indistinguishable from the loyal.
passages.text: Then the disciples looked one on another, doubting of whom he spake. Now there was leaning on Jesus’ bosom one of his disciples, whom Jesus loved. Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him, that he should ask who it should be of whom he spake.

passages.g: The betrayer is identified by an act of intimacy rather than by exposure.
passages.text: He then lying on Jesus’ breast saith unto him, Lord, who is it? Jesus answered, He it is, to whom I shall give a sop, when I have dipped it. And when he had dipped the sop, he gave it to Judas Iscariot, the son of Simon.

passages.g: Once a person has settled on betrayal, delay only prolongs it.
passages.text: And after the sop Satan entered into him. Then said Jesus unto him, That thou doest, do quickly. Now no man at the table knew for what intent he spake this unto him.

passages.g: Those closest to a decisive turn routinely misread it while it is happening.
passages.text: For some of them thought, because Judas had the bag, that Jesus had said unto him, Buy those things that we have need of against the feast; or, that he should give something to the poor. He then having received the sop went immediately out: and it was night.

passages.g: What looks from outside like defeat can be the moment of greatest honor.
passages.text: Therefore, when he was gone out, Jesus said, Now is the Son of man glorified, and God is glorified in him. If God be glorified in him, God shall also glorify him in himself, and shall straightway glorify him.

passages.g: Love one another in the same way you yourselves have been loved.
passages.text: Little children, yet a little while I am with you. Ye shall seek me: and as I said unto the Jews, Whither I go, ye cannot come; so now I say to you. A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.

passages.g: A community is recognized by how its members treat each other, not by what it claims; and some paths cannot be walked until you are ready for them.
passages.text: By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another. Simon Peter said unto him, Lord, whither goest thou? Jesus answered him, Whither I go, thou canst not follow me now; but thou shalt follow me afterwards.

passages.g: Confidence in your own courage outruns your actual capacity for it.
passages.text: Peter said unto him, Lord, why cannot I follow thee now? I will lay down my life for thy sake. Jesus answered him, Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake? Verily, verily, I say unto thee, The cock shall not crow, till thou hast denied me thrice.

passages.g: There is room enough for everyone, so do not let your heart be troubled.
passages.text: Let not your heart be troubled: ye believe in God, believe also in me. In my Father’s house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.

passages.g: People often already possess the knowledge they insist they lack.
passages.text: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also. And whither I go ye know, and the way ye know. Thomas saith unto him, Lord, we know not whither thou goest; and how can we know the way?

passages.g: The way, the truth, and the life are not separate destinations but one and the same, reached through what stands concretely in front of you.
passages.text: Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me. If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also: and from henceforth ye know him, and have seen him. Philip saith unto him, Lord, shew us the Father, and it sufficeth us.

passages.g: The invisible source is fully visible in what it does and produces.
passages.text: Jesus saith unto him, Have I been so long time with you, and yet hast thou not known me, Philip? he that hath seen me hath seen the Father; and how sayest thou then, Shew us the Father?

passages.g: If someone's words do not convince you, judge them by their works instead.
passages.text: Believest thou not that I am in the Father, and the Father in me? the words that I speak unto you I speak not of myself: but the Father that dwelleth in me, he doeth the works. Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father in me: or else believe me for the very works’ sake.

passages.g: A genuine teacher's students go on to do more than the teacher did.
passages.text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do; because I go unto my Father. And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son.

passages.g: Love shows itself as obedience rather than sentiment.
passages.text: If ye shall ask any thing in my name, I will do it. If ye love me, keep my commandments. And I will pray the Father, and he shall give you another Comforter, that he may abide with you for ever;

passages.g: Truth is invisible to those who have never known it from the inside.
passages.text: Even the Spirit of truth; whom the world cannot receive, because it seeth him not, neither knoweth him: but ye know him; for he dwelleth with you, and shall be in you. I will not leave you comfortless: I will come to you.

passages.g: A life shared with its source outlasts the source's visible presence.
passages.text: Yet a little while, and the world seeth me no more; but ye see me: because I live, ye shall live also. At that day ye shall know that I am in my Father, and ye in me, and I in you.

passages.g: What you actually do reveals whom you love, and that love is what opens deeper knowledge.
passages.text: He that hath my commandments, and keepeth them, he it is that loveth me: and he that loveth me shall be loved of my Father, and I will love him, and will manifest myself to him. Judas saith unto him, not Iscariot, Lord, how is it that thou wilt manifest thyself unto us, and not unto the world?

passages.g: Love makes a dwelling place, and the teaching a true teacher gives was never his own to begin with.
passages.text: Jesus answered and said unto him, If a man love me, he will keep my words: and my Father will love him, and we will come unto him, and make our abode with him. He that loveth me not keepeth not my sayings: and the word which ye hear is not mine, but the Father’s which sent me.

passages.g: What you were once taught is recovered later by an inner teacher that brings it back to mind.
passages.text: These things have I spoken unto you, being yet present with you. But the Comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name, he shall teach you all things, and bring all things to your remembrance, whatsoever I have said unto you.

passages.g: Real peace does not depend on circumstances the way ordinary peace of mind does.
passages.text: Peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you: not as the world giveth, give I unto you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.

passages.g: To love someone is to want their good even when it costs you their presence.
passages.text: Ye have heard how I said unto you, I go away, and come again unto you. If ye loved me, ye would rejoice, because I said, I go unto the Father: for my Father is greater than I. And now I have told you before it come to pass, that, when it is come to pass, ye might believe.

passages.g: Nothing external can get a grip on a person who has nothing inside for it to grip.
passages.text: Hereafter I will not talk much with you: for the prince of this world cometh, and hath nothing in me. But that the world may know that I love the Father; and as the Father gave me commandment, even so I do. Arise, let us go hence.

passages.g: What bears fruit gets cut back so it will bear more; what bears nothing is removed entirely.
passages.text: I am the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman. Every branch in me that beareth not fruit he taketh away: and every branch that beareth fruit, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit. Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you.

passages.g: Nothing bears fruit once it is cut off from its source.
passages.text: Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine; no more can ye, except ye abide in me. I am the vine, ye are the branches: He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing.

passages.g: When your desires stay rooted in the source, what you ask for is what you receive.
passages.text: If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered; and men gather them, and cast them into the fire, and they are burned. If ye abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you.

passages.g: You remain inside love by continuing to act according to it.
passages.text: Herein is my Father glorified, that ye bear much fruit; so shall ye be my disciples. As the Father hath loved me, so have I loved you: continue ye in my love. If ye keep my commandments, ye shall abide in my love; even as I have kept my Father’s commandments, and abide in his love.

passages.g: The highest form of love gives up its own life for the sake of friends, and the point of it is shared joy.
passages.text: These things have I spoken unto you, that my joy might remain in you, and that your joy might be full. This is my commandment, That ye love one another, as I have loved you. Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends.

passages.g: Withholding information keeps people servants; telling them everything makes them friends.
passages.text: Ye are my friends, if ye do whatsoever I command you. Henceforth I call you not servants; for the servant knoweth not what his lord doeth: but I have called you friends; for all things that I have heard of my Father I have made known unto you.

passages.g: You did not do the choosing, and the purpose of being chosen is to produce something that lasts.
passages.text: Ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you, and ordained you, that ye should go and bring forth fruit, and that your fruit should remain: that whatsoever ye shall ask of the Father in my name, he may give it you. These things I command you, that ye love one another.

passages.g: Any group loves its own, so being genuinely different from it guarantees hostility.
passages.text: If the world hate you, ye know that it hated me before it hated you. If ye were of the world, the world would love his own: but because ye are not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.

passages.g: Expect to receive the same treatment your teacher received.
passages.text: Remember the word that I said unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you; if they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also. But all these things will they do unto you for my name’s sake, because they know not him that sent me.

passages.g: Once the truth has been made plain, ignorance is no longer an excuse.
passages.text: If I had not come and spoken unto them, they had not had sin: but now they have no cloak for their sin. He that hateth me hateth my Father also. If I had not done among them the works which none other man did, they had not had sin: but now have they both seen and hated both me and my Father.

passages.g: Hatred often has no cause behind it at all.
passages.text: But this cometh to pass, that the word might be fulfilled that is written in their law, They hated me without a cause. But when the Comforter is come, whom I will send unto you from the Father, even the Spirit of truth, which proceedeth from the Father, he shall testify of me:

passages.g: Firsthand presence from the beginning is what qualifies a person to testify.
passages.text: And ye also shall bear witness, because ye have been with me from the beginning.

passages.g: Offer your whole living self rather than a ritual substitute; that is the only response that makes sense.
passages.text: I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service.

passages.g: Do not let the surrounding culture shape you; be changed instead by remaking the way you think.
passages.text: And be not conformed to this world: but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind, that ye may prove what is that good, and acceptable, and perfect, will of God.

passages.g: Assess yourself soberly, not more highly than you actually are.
passages.text: For I say, through the grace given unto me, to every man that is among you, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think; but to think soberly, according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith.

passages.g: People differ in function but form one body, each belonging to the others.
passages.text: For as we have many members in one body, and all members have not the same office: So we, being many, are one body in Christ, and every one members one of another.

passages.g: Do the thing you are actually gifted for, in proportion to the gift you have.
passages.text: Having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us, whether prophecy, let us prophesy according to the proportion of faith; Or ministry, let us wait on our ministering: or he that teacheth, on teaching;

passages.g: Carry out your role wholeheartedly and in the spirit it calls for, and let your love be genuine rather than performed.
passages.text: Or he that exhorteth, on exhortation: he that giveth, let him do it with simplicity; he that ruleth, with diligence; he that sheweth mercy, with cheerfulness. Let love be without dissimulation. Abhor that which is evil; cleave to that which is good.

passages.g: Prefer other people's honor to your own, stay diligent in work, and stay warm in spirit.
passages.text: Be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love; in honour preferring one another; Not slothful in business; fervent in spirit; serving the Lord; Rejoicing in hope; patient in tribulation; continuing instant in prayer; Distributing to the necessity of saints; given to hospitality.

passages.g: Bless those who harm you, enter into other people's joy and grief, and never think yourself above anyone.
passages.text: Bless them which persecute you: bless, and curse not. Rejoice with them that do rejoice, and weep with them that weep. Be of the same mind one toward another. Mind not high things, but condescend to men of low estate. Be not wise in your own conceits.

passages.g: Never repay evil with evil or take revenge; live at peace with everyone as far as it depends on you.
passages.text: Recompense to no man evil for evil. Provide things honest in the sight of all men. If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men. Dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath: for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord.

passages.g: Meet hostility with practical kindness rather than retaliation; evil is defeated by good, not by more evil.
passages.text: Therefore if thine enemy hunger, feed him; if he thirst, give him drink: for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head. Be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good.

passages.g: Eloquence without love is meaningless noise.
passages.text: Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal.

passages.g: Insight, knowledge, and conviction are worth nothing in a person who lacks love.
passages.text: And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge; and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing.

passages.g: Even total self-sacrifice gains nothing without love. Love is patient and kind, and free of envy, boasting, and self-importance.
passages.text: And though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing. Charity suffereth long, and is kind; charity envieth not; charity vaunteth not itself, is not puffed up,

passages.g: Love is not rude, self-seeking, irritable, or resentful; it takes no pleasure in wrongdoing but delights in truth, and it bears, trusts, hopes, and endures without limit.
passages.text: Doth not behave itself unseemly, seeketh not her own, is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil; Rejoiceth not in iniquity, but rejoiceth in the truth; Beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things.

passages.g: Love outlasts everything else, because all human knowledge and insight is partial and will eventually be superseded.
passages.text: Charity never faileth: but whether there be prophecies, they shall fail; whether there be tongues, they shall cease; whether there be knowledge, it shall vanish away. For we know in part, and we prophesy in part. But when that which is perfect is come, then that which is in part shall be done away.

passages.g: Understanding matures as a person matures, and our present grasp of things is indirect and incomplete compared with seeing them fully.
passages.text: When I was a child, I spake as a child, I understood as a child, I thought as a child: but when I became a man, I put away childish things. For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known.

passages.g: Of faith, hope, and love, love is the greatest.
passages.text: And now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity.

passages.g: Difficulty is worth welcoming, because being tested is what produces endurance.
passages.text: James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad, greeting. My brethren, count it all joy when ye fall into divers temptations; Knowing this, that the trying of your faith worketh patience.

passages.g: Let endurance finish its work and you become complete. Wisdom is freely available to anyone who genuinely asks for it.
passages.text: But let patience have her perfect work, that ye may be perfect and entire, wanting nothing. If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him.

passages.g: Ask with settled conviction rather than divided intent, because a person at odds with himself is unstable in everything he does and gains nothing.
passages.text: But let him ask in faith, nothing wavering. For he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed. For let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord. A double minded man is unstable in all his ways.

passages.g: Ranks are reversed in the end: the lowly are raised up, and the rich are brought down because their standing is as short-lived as a wildflower.
passages.text: Let the brother of low degree rejoice in that he is exalted: But the rich, in that he is made low: because as the flower of the grass he shall pass away.

passages.g: Wealth withers as fast as grass in the heat, and the rich man disappears in the middle of his pursuits.
passages.text: For the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat, but it withereth the grass, and the flower thereof falleth, and the grace of the fashion of it perisheth: so also shall the rich man fade away in his ways.

passages.g: Enduring a trial is rewarded. But no one should blame the divine for tempting him, since what is wholly good neither feels temptation nor supplies it.
passages.text: Blessed is the man that endureth temptation: for when he is tried, he shall receive the crown of life, which the Lord hath promised to them that love him. Let no man say when he is tempted, I am tempted of God: for God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man:

passages.g: Temptation comes from a person's own desire; desire once indulged produces wrongdoing, and wrongdoing fully grown produces death.
passages.text: But every man is tempted, when he is drawn away of his own lust, and enticed. Then when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death. Do not err, my beloved brethren.

passages.g: Everything genuinely good originates in an unchanging source, which brings us into being through truth.
passages.text: Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights, with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning. Of his own will begat he us with the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures.

passages.g: Be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to anger, since human anger produces nothing righteous. Strip away corruption and receive teaching humbly.
passages.text: Wherefore, my beloved brethren, let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath: For the wrath of man worketh not the righteousness of God. Wherefore lay apart all filthiness and superfluity of naughtiness, and receive with meekness the engrafted word, which is able to save your souls.

passages.g: Hearing a teaching without acting on it is self-deception, like glancing at your reflection and immediately forgetting what you look like.
passages.text: But be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves. For if any be a hearer of the word, and not a doer, he is like unto a man beholding his natural face in a glass: For he beholdeth himself, and goeth his way, and straightway forgetteth what manner of man he was.

passages.g: The one who keeps attending to the law and acts on it is blessed in what he does. Religion that leaves the tongue unchecked is worthless.
passages.text: But whoso looketh into the perfect law of liberty, and continueth therein, he being not a forgetful hearer, but a doer of the work, this man shall be blessed in his deed. If any man among you seem to be religious, and bridleth not his tongue, but deceiveth his own heart, this man’s religion is vain.

passages.g: Genuine religion is caring for the vulnerable and keeping yourself uncorrupted by the world.
passages.text: Pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father is this, To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep himself unspotted from the world.

passages.g: Faith is incompatible with treating people differently according to their wealth.
passages.text: My brethren, have not the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with respect of persons. For if there come unto your assembly a man with a gold ring, in goodly apparel, and there come in also a poor man in vile raiment;

passages.g: Seating the well-dressed above the poor makes you a judge with corrupt standards.
passages.text: And ye have respect to him that weareth the gay clothing, and say unto him, Sit thou here in a good place; and say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool: Are ye not then partial in yourselves, and are become judges of evil thoughts?

passages.g: The poor are rich in what matters, yet they are the ones dishonored, while the rich who exploit and drag people into court are deferred to.
passages.text: Hearken, my beloved brethren, Hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom which he hath promised to them that love him? But ye have despised the poor. Do not rich men oppress you, and draw you before the judgment seats?

passages.g: Loving your neighbour as yourself is the highest rule, and favouritism is a straightforward violation of it.
passages.text: Do not they blaspheme that worthy name by the which ye are called? If ye fulfil the royal law according to the scripture, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself, ye do well: But if ye have respect to persons, ye commit sin, and are convinced of the law as transgressors.

passages.g: Moral law is a single whole, so breaking it at one point makes you a lawbreaker, however scrupulous you are elsewhere.
passages.text: For whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all. For he that said, Do not commit adultery, said also, Do not kill. Now if thou commit no adultery, yet if thou kill, thou art become a transgressor of the law.

passages.g: Judgment is merciless toward those who showed no mercy, and mercy overrides judgment. Claimed faith unaccompanied by action cannot save anyone.
passages.text: So speak ye, and so do, as they that shall be judged by the law of liberty. For he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy; and mercy rejoiceth against judgment. What doth it profit, my brethren, though a man say he hath faith, and have not works? can faith save him?

passages.g: Wishing someone well while withholding what they actually need helps nobody; faith without corresponding action is dead.
passages.text: If a brother or sister be naked, and destitute of daily food, And one of you say unto them, Depart in peace, be ye warmed and filled; notwithstanding ye give them not those things which are needful to the body; what doth it profit? Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone.

passages.g: Belief is demonstrated by conduct, not assertion; even demons believe God exists.
passages.text: Yea, a man may say, Thou hast faith, and I have works: shew me thy faith without thy works, and I will shew thee my faith by my works. Thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well: the devils also believe, and tremble. But wilt thou know, O vain man, that faith without works is dead?

passages.g: Faith is completed and proved by the actions that follow from it.
passages.text: Was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac his son upon the altar? Seest thou how faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect?

passages.g: A person is shown to be righteous by what he does, not by belief alone.
passages.text: And the scripture was fulfilled which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed unto him for righteousness: and he was called the Friend of God. Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only.

passages.g: Actions are what vindicate a person, and faith without them is as lifeless as a body without breath.
passages.text: Likewise also was not Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she had received the messengers, and had sent them out another way? For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also.

passages.g: Don't be eager to instruct others, since teachers are judged more strictly. Everyone fails, and anyone who never errs in speech has mastered himself entirely.
passages.text: My brethren, be not many masters, knowing that we shall receive the greater condemnation. For in many things we offend all. If any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man, and able also to bridle the whole body.

passages.g: A very small instrument can steer something enormous.
passages.text: Behold, we put bits in the horses’ mouths, that they may obey us; and we turn about their whole body. Behold also the ships, which though they be so great, and are driven of fierce winds, yet are they turned about with a very small helm, whithersoever the governor listeth.

passages.g: Speech is a small thing that makes large claims and produces consequences out of all proportion to its size.
passages.text: Even so the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things. Behold, how great a matter a little fire kindleth!

passages.g: Speech is a destructive force that can corrupt a person's entire life.
passages.text: And the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity: so is the tongue among our members, that it defileth the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature; and it is set on fire of hell.

passages.g: Humans have tamed every kind of animal, but no one can tame his own tongue; it is restless and poisonous.
passages.text: For every kind of beasts, and of birds, and of serpents, and of things in the sea, is tamed, and hath been tamed of mankind: But the tongue can no man tame; it is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison.

passages.g: It is incoherent to praise and to curse with the same mouth, since a single source cannot yield opposite things.
passages.text: Therewith bless we God, even the Father; and therewith curse we men, which are made after the similitude of God. Out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing. My brethren, these things ought not so to be. Doth a fountain send forth at the same place sweet water and bitter?

passages.g: Nothing produces what is contrary to its own nature. Genuine wisdom shows itself in humble good conduct rather than in claims.
passages.text: Can the fig tree, my brethren, bear olive berries? either a vine, figs? so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh. Who is a wise man and endued with knowledge among you? let him shew out of a good conversation his works with meekness of wisdom.

passages.g: Bitter envy and rivalry are not wisdom but a lower, destructive substitute, and wherever they exist there is disorder and every kind of harm.
passages.text: But if ye have bitter envying and strife in your hearts, glory not, and lie not against the truth. This wisdom descendeth not from above, but is earthly, sensual, devilish. For where envying and strife is, there is confusion and every evil work.

passages.g: Real wisdom is pure, peaceable, gentle, open to reason, merciful, impartial, and sincere, and righteousness grows out of peacemaking.
passages.text: But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy. And the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace of them that make peace.

passages.g: Conflict originates in unsatisfied craving: people fight because they want what they don't have.
passages.text: From whence come wars and fightings among you? come they not hence, even of your lusts that war in your members? Ye lust, and have not: ye kill, and desire to have, and cannot obtain: ye fight and war, yet ye have not, because ye ask not.

passages.g: Requests go unanswered when their motive is self-indulgence, and allegiance to the world's values amounts to hostility toward God.
passages.text: Ye ask, and receive not, because ye ask amiss, that ye may consume it upon your lusts. Ye adulterers and adulteresses, know ye not that the friendship of the world is enmity with God? whosoever therefore will be a friend of the world is the enemy of God.

passages.g: The proud are opposed and the humble are given grace, so yield to what is good and resist what is evil, and it will retreat.
passages.text: Do ye think that the scripture saith in vain, The spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to envy? But he giveth more grace. Wherefore he saith, God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble. Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.

passages.g: Move toward God and he moves toward you. Clean up your conduct, take your failings seriously enough to grieve them, and humility will raise you.
passages.text: Draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you. Cleanse your hands, ye sinners; and purify your hearts, ye double minded. Be afflicted, and mourn, and weep: let your laughter be turned to mourning, and your joy to heaviness. Humble yourselves in the sight of the Lord, and he shall lift you up.

passages.g: Slandering and judging another person sets you above the law rather than under it.
passages.text: Speak not evil one of another, brethren. He that speaketh evil of his brother, and judgeth his brother, speaketh evil of the law, and judgeth the law: but if thou judge the law, thou art not a doer of the law, but a judge.

passages.g: There is only one lawgiver and judge, so no one else is qualified to pass judgment on another.
passages.text: There is one lawgiver, who is able to save and to destroy: who art thou that judgest another? Go to now, ye that say, To day or to morrow we will go into such a city, and continue there a year, and buy and sell, and get gain:

passages.g: You do not know what tomorrow holds and life is brief, so confident boasting about future plans is arrogance.
passages.text: Whereas ye know not what shall be on the morrow. For what is your life? It is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time, and then vanisheth away. For that ye ought to say, If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this, or that. But now ye rejoice in your boastings: all such rejoicing is evil.

passages.g: Knowing the right thing to do and failing to do it is itself a wrong.
passages.text: Therefore to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin.

passages.g: Hoarded wealth rots, and those who trust in it should expect misery.
passages.text: Go to now, ye rich men, weep and howl for your miseries that shall come upon you. Your riches are corrupted, and your garments are motheaten.

passages.g: Accumulated riches corrode, and the corrosion becomes evidence against those who stockpiled them.
passages.text: Your gold and silver is cankered; and the rust of them shall be a witness against you, and shall eat your flesh as it were fire. Ye have heaped treasure together for the last days.

passages.g: Wages withheld from the workers who earned them cry out and are heard.
passages.text: Behold, the hire of the labourers who have reaped down your fields, which is of you kept back by fraud, crieth: and the cries of them which have reaped are entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth.

passages.g: Living in luxury and self-indulgence while condemning and destroying the defenceless is a fattening of yourself for slaughter.
passages.text: Ye have lived in pleasure on the earth, and been wanton; ye have nourished your hearts, as in a day of slaughter. Ye have condemned and killed the just; and he doth not resist you.

passages.g: Be patient the way a farmer is patient waiting for a crop, and steady your resolve while you wait.
passages.text: Be patient therefore, brethren, unto the coming of the Lord. Behold, the husbandman waiteth for the precious fruit of the earth, and hath long patience for it, until he receive the early and latter rain. Be ye also patient; stablish your hearts: for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh.

passages.g: Don't hold grudges against one another; take those who suffered and stayed patient as your model.
passages.text: Grudge not one against another, brethren, lest ye be condemned: behold, the judge standeth before the door. Take, my brethren, the prophets, who have spoken in the name of the Lord, for an example of suffering affliction, and of patience.

passages.g: We count those who endure as fortunate, because the outcome reveals compassion and mercy.
passages.text: Behold, we count them happy which endure. Ye have heard of the patience of Job, and have seen the end of the Lord; that the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy.

passages.g: Don't swear oaths; let a plain yes and no be enough. Pray when you suffer and sing when you are glad.
passages.text: But above all things, my brethren, swear not, neither by heaven, neither by the earth, neither by any other oath: but let your yea be yea; and your nay, nay; lest ye fall into condemnation. Is any among you afflicted? let him pray. Is any merry? let him sing psalms.

passages.g: When someone is ill, the community should gather and pray for them, and prayer offered in faith restores and forgives.
passages.text: Is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church; and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord: And the prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up; and if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven him.

passages.g: Admit your failings to one another and pray for one another, because that is how healing comes, and the earnest prayer of a good person accomplishes a great deal.
passages.text: Confess your faults one to another, and pray one for another, that ye may be healed. The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much.

passages.g: An ordinary person with the same weaknesses as anyone else can, by praying earnestly, move even the natural world.
passages.text: Elias was a man subject to like passions as we are, and he prayed earnestly that it might not rain: and it rained not on the earth by the space of three years and six months. And he prayed again, and the heaven gave rain, and the earth brought forth her fruit.

passages.g: Bringing someone back from a mistaken path rescues them and covers over a great deal of wrong.
passages.text: Brethren, if any of you do err from the truth, and one convert him; Let him know, that he which converteth the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death, and shall hide a multitude of sins.

passages.g: Before committing to a fight, a leader takes stock of the opposing force and names its most formidable members.
passages.text: Ranged thus for battle on the sacred plain — On Kurukshetra — say, Sanjaya! say What wrought my people, and the Pandavas? When he beheld the host of Pandavas, Raja Duryodhana to Drona drew, And spake these words: "Ah, Guru! see this line, How vast it is of Pandu fighting-men, Embattled by the son of Drupada, Thy scholar in the war! Therein stand ranked Chiefs like Arjuna, like to Bhima chiefs, Benders of bows; Virata, Yuyudhan, Drupada, eminent upon his car, Dhrishtaket, Chekitan, Kasi's stout lord, Purujit, Kuntibhoj, and Saivya, With Yudhamanyu, and Uttamauj Subhadra's child; and Drupadi's;-all famed! All mounted on their shining chariots! On our side, too, — thou best of Brahmans!

passages.g: Cataloguing one's own strengths does not remove the need to shore up the weakest point in the line.
passages.text: see Excellent chiefs, commanders of my line, Whose names I joy to count: thyself the first, Then Bhishma, Karna, Kripa fierce in fight, Vikarna, Aswatthaman; next to these Strong Saumadatti, with full many more Valiant and tried, ready this day to die For me their king, each with his weapon grasped, Each skilful in the field. Weakest-meseems- Our battle shows where Bhishma holds command, And Bhima, fronting him, something too strong! Have care our captains nigh to Bhishma's ranks Prepare what help they may! Now, blow my shell!"

passages.g: Ceremony and noise before a conflict serve to rouse the participants and announce that commitment is final.
passages.text: Then, at the signal of the aged king, With blare to wake the blood, rolling around Like to a lion's roar, the trumpeter Blew the great Conch; and, at the noise of it, Trumpets and drums, cymbals and gongs and horns Burst into sudden clamour; as the blasts Of loosened tempest, such the tumult seemed! Then might be seen, upon their car of gold Yoked with white steeds, blowing their battle-shells, Krishna the God, Arjuna at his side: Krishna, with knotted locks, blew his great conch Carved of the "Giant's bone;" Arjuna blew Indra's loud gift; Bhima the terrible — Wolf-bellied Bhima-blew a long reed-conch;

passages.g: A collective display of resolve is itself a weapon, unsettling opponents before any blow is struck.
passages.text: And Yudhisthira, Kunti's blameless son, Winded a mighty shell, "Victory's Voice;" And Nakula blew shrill upon his conch Named the "Sweet-sounding," Sahadev on his Called"Gem-bedecked," and Kasi's Prince on his. Sikhandi on his car, Dhrishtadyumn, Virata, Satyaki the Unsubdued, Drupada, with his sons, (O Lord of Earth!) Long-armed Subhadra's children, all blew loud, So that the clangour shook their foemen's hearts, With quaking earth and thundering heav'n.

passages.g: Before acting, a person may need to look directly at who will be harmed by the act.
passages.text: Then 'twas- Beholding Dhritirashtra's battle set, Weapons unsheathing, bows drawn forth, the war Instant to break-Arjun, whose ensign-badge Was Hanuman the monkey, spake this thing To Krishna the Divine, his charioteer: "Drive, Dauntless One! to yonder open ground Betwixt the armies; I would see more nigh These who will fight with us, those we must slay To-day, in war's arbitrament; for, sure, On bloodshed all are bent who throng this plain, Obeying Dhritirashtra's sinful son."

passages.g: Recognizing an opponent as one's own kin converts resolve into pity.
passages.text: Thus, by Arjuna prayed, (O Bharata!) Between the hosts that heavenly Charioteer Drove the bright car, reining its milk-white steeds Where Bhishma led,and Drona,and their Lords. "See!" spake he to Arjuna, "where they stand, Thy kindred of the Kurus:" and the Prince Marked on each hand the kinsmen of his house, Grandsires and sires, uncles and brothers and sons, Cousins and sons-in-law and nephews, mixed With friends and honoured elders; some this side, Some that side ranged: and, seeing those opposed, Such kith grown enemies-Arjuna's heart Melted with pity, while he uttered this:

passages.g: When the people you must destroy are your own, the body itself revolts and the prospect of victory loses all value; no power or pleasure won that way is worth having.
passages.text: Arjuna. Krishna! as I behold, come here to shed Their common blood, yon concourse of our kin, My members fail, my tongue dries in my mouth, A shudder thrills my body, and my hair Bristles with horror; from my weak hand slips Gandiv, the goodly bow; a fever burns My skin to parching; hardly may I stand; The life within me seems to swim and faint; Nothing do I foresee save woe and wail! It is not good, O Keshav! nought of good Can spring from mutual slaughter! Lo, I hate Triumph and domination, wealth and ease, Thus sadly won! Aho! what victory Can bring delight, Govinda! what rich spoils Could profit; what rule recompense; what span Of life itself seem sweet, bought with such blood?

passages.g: The people who make life worth living should not be killed even when they are trying to kill you.
passages.text: Seeing that these stand here, ready to die, For whose sake life was fair, and pleasure pleased, And power grew precious:-grandsires, sires, and sons, Brothers, and fathers-in-law, and sons-in-law, Elders and friends! Shall I deal death on these Even though they seek to slay us? Not one blow, O Madhusudan! will I strike to gain

passages.g: Killing brings guilt onto the killer, and those who can perceive the wrong are more culpable than those blinded by greed.
passages.text: The rule of all Three Worlds; then, how much less To seize an earthly kingdom! Killing these Must breed but anguish, Krishna! If they be Guilty, we shall grow guilty by their deaths; Their sins will light on us, if we shall slay Those sons of Dhritirashtra, and our kin; What peace could come of that, O Madhava? For if indeed, blinded by lust and wrath, These cannot see, or will not see, the sin Of kingly lines o'erthrown and kinsmen slain, How should not we, who see, shun such a crime — We who perceive the guilt and feel the shame — O thou Delight of Men, Janardana?

passages.g: Destroying a family destroys the customs that hold it together, and the corruption spreads to its descendants; better to be killed unresisting than to answer violence in kind.
passages.text: By overthrow of houses perisheth Their sweet continuous household piety, And-rites neglected, piety extinct — Enters impiety upon that home; Its women grow unwomaned, whence there spring Mad passions, and the mingling-up of castes, Sending a Hell-ward road that family, And whoso wrought its doom by wicked wrath. Nay, and the souls of honoured ancestors Fall from their place of peace, being bereft Of funeral-cakes and the wan death-water. So teach our holy hymns. Thus, if we slay Kinsfolk and friends for love of earthly power, Ahovat! what an evil fault it were! Better I deem it, if my kinsmen strike, To face them weaponless, and bare my breast To shaft and spear, than answer blow with blow.

passages.g: Overwhelming grief can make a person drop the very task he came to perform.
passages.text: So speaking, in the face of those two hosts, Arjuna sank upon his chariot-seat, And let fall bow and arrows, sick at heart.

passages.g: Despair that blocks you from doing what is right is a weakness to be shaken off, not indulged.
passages.text: Sanjaya. Him, filled with such compassion and such grief, With eyes tear-dimmed, despondent, in stern words The Driver, Madhusudan, thus addressed: Krishna. How hath this weakness taken thee? Whence springs The inglorious trouble, shameful to the brave, Barring the path of virtue? Nay, Arjun! Forbid thyself to feebleness! it mars Thy warrior-name! cast off the coward-fit! Wake! Be thyself! Arise, Scourge of thy Foes!

passages.g: It is better to live in poverty than to prosper by destroying those you revere, and deep doubt makes it impossible to tell which outcome would even count as winning.
passages.text: Arjuna. How can I, in the battle, shoot with shafts On Bhishma, or on Drona-O thou Chief! — Both worshipful, both honourable men? Better to live on beggar's bread With those we love alive, Than taste their blood in rich feasts spread, And guiltily survive! Ah! were it worse-who knows? — to be Victor or vanquished here, When those confront us angrily Whose death leaves living drear? In pity lost, by doubtings tossed, My thoughts-distracted-turn To Thee, the Guide I reverence most, I know not what would heal the grief Burned into soul and sense, If I were earth's unchallenged chief — A god — and these gone thence!

passages.g: The wise mourn neither the living nor the dead, because what animates a person never began and will never end; passing from one body to another is no stranger than passing from childhood to old age.
passages.text: Sanjaya. So spake Arjuna to the Lord of Hearts, And sighing,"I will not fight!" held silence then. To whom, with tender smile, (O Bharata! ) While the Prince wept despairing 'twixt those hosts, Krishna. Thou grievest where no grief should be! thou speak'st Words lacking wisdom! for the wise in heart Mourn not for those that live, nor those that die. Nor I, nor thou, nor any one of these, Ever was not, nor ever will not be, For ever and for ever afterwards. All, that doth live, lives always! To man's frame As there come infancy and youth and age, So come there raisings-up and layings-down Of other and of other life-abodes, Which the wise know, and fear not.

passages.g: Sensory experience brings alternating heat and cold, joy and sorrow, all of it brief; the steady person endures both alike. What genuinely exists cannot cease, and what does not exist never comes into being.
passages.text: This that irks — Thy sense-life, thrilling to the elements — Bringing thee heat and cold, sorrows and joys, 'Tis brief and mutable! Bear with it, Prince! As the wise bear. The soul which is not moved, The soul that with a strong and constant calm Takes sorrow and takes joy indifferently, Lives in the life undying! That which is Can never cease to be; that which is not Will not exist. To see this truth of both Is theirs who part essence from accident, Substance from shadow. Indestructible, Learn thou! the Life is, spreading life through all; It cannot anywhere, by any means, Be anywise diminished, stayed, or changed.

passages.g: Bodies perish, but the self within them is never born and never dies; whoever thinks he kills, or is killed, misunderstands what he is.
passages.text: But for these fleeting frames which it informs With spirit deathless, endless, infinite, They perish. Let them perish, Prince! and fight! He who shall say, "Lo! I have slain a man!" He who shall think, "Lo! I am slain!" those both Know naught! Life cannot slay. Life is not slain! Never the spirit was born; the spirit shall cease to be never; Never was time it was not; End and Beginning are dreams! Birthless and deathless and changeless remaineth the spirit for ever; Death hath not touched it at all, dead though the house of it seems!

passages.g: The self sheds a worn-out body and takes on a new one the way a person changes clothes, and nothing physical — weapon, fire, water, wind — can touch it.
passages.text: Who knoweth it exhaustless, self-sustained, Immortal, indestructible, — shall such Say, "I have killed a man, or caused to kill?" Nay, but as when one layeth His worn-out robes away, And taking new ones, sayeth, "These will I wear to-day!" So putteth by the spirit Lightly its garb of flesh, And passeth to inherit A residence afresh. I say to thee weapons reach not the Life; Flame burns it not, waters cannot o'erwhelm, Nor dry winds wither it. Impenetrable, Unentered, unassailed, unharmed, untouched, Immortal, all-arriving, stable, sure, Invisible, ineffable, by word And thought uncompassed, ever all itself, Thus is the Soul declared!

passages.g: Death follows birth and birth follows death, so grieving over what could not have happened otherwise is pointless; beings are unseen before birth and after death, and only the middle stretch is visible to us.
passages.text: How wilt thou, then, — Knowing it so, — grieve when thou shouldst not grieve? How, if thou hearest that the man new-dead Is, like the man new-born, still living man — One same, existent Spirit — wilt thou weep? The end of birth is death; the end of death Is birth: this is ordained! and mournest thou, Chief of the stalwart arm! for what befalls Which could not otherwise befall?
