passages.g: In a contest the point is not raw force, since people are not equally strong.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In archery it is not going through the leather which is the principal thing; — because people's strength is not equal. This was the old way.'

passages.g: Do not abolish a costly practice merely to save the cost; the practice itself is worth more than what it consumes.
passages.text: Tsze-kung wished to do away with the offering of a sheep connected with the inauguration of the first day of each month. The Master said, 'Ts'ze, you love the sheep; I love the ceremony.'

passages.g: Scrupulous correctness toward those in power is routinely mistaken by onlookers for sycophancy.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The full observance of the rules of propriety in serving one's prince is accounted by people to be flattery.'

passages.g: Those in authority should treat their subordinates with propriety, and subordinates should serve with faithfulness.
passages.text: The Duke Ting asked how a prince should employ his ministers, and how ministers should serve their prince. Confucius replied, 'A prince should employ his minister according to according to the rules of propriety; ministers should serve their prince with faithfulness.'

passages.g: The best art expresses pleasure without becoming licentious and grief without becoming destructive.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The Kwan Tsu is expressive of enjoyment without being licentious, and of grief without being hurtfully excessive.'

passages.g: What is already done cannot be undone, so it is pointless to protest, argue over, or assign blame for the past.
passages.text: The Duke Ai asked Tsai Wo about the altars of the spirits of the land. Tsai Wo replied, 'The Hsia sovereign planted the pine tree about them; the men of the Yin planted the cypress; and the men of the Chau planted the chestnut tree, meaning thereby to cause the people to be in awe.' When the Master heard it, he said, 'Things that are done, it is needless to speak about; things that have had their course, it is needless to remonstrate about; things that are past, it is needless to blame.'

passages.g: A person can be powerful and effective and still be small in character, taking the trappings of a rank he has not earned while understanding nothing of what they mean.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Small indeed was the capacity of Kwan Chung!' Some one said, 'Was Kwan Chung parsimonious?' 'Kwan,' was the reply, 'had the San Kwei, and his officers performed no double duties; how can he be considered parsimonious?' 'Then, did Kwan Chung know the rules of propriety?' The Master said, 'The princes of States have a screen intercepting the view at their gates. Kwan had likewise a screen at his gate. The princes of States on any friendly meeting between two of them, had a stand on which to place their inverted cups. Kwan had also such a stand. If Kwan knew the rules of propriety, who does not know them?'

passages.g: A fine whole begins with all its parts sounding at once, then holds them distinct yet harmonious and unbroken through to the end.
passages.text: The Master instructing the grand music-master of Lu said, 'How to play music may be known. At the commencement of the piece, all the parts should sound together. As it proceeds, they should be in harmony while severally distinct and flowing without break, and thus on to the conclusion.'

passages.g: Losing one's position may be the very means by which a person comes to instruct an entire age.
passages.text: The border warden at Yi requested to be introduced to the Master, saying, 'When men of superior virtue have come to this, I have never been denied the privilege of seeing them.' The followers of the sage introduced him, and when he came out from the interview, he said, 'My friends, why are you distressed by your master's loss of office? The kingdom has long been without the principles of truth and right; Heaven is going to use your master as a bell with its wooden tongue.'

passages.g: Something can be perfectly beautiful without being perfectly good; beauty and goodness are separate qualities.
passages.text: The Master said of the Shao that it was perfectly beautiful and also perfectly good. He said of the Wu that it was perfectly beautiful but not perfectly good.

passages.g: High office without generosity, ceremony without reverence, mourning without sorrow — such forms hollowed of their inner quality are not worth looking at.
passages.text: The Master said, 'High station filled without indulgent generosity; ceremonies performed without reverence; mourning conducted without sorrow; — wherewith should I contemplate such ways?'

passages.g: The character of the people around you is what makes a place good, and choosing where to live without weighing that is foolish.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is virtuous manners which constitute the excellence of a neighborhood. If a man in selecting a residence, do not fix on one where such prevail, how can he be wise?'

passages.g: Without inner virtue a person can endure neither prolonged hardship nor prolonged ease; the good rest in goodness, while the wise pursue it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Those who are without virtue cannot abide long either in a condition of poverty and hardship, or in a condition of enjoyment. The virtuous rest in virtue; the wise desire virtue.'

passages.g: Only a genuinely good person is capable of loving or hating others rightly.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is only the (truly) virtuous man, who can love, or who can hate, others.'

passages.g: If the will is truly set on goodness, wickedness does not get practiced.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the will be set on virtue, there will be no practice of wickedness.'

passages.g: Wealth and status are worth having only if gained rightly, and poverty worth escaping only if escaped rightly. A person of integrity does not abandon virtue even for the length of a single meal, clinging to it in haste and in danger alike.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Riches and honours are what men desire. If it cannot be obtained in the proper way, they should not be held. Poverty and meanness are what men dislike. If it cannot be avoided in the proper way, they should not be avoided. 'If a superior man abandon virtue, how can he fulfil the requirements of that name? 'The superior man does not, even for the space of a single meal, act contrary to virtue. In moments of haste, he cleaves to it. In seasons of danger, he cleaves to it.'

passages.g: No one lacks the strength to be good for a single day; the shortfall is in desire, not in capacity.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I have not seen a person who loved virtue, or one who hated what was not virtuous. He who loved virtue, would esteem nothing above it. He who hated what is not virtuous, would practise virtue in such a way that he would not allow anything that is not virtuous to approach his person. 'Is any one able for one day to apply his strength to virtue? I have not seen the case in which his strength would be insufficient. 'Should there possibly be any such case, I have not seen it.'

passages.g: People err according to the kind of person they are, so you can learn a person's character from the kind of mistakes they make.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The faults of men are characteristic of the class to which they belong. By observing a man's faults, it may be known that he is virtuous.'

passages.g: Grasping how to live rightly is worth more than however much life remains afterward.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If a man in the morning hear the right way, he may die in the evening without regret.'

passages.g: Anyone who claims to care about truth but is embarrassed by shabby clothes and plain food is not serious enough to be worth talking to.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A scholar, whose mind is set on truth, and who is ashamed of bad clothes and bad food, is not fit to be discoursed with.'

passages.g: A good person holds no fixed bias for or against anything and simply follows whatever is right in the situation.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man, in the world, does not set his mind either for anything, or against anything; what is right he will follow.'

passages.g: Admirable people are preoccupied with character and principle, lesser people with comfort and the favors they might receive.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man thinks of virtue; the small man thinks of comfort. The superior man thinks of the sanctions of law; the small man thinks of favours which he may receive.'

passages.g: Acting always with an eye to your own advantage earns widespread resentment.
passages.text: The Master said: 'He who acts with a constant view to his own advantage will be much murmured against.'

passages.g: Rules of proper conduct are useless to a ruler who lacks the underlying deference they express; with that deference, governing is easy.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If a prince is able to govern his kingdom with the complaisance proper to the rules of propriety, what difficulty will he have? If he cannot govern it with that complaisance, what has he to do with the rules of propriety?'

passages.g: Do not worry about lacking a position or recognition; worry about whether you are worthy of one.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A man should say, I am not concerned that I have no place, I am concerned how I may fit myself for one. I am not concerned that I am not known, I seek to be worthy to be known.'

passages.g: Everything worth teaching reduces to being true to your own nature and extending that same regard to others.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Shan, my doctrine is that of an all-pervading unity.' The disciple Tsang replied, 'Yes.' The Master went out, and the other disciples asked, saying, 'What do his words mean?' Tsang said, 'The doctrine of our master is to be true to the principles of our nature and the benevolent exercise of them to others, — this and nothing more.'

passages.g: The admirable person's attention rests on what is right; the petty person's on what is profitable.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The mind of the superior man is conversant with righteousness; the mind of the mean man is conversant with gain.'

passages.g: When you meet someone better than you, try to equal them; when you meet someone worse, look inward for the same fault.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When we see men of worth, we should think of equalling them; when we see men of a contrary character, we should turn inwards and examine ourselves.'

passages.g: Correct your parents gently, and if they will not listen, keep your respect and your purpose without letting resentment in.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In serving his parents, a son may remonstrate with them, but gently; when he sees that they do not incline to follow his advice, he shows an increased degree of reverence, but does not abandon his purpose; and should they punish him, he does not allow himself to murmur.'

passages.g: While your parents are alive, stay near them, and if you must go far, let them know where you are.
passages.text: The Master said, 'While his parents are alive, the son may not go abroad to a distance. If he does go abroad, he must have a fixed place to which he goes.'

passages.g: Keeping to a parent's ways long after their death is a mark of genuine devotion.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the son for three years does not alter from the way of his father, he may be called filial.'

passages.g: Always keep your parents' ages in mind, as a cause both for gladness at the years they have and for dread at how few remain.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The years of parents may by no means not be kept in the memory, as an occasion at once for joy and for fear.'

passages.g: People once spoke sparingly because they feared their actions would fail to match their words.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The reason why the ancients did not readily give utterance to their words, was that they feared lest their actions should not come up to them.'

passages.g: Be slow in speech and earnest in conduct.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man wishes to be slow in his speech and earnest in his conduct.'

passages.g: Virtue is never left isolated; whoever practices it draws others to them.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Virtue is not left to stand alone. He who practises it will have neighbors.'

passages.g: Repeated criticism brings disgrace from a superior and coldness from a friend.
passages.text: Tsze-yu said, 'In serving a prince, frequent remonstrances lead to disgrace. Between friends, frequent reproofs make the friendship distant.'

passages.g: Judge people by their actual conduct, not by whether punishment or misfortune has fallen on them.
passages.text: The Master said of Kung-ye Ch'ang that he might be wived; although he was put in bonds, he had not been guilty of any crime. Accordingly, he gave him his own daughter to wife. Of Nan Yung he said that if the country were well governed he would not be out of office, and if it were ill-governed, he would escape punishment and disgrace. He gave him the daughter of his own elder brother to wife.

passages.g: A person's excellence is evidence of the quality of the community that formed them.
passages.text: The Master said of Tsze-chien, 'Of superior virtue indeed is such a man! If there were not virtuous men in Lu, how could this man have acquired this character?'

passages.g: To be a fine and valuable instrument is real praise, but a person confined to a single use has still not become complete.
passages.text: Tsze-kung asked, 'What do you say of me, Ts'ze? The Master said, 'You are a utensil.' 'What utensil?' 'A gemmed sacrificial utensil.'

passages.g: Quickness of tongue accomplishes nothing and mostly earns dislike; it is no measure of a person's goodness.
passages.text: Some one said, 'Yung is truly virtuous, but he is not ready with his tongue.' The Master said, 'What is the good of being ready with the tongue? They who encounter men with smartnesses of speech for the most part procure themselves hatred. I know not whether he be truly virtuous, but why should he show readiness of the tongue?'

passages.g: Declining a position because you do not yet feel settled in your own grounding is itself a sign of maturity.
passages.text: The Master was wishing Ch'i-tiao K'ai to enter on official employment. He replied, 'I am not yet able to rest in the assurance of THIS.' The Master was pleased.

passages.g: Boldness without the judgment to weigh what it is spent on is not a virtue.
passages.text: The Master said, 'My doctrines make no way. I will get upon a raft, and float about on the sea. He that will accompany me will be Yu, I dare say.' Tsze-lu hearing this was glad, upon which the Master said, 'Yu is fonder of daring than I am. He does not exercise his judgment upon matters.'

passages.g: It is natural to ask not merely whether someone is capable but whether they are wholly good.
passages.text: Mang Wu asked about Tsze-lu, whether he was perfectly virtuous.

passages.g: A person may be plainly fit for a particular office without that proving anything about whether they are completely good.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I do not know.' He asked again, when the Master replied, 'In a kingdom of a thousand chariots, Yu might be employed to manage the military levies, but I do not know whether he be perfectly virtuous.' 'And what do you say of Ch'iu?' The Master replied, 'In a city of a thousand families, or a clan of a hundred chariots, Ch'iu might be employed as governor, but I do not know whether he is perfectly virtuous.' 'What do you say of Ch'ih?' The Master replied, 'With his sash girt and standing in a court, Ch'ih might be employed to converse with the visitors and guests, but I do not know whether he is perfectly virtuous.'

passages.g: Honestly acknowledging that another person surpasses you is itself a form of insight.
passages.text: The Master said to Tsze-kung, 'Which do you consider superior, yourself or Hui?' Tsze-kung replied, 'How dare I compare myself with Hui? Hui hears one point and knows all about a subject; I hear one point, and know a second.' The Master said, 'You are not equal to him. I grant you, you are not equal to him.'

passages.g: Judge people by watching what they do rather than by crediting what they say.
passages.text: Tsai Yu being asleep during the daytime, the Master said, 'Rotten wood cannot be carved; a wall of dirty earth will not receive the trowel. This Yu! — what is the use of my reproving him?' The Master said, 'At first, my way with men was to hear their words, and give them credit for their conduct. Now my way is to hear their words, and look at their conduct. It is from Yu that I have learned to make this change.'

passages.g: Someone ruled by his own desires cannot be called firm, however unyielding he appears.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I have not seen a firm and unbending man.' Some one replied, 'There is Shan Ch'ang.' 'Ch'ang,' said the Master, 'is under the influence of his passions; how can he be pronounced firm and unbending?'

passages.g: Not doing to others what you would not want done to you is far easier to state than to actually reach.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'What I do not wish men to do to me, I also wish not to do to men.' The Master said, 'Ts'ze, you have not attained to that.'

passages.g: A teacher's conduct and ordinary lessons are open to all, but their deepest views on human nature and the ultimate order of things often go unspoken.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'The Master's personal displays of his principles and ordinary descriptions of them may be heard. His discourses about man's nature, and the way of Heaven, cannot be heard.'

passages.g: Someone who takes a teaching seriously dreads hearing the next one before having put the last into practice.
passages.text: When Tsze-lu heard anything, if he had not yet succeeded in carrying it into practice, he was only afraid lest he should hear something else.

passages.g: Being quick by nature yet still eager to learn, and unashamed to learn from those beneath you, is what deserves to be called cultured.
passages.text: Tsze-kung asked, saying, 'On what ground did Kung-wan get that title of Wan?' The Master said, 'He was of an active nature and yet fond of learning, and he was not ashamed to ask and learn of his inferiors! — On these grounds he has been styled Wan.'

passages.g: Four marks of an admirable person: humility in oneself, respect toward superiors, kindness in providing for people, and justice in directing them.
passages.text: The Master said of Tsze-ch'an that he had four of the characteristics of a superior man: — in his conduct of himself, he was humble; in serving his superiors, he was respectful; in nourishing the people, he was kind; in ordering the people, he was just.'

passages.g: The test of skill in friendship is showing the same respect after long acquaintance as on the first day.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Yen P'ing knew well how to maintain friendly intercourse. The acquaintance might be long, but he showed the same respect as at first.'

passages.g: Elaborate displays of piety are no substitute for wisdom.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Tsang Wan kept a large tortoise in a house, on the capitals of the pillars of which he had hills made, and with representations of duckweed on the small pillars above the beams supporting the rafters. — Of what sort was his wisdom?'

passages.g: Serving without eagerness and leaving without bitterness is loyalty, but loyalty alone does not amount to complete goodness.
passages.text: Tsze-chang asked, saying, 'The minister Tsze- wan thrice took office, and manifested no joy in his countenance. Thrice he retired from office, and manifested no displeasure. He made it a point to inform the new minister of the way in which he had conducted the government; — what do you say of him?' The Master replied. 'He was loyal.' 'Was he perfectly virtuous?' 'I do not know. How can he be pronounced perfectly virtuous?' Tsze-chang proceeded, 'When the officer Ch'ui killed the prince of Ch'i, Ch'an Wan, though he was the owner of forty horses, abandoned them and left the country. Coming to another State, he said, "They are here like our great officer, Ch'ui," and left it.

passages.g: Abandoning wealth and country rather than live under corrupt rulers is purity, but purity alone does not amount to complete goodness.
passages.text: He came to a second State, and with the same observation left it also; — what do you say of him?' The Master replied, 'He was pure.' 'Was he perfectly virtuous?' 'I do not know. How can he be pronounced perfectly virtuous?'

passages.g: Deliberating twice is enough; further deliberation delays action without improving it.
passages.text: Chi Wan thought thrice, and then acted. When the Master was informed of it, he said, 'Twice may do.'

passages.g: Under bad government the wise conceal their intelligence and play the fool, and that concealment is harder to imitate than the wisdom itself.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When good order prevailed in his country, Ning Wu acted the part of a wise man. When his country was in disorder, he acted the part of a stupid man. Others may equal his wisdom, but they cannot equal his stupidity.'

passages.g: Talent and ambition leave a person unfinished until they learn to restrain and shape themselves.
passages.text: When the Master was in Ch'an, he said, 'Let me return! Let me return! The little children of my school are ambitious and too hasty. They are accomplished and complete so far, but they do not know how to restrict and shape themselves.'

passages.g: Those who do not hold people's past wrongs against them are met with little resentment.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Po-i and Shu-ch'i did not keep the former wickednesses of men in mind, and hence the resentments directed towards them were few.'

passages.g: Quietly borrowing in order to appear generous is not uprightness but a performance of it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Who says of Wei-shang Kao that he is upright? One begged some vinegar of him, and he begged it of a neighbor and gave it to the man.'

passages.g: Flattery, ingratiating manner, excessive deference, and feigning friendship toward someone you secretly resent are all shameful.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Fine words, an insinuating appearance, and excessive respect; — Tso Ch'iu-ming was ashamed of them. I also am ashamed of them. To conceal resentment against a person, and appear friendly with him; — Tso Ch'iu-ming was ashamed of such conduct. I also am ashamed of it.'

passages.g: People aspire at different heights: to sharing possessions without regret, to acting well without boasting of it, and highest of all to giving the old rest, friends sincerity, and the young tenderness.
passages.text: Yen Yuan and Chi Lu being by his side, the Master said to them, 'Come, let each of you tell his wishes.' Tsze-lu said, 'I should like, having chariots and horses, and light fur dresses, to share them with my friends, and though they should spoil them, I would not be displeased.' Yen Yuan said, 'I should like not to boast of my excellence, nor to make a display of my meritorious deeds.' Tsze-lu then said, 'I should like, sir, to hear your wishes.' The Master said, 'They are, in regard to the aged, to give them rest; in regard to friends, to show them sincerity; in regard to the young, to treat them tenderly.'

passages.g: Almost no one is able to recognize their own faults and hold themselves inwardly to account for them.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is all over! I have not yet seen one who could perceive his faults, and inwardly accuse himself.'

passages.g: Honesty and sincerity are common; a genuine love of learning is rare.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In a hamlet of ten families, there may be found one honourable and sincere as I am, but not so fond of learning.'

passages.g: Being easygoing about small matters is acceptable when it rests on underlying seriousness, but easygoing all the way down is negligence.
passages.text: The Master said, 'There is Yung! — He might occupy the place of a prince.' Chung-kung asked about Tsze-sang Po-tsze. The Master said, 'He may pass. He does not mind small matters.' Chung-kung said, 'If a man cherish in himself a reverential feeling of the necessity of attention to business, though he may be easy in small matters in his government of the people, that may be allowed. But if he cherish in himself that easy feeling, and also carry it out in his practice, is not such an easy mode of procedure excessive?' The Master said, 'Yung's words are right.'

passages.g: The real sign of loving to learn is never redirecting your anger onto others and never repeating a fault.
passages.text: The Duke Ai asked which of the disciples loved to learn. Confucius replied to him, 'There was Yen Hui; HE loved to learn. He did not transfer his anger; he did not repeat a fault. Unfortunately, his appointed time was short and he died; and now there is not such another. I have not yet heard of any one who loves to learn as he did.'

passages.g: Help those in genuine need rather than adding to the wealth of people who already have plenty. What is rightly yours should not be refused, since you can always pass it on to those who lack it.
passages.text: Tsze-hwa being employed on a mission to Ch'i, the disciple Zan requested grain for his mother. The Master said, 'Give her a fu.' Yen requested more. 'Give her an yu,' said the Master. Yen gave her five ping. The Master said, 'When Ch'ih was proceeding to Ch'i, he had fat horses to his carriage, and wore light furs. I have heard that a superior man helps the distressed, but does not add to the wealth of the rich.' Yuan Sze being made governor of his town by the Master, he gave him nine hundred measures of grain, but Sze declined them. The Master said, 'Do not decline them. May you not give them away in the neighborhoods, hamlets, towns, and villages?'

passages.g: A person's worth rests on their own qualities, whatever the disrepute of the family they came from.
passages.text: The Master, speaking of Chung-kung, said, 'If the calf of a brindled cow be red and horned, although men may not wish to use it, would the spirits of the mountains and rivers put it aside?'

passages.g: What distinguishes the truly good is sustaining goodness over long stretches rather than in occasional bursts.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Such was Hui that for three months there would be nothing in his mind contrary to perfect virtue. The others may attain to this on some days or in some months, but nothing more.'

passages.g: Decisiveness, intelligence, and versatility are each on their own enough to make someone fit for public office.
passages.text: Chi K'ang asked about Chung-yu, whether he was fit to be employed as an officer of government. The Master said, 'Yu is a man of decision; what difficulty would he find in being an officer of government?' K'ang asked, 'Is Ts'ze fit to be employed as an officer of government?' and was answered, 'Ts'ze is a man of intelligence; what difficulty would he find in being an officer of government?' And to the same question about Ch'iu the Master gave the same reply, saying, 'Ch'iu is a man of various ability.'

passages.g: Refusing to serve a corrupt power, and being ready to flee rather than be pressed into it, is a form of integrity.
passages.text: The chief of the Chi family sent to ask Min Tsze- ch'ien to be governor of Pi. Min Tsze-ch'ien said, 'Decline the offer for me politely. If any one come again to me with a second invitation, I shall be obliged to go and live on the banks of the Wan.'

passages.g: Devastating illness strikes the best of people; there is no justice in who it takes.
passages.text: Po-niu being ill, the Master went to ask for him. He took hold of his hand through the window, and said, 'It is killing him. It is the appointment of Heaven, alas! That such a man should have such a sickness! That such a man should have such a sickness!'

passages.g: Real virtue keeps its joy intact in poverty that others would find unbearable.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui! With a single bamboo dish of rice, a single gourd dish of drink, and living in his mean narrow lane, while others could not have endured the distress, he did not allow his joy to be affected by it. Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui!'

passages.g: Those who genuinely lack the strength collapse partway; announcing you cannot do it before starting is drawing your own limit.
passages.text: Yen Ch'iu said, 'It is not that I do not delight in your doctrines, but my strength is insufficient.' The Master said, 'Those whose strength is insufficient give over in the middle of the way but now you limit yourself.'

passages.g: Pursue learning in the manner of a person of character rather than for petty ends.
passages.text: The Master said to Tsze-hsia, 'Do you be a scholar after the style of the superior man, and not after that of the mean man.'

passages.g: A good person takes no shortcuts and approaches those in power only on legitimate business.
passages.text: Tsze-yu being governor of Wu-ch'ang, the Master said to him, 'Have you got good men there?' He answered, 'There is Tan-t'ai Mieh-ming, who never in walking takes a short cut, and never comes to my office, excepting on public business.'

passages.g: Modesty finds a way to credit circumstance rather than claim praise for its own courage.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Mang Chih-fan does not boast of his merit. Being in the rear on an occasion of flight, when they were about to enter the gate, he whipped up his horse, saying, "It is not that I dare to be last. My horse would not advance."'

passages.g: In a corrupt age, glib speech and good looks protect a person better than integrity does.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Without the specious speech of the litanist T'o and the beauty of the prince Chao of Sung, it is difficult to escape in the present age.'

passages.g: People readily follow the obvious practical path in daily life, yet ignore the equally obvious right path in how they live.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Who can go out but by the door? How is it that men will not walk according to these ways?'

passages.g: Raw substance without refinement makes a person crude, and refinement without substance makes a person a hollow functionary; character is the even blend of the two.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Where the solid qualities are in excess of accomplishments, we have rusticity; where the accomplishments are in excess of the solid qualities, we have the manners of a clerk. When the accomplishments and solid qualities are equally blended, we then have the man of virtue.'

passages.g: Human beings are made for straight dealing, and anyone who abandons it and still prospers is surviving on luck alone.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Man is born for uprightness. If a man lose his uprightness, and yet live, his escape from death is the effect of mere good fortune.'

passages.g: Knowing something is less than loving it, and loving it is less than taking delight in it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'They who know the truth are not equal to those who love it, and they who love it are not equal to those who delight in it.'

passages.g: Advanced teaching only takes hold in someone who has the capacity to receive it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'To those whose talents are above mediocrity, the highest subjects may be announced. To those who are below mediocrity, the highest subjects may not be announced.'

passages.g: Wisdom is throwing yourself into your obligations to other people while treating the supernatural with respect but distance. Excellence of character means putting the hard work first and treating success as an afterthought.
passages.text: Fan Ch'ih asked what constituted wisdom. The Master said, 'To give one's self earnestly to the duties due to men, and, while respecting spiritual beings, to keep aloof from them, may be called wisdom.' He asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'The man of virtue makes the difficulty to be overcome his first business, and success only a subsequent consideration; — this may be called perfect virtue.'

passages.g: The clever are drawn to movement and find joy in it; the good are drawn to stillness and endure.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The wise find pleasure in water; the virtuous find pleasure in hills. The wise are active; the virtuous are tranquil. The wise are joyful; the virtuous are long-lived.'

passages.g: A society can be reformed in stages, each state of affairs capable of moving one step closer to a just one.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Ch'i, by one change, would come to the State of Lu. Lu, by one change, would come to a State where true principles predominated.'

passages.g: When a thing loses the defining feature its name refers to, calling it by that name is a kind of lie.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A cornered vessel without corners. — A strange cornered vessel! A strange cornered vessel!'

passages.g: A good person can be lured into a bad situation but not into self-destruction; you can deceive them about facts, but you cannot make them abandon judgment.
passages.text: Tsai Wo asked, saying, 'A benevolent man, though it be told him, — 'There is a man in the well' will go in after him, I suppose.' Confucius said, 'Why should he do so?' A superior man may be made to go to the well, but he cannot be made to go down into it. He may be imposed upon, but he cannot be fooled.'

passages.g: Wide learning held in check by disciplined conduct keeps a person from going wrong.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man, extensively studying all learning, and keeping himself under the restraint of the rules of propriety, may thus likewise not overstep what is right.'

passages.g: When falsely suspected and unable to prove innocence, one can only stake one's integrity on it.
passages.text: The Master having visited Nan-tsze, Tsze-lu was displeased, on which the Master swore, saying, 'Wherein I have done improperly, may Heaven reject me, may Heaven reject me!'

passages.g: The virtue of moderation is the highest, and almost nobody practices it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Perfect is the virtue which is according to the Constant Mean! Rare for a long time has been its practise among the people.'

passages.g: Someone who genuinely benefits everyone has gone beyond good character into something rarer, which even the greatest rulers found hard. The method of goodness is to secure for others what you want for yourself, judging their needs from what you know of your own.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'Suppose the case of a man extensively conferring benefits on the people, and able to assist all, what would you say of him? Might he be called perfectly virtuous?' The Master said, 'Why speak only of virtue in connexion with him? Must he not have the qualities of a sage? Even Yao and Shun were still solicitous about this. 'Now the man of perfect virtue, wishing to be established himself, seeks also to establish others; wishing to be enlarged himself, he seeks also to enlarge others. 'To be able to judge of others by what is nigh in ourselves; — this may be called the art of virtue.'

passages.g: One can contribute by faithfully passing on what was inherited rather than by inventing something new.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A transmitter and not a maker, believing in and loving the ancients, I venture to compare myself with our old P'ang.'

passages.g: Absorbing knowledge quietly, learning without ever tiring of it, and teaching without growing weary are the marks of a serious mind.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The silent treasuring up of knowledge; learning without satiety; and instructing others without being wearied: — which one of these things belongs to me?'

passages.g: The things worth worrying about are failing to cultivate character, failing to think through what you learn, failing to act on what you know is right, and failing to correct your faults.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The leaving virtue without proper cultivation; the not thoroughly discussing what is learned; not being able to move towards righteousness of which a knowledge is gained; and not being able to change what is not good: — these are the things which occasion me solicitude.'

passages.g: A person of settled character is relaxed and cheerful when at ease, not merely composed under pressure.
passages.text: When the Master was unoccupied with business, his manner was easy, and he looked pleased.

passages.g: When the ideals that once filled a person's imagination stop visiting them, their powers are failing.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Extreme is my decay. For a long time, I have not dreamed, as I was wont to do, that I saw the duke of Chau.'

passages.g: Aim your will at what you ought to do, hold firmly to every gain in goodness, act in accord with your character, and take your recreation in the arts.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Let the will be set on the path of duty. 'Let every attainment in what is good be firmly grasped. 'Let perfect virtue be accorded with. 'Let relaxation and enjoyment be found in the polite arts.'

passages.g: Anyone who offers even a token of serious intent deserves to be taught.
passages.text: The Master said, 'From the man bringing his bundle of dried flesh for my teaching upwards, I have never refused instruction to any one.'

passages.g: Teaching only works on someone straining to understand; if a learner cannot extend a single example on their own, repeating it is wasted.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I do not open up the truth to one who is not eager to get knowledge, nor help out any one who is not anxious to explain himself. When I have presented one corner of a subject to any one, and he cannot from it learn the other three, I do not repeat my lesson.'

passages.g: Being near someone's grief should change your own appetites and pleasures that day.
passages.text: When the Master was eating by the side of a mourner, he never ate to the full. He did not sing on the same day in which he had been weeping.

passages.g: Reckless courage that welcomes death is worthless; the person worth working with is the one who approaches action anxiously, plans carefully, and only then acts.
passages.text: The Master said to Yen Yuan, 'When called to office, to undertake its duties; when not so called, to lie retired; — it is only I and you who have attained to this.' Tsze-lu said, 'If you had the conduct of the armies of a great State, whom would you have to act with you?' The Master said, 'I would not have him to act with me, who will unarmed attack a tiger, or cross a river without a boat, dying without any regret. My associate must be the man who proceeds to action full of solicitude, who is fond of adjusting his plans, and then carries them into execution.'

passages.g: If wealth could be had reliably, it might be worth any indignity; since it cannot, one may as well do what one loves.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If the search for riches is sure to be successful, though I should become a groom with whip in hand to get them, I will do so. As the search may not be successful, I will follow after that which I love.'

passages.g: The matters that demand the most care are the sacred, the violent, and the mortal.
passages.text: The things in reference to which the Master exercised the greatest caution were — fasting, war, and sickness.

passages.g: Beauty of a high enough order can absorb a person so completely that ordinary pleasures lose their savor.
passages.text: When the Master was in Ch'i, he heard the Shao, and for three months did not know the taste of flesh. 'I did not think'' he said, 'that music could have been made so excellent as this.'

passages.g: Those who set out to act rightly and did so have nothing to regret, whatever it cost them.
passages.text: Yen Yu said, 'Is our Master for the ruler of Wei?' Tsze-kung said, 'Oh! I will ask him.' He went in accordingly, and said, 'What sort of men were Po-i and Shu-ch'i?' 'They were ancient worthies,' said the Master. 'Did they have any repinings because of their course?' The Master again replied, 'They sought to act virtuously, and they did so; what was there for them to repine about?' On this, Tsze-kung went out and said, 'Our Master is not for him.'

passages.g: Joy is possible in poverty, and wealth obtained by wrongdoing is as insubstantial as a passing cloud.
passages.text: The Master said, 'With coarse rice to eat, with water to drink, and my bended arm for a pillow; — I have still joy in the midst of these things. Riches and honours acquired by unrighteousness, are to me as a floating cloud.'

passages.g: A lifetime is barely enough study to become free of serious error. What a person repeatedly returns to in conversation reveals what they take to be foundational.
passages.text: The Master said, 'If some years were added to my life, I would give fifty to the study of the Yi, and then I might come to be without great faults.' CHAP. XVII The Master's frequent themes of discourse were — the Odes, the History, and the maintenance of the Rules of Propriety. On all these he frequently discoursed.

passages.g: A person can be so absorbed in seeking and finding knowledge that they forget to eat, forget their griefs, and fail to notice they are growing old.
passages.text: The Duke of Sheh asked Tsze-lu about Confucius, and Tsze-lu did not answer him. The Master said, 'Why did you not say to him, — He is simply a man, who in his eager pursuit (of knowledge) forgets his food, who in the joy of its attainment forgets his sorrows, and who does not perceive that old age is coming on?'

passages.g: Understanding is not innate; it is earned by loving the past and searching it diligently.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I am not one who was born in the possession of knowledge; I am one who is fond of antiquity, and earnest in seeking it there.'

passages.g: Some subjects are better left undiscussed: marvels, displays of force, chaos, and spirits.
passages.text: The subjects on which the Master did not talk, were — extraordinary things, feats of strength, disorder, and spiritual beings.

passages.g: Anyone you spend time with can teach you, since you can copy what is good in them and take what is bad as a warning.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When I walk along with two others, they may serve me as my teachers. I will select their good qualities and follow them, their bad qualities and avoid them.'

passages.g: Someone certain of the worth of what is in them is not frightened by those who would destroy them.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Heaven produced the virtue that is in me. Hwan T'ui — what can he do to me?'

passages.g: A teacher should hide nothing, letting everything they do be visible to those learning from them.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Do you think, my disciples, that I have any concealments? I conceal nothing from you. There is nothing which I do that is not shown to you, my disciples; — that is my way.'

passages.g: A complete education covers knowledge, right conduct, wholehearted commitment, and honesty.
passages.text: There were four things which the Master taught, — letters, ethics, devotion of soul, and truthfulness.

passages.g: Perfection is not to be found, and one would settle for mere consistency of character. Consistency is impossible for anyone who pretends to possess, to be full, or to be at ease when they are not.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A sage it is not mine to see; could I see a man of real talent and virtue, that would satisfy me.' The Master said, 'A good man it is not mine to see; could I see a man possessed of constancy, that would satisfy me. 'Having not and yet affecting to have, empty and yet affecting to be full, straitened and yet affecting to be at ease: — it is difficult with such characteristics to have constancy.'

passages.g: Even when taking what you need, refuse the advantages that come from ambush and defenselessness.
passages.text: The Master angled, — but did not use a net. He shot, — but not at birds perching.

passages.g: Acting without understanding why is a failure; the next best thing to true knowledge is to take in a great deal, keep what is sound, and remember it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'There may be those who act without knowing why. I do not do so. Hearing much and selecting what is good and following it; seeing much and keeping it in memory: — this is the second style of knowledge.'

passages.g: Accept someone on the basis of the effort they are making now, without vouching for their past or their future.
passages.text: It was difficult to talk (profitably and reputably) with the people of Hu-hsiang, and a lad of that place having had an interview with the Master, the disciples doubted. The Master said, 'I admit people's approach to me without committing myself as to what they may do when they have retired. Why must one be so severe? If a man purify himself to wait upon me, I receive him so purified, without guaranteeing his past conduct.'

passages.g: Goodness is never far away; the moment you genuinely want it, it is already present.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Is virtue a thing remote? I wish to be virtuous, and lo! virtue is at hand.'

passages.g: It is good fortune to have your faults noticed by others, because otherwise they go uncorrected.
passages.text: The minister of crime of Ch'an asked whether the duke Chao knew propriety, and Confucius said, 'He knew propriety.' Confucius having retired, the minister bowed to Wu-ma Ch'i to come forward, and said, 'I have heard that the superior man is not a partisan. May the superior man be a partisan also? The prince married a daughter of the house of Wu, of the same surname with himself, and called her, — "The elder Tsze of Wu." If the prince knew propriety, who does not know it?' Wu-ma Ch'i reported these remarks, and the Master said, 'I am fortunate! If I have any errors, people are sure to know them.'

passages.g: When someone does something well, ask them to do it again and join in.
passages.text: When the Master was in company with a person who was singing, if he sang well, he would make him repeat the song, while he accompanied it with his own voice.

passages.g: Knowing what a good character requires is common; actually living out what you profess is rare.
passages.text: The Master said, 'In letters I am perhaps equal to other men, but the character of the superior man, carrying out in his conduct what he professes, is what I have not yet attained to.'

passages.g: One cannot claim to be wise or good, only to strive endlessly toward it and to teach others tirelessly.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The sage and the man of perfect virtue; — how dare I rank myself with them? It may simply be said of me, that I strive to become such without satiety, and teach others without weariness.' Kung-hsi Hwa said, 'This is just what we, the disciples, cannot imitate you in.'

passages.g: A life lived rightly is itself a continuous prayer, so there is nothing to petition for at the end.
passages.text: The Master being very sick, Tsze-lu asked leave to pray for him. He said, 'May such a thing be done?' Tsze-lu replied, 'It may. In the Eulogies it is said, "Prayer has been made for thee to the spirits of the upper and lower worlds."' The Master said, 'My praying has been for a long time.'

passages.g: Excess breeds arrogance and stinginess breeds pettiness; if you must err, err toward pettiness.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Extravagance leads to insubordination, and parsimony to meanness. It is better to be mean than to be insubordinate.'

passages.g: The person of good character is calm and at ease, while the small-minded person lives in constant anxiety.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The superior man is satisfied and composed; the mean man is always full of distress.'

passages.g: Genuine authority combines opposites: gentleness with dignity, imposing presence without harshness, deference without stiffness.
passages.text: The Master was mild, and yet dignified; majestic, and yet not fierce; respectful, and yet easy.

passages.g: The highest goodness is the sacrifice nobody knows about and therefore nobody can praise.
passages.text: The Master said, 'T'ai-po may be said to have reached the highest point of virtuous action. Thrice he declined the kingdom, and the people in ignorance of his motives could not express their approbation of his conduct.'

passages.g: Without a form to shape them, virtues curdle: respect into fussiness, caution into cowardice, courage into insolence, candor into rudeness. When those with power treat their families and old friends well, ordinary people take their moral cues from it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Respectfulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes laborious bustle; carefulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes timidity; boldness, without the rules of propriety, becomes insubordination; straightforwardness, without the rules of propriety, becomes rudeness. 'When those who are in high stations perform well all their duties to their relations, the people are aroused to virtue. When old friends are not neglected by them, the people are preserved from meanness.'

passages.g: A person who has lived cautiously, as if walking on thin ice, comes to the end intact.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang being ill, he called to him the disciples of his school, and said, 'Uncover my feet, uncover my hands. It is said in the Book of Poetry, "We should be apprehensive and cautious, as if on the brink of a deep gulf, as if treading on thin ice," and so have I been. Now and hereafter, I know my escape from all injury to my person, O ye, my little children.'

passages.g: Dying words carry weight because nothing is left to gain by falsehood. What a person in authority owes attention to is their bearing, their sincerity of expression, and their tone of speech; the technical details can be left to specialists.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang being ill, Meng Chang went to ask how he was. Tsang said to him, 'When a bird is about to die, its notes are mournful; when a man is about to die, his words are good. 'There are three principles of conduct which the man of high rank should consider specially important: — that in his deportment and manner he keep from violence and heedlessness; that in regulating his countenance he keep near to sincerity; and that in his words and tones he keep far from lowness and impropriety. As to such matters as attending to the sacrificial vessels, there are the proper officers for them.'

passages.g: Real excellence means questioning those less able than yourself, holding much while behaving as if you held nothing, and letting offenses pass without argument.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Gifted with ability, and yet putting questions to those who were not so; possessed of much, and yet putting questions to those possessed of little; having, as though he had not; full, and yet counting himself as empty; offended against, and yet entering into no altercation; formerly I had a friend who pursued this style of conduct.'

passages.g: The mark of a great person is that no crisis, however severe, can move them off their principles.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Suppose that there is an individual who can be entrusted with the charge of a young orphan prince, and can be commissioned with authority over a state of a hundred li, and whom no emergency however great can drive from his principles: — is such a man a superior man? He is a superior man indeed.'

passages.g: Anyone who takes moral responsibility as their work needs endurance, since the burden is heavy and the road ends only at death.
passages.text: The philosopher Tsang said, 'The officer may not be without breadth of mind and vigorous endurance. His burden is heavy and his course is long. 'Perfect virtue is the burden which he considers it is his to sustain; — is it not heavy? Only with death does his course stop; — is it not long?

passages.g: Poetry stirs the mind, ritual and custom give the character its shape, and music brings it to completion.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is by the Odes that the mind is aroused. 'It is by the Rules of Propriety that the character is established. 'It is from Music that the finish is received.'

passages.g: People can be brought to follow a course of action far more easily than they can be brought to understand it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The people may be made to follow a path of action, but they may not be made to understand it.'

passages.g: Recklessness paired with resentment of one's own poverty leads to revolt, and so does pushing your contempt for a bad person past the point he can bear.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The man who is fond of daring and is dissatisfied with poverty, will proceed to insubordination. So will the man who is not virtuous, when you carry your dislike of him to an extreme.'

passages.g: Remarkable talent counts for nothing if the person holding it is arrogant and stingy.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Though a man have abilities as admirable as those of the Duke of Chau, yet if he be proud and niggardly, those other things are really not worth being looked at.'

passages.g: It is rare for someone to study seriously for years and not become a better person for it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'It is not easy to find a man who has learned for three years without coming to be good.'

passages.g: Hold to what you have learned until death, and refuse to prop up a state that is collapsing or corrupt. In a well-run society your poverty is your own disgrace; in a badly run one, your wealth is.
passages.text: The Master said, 'With sincere faith he unites the love of learning; holding firm to death, he is perfecting the excellence of his course. 'Such an one will not enter a tottering State, nor dwell in a disorganized one. When right principles of government prevail in the kingdom, he will show himself; when they are prostrated, he will keep concealed. 'When a country is well-governed, poverty and a mean condition are things to be ashamed of. When a country is ill- governed, riches and honour are things to be ashamed of.'

passages.g: Do not make plans for duties that belong to an office you do not hold.
passages.text: The Master said, 'He who is not in any particular office, has nothing to do with plans for the administration of its duties.'

passages.g: When someone who has truly mastered a craft takes charge of it, the result overwhelms everyone who encounters it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When the music master Chih first entered on his office, the finish of the Kwan Tsu was magnificent; — how it filled the ears!'

passages.g: A strength without the quality that should accompany it - eagerness without honesty, slowness without care, simplicity without sincerity - leaves a person beyond understanding or help.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Ardent and yet not upright; stupid and yet not attentive; simple and yet not sincere: — such persons I do not understand.'

passages.g: Pursue learning as though you could never quite catch it, and as though what you have caught could slip away.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Learn as if you could not reach your object, and were always fearing also lest you should lose it.'

passages.g: The greatest holders of power are the ones on whom power sits lightly, as if it were nothing to them.
passages.text: The Master said, 'How majestic was the manner in which Shun and Yu held possession of the empire, as if it were nothing to them!'

passages.g: The highest human greatness is so vast that people cannot find words for it, and it shows itself in the works and institutions it leaves behind.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Great indeed was Yao as a sovereign! How majestic was he! It is only Heaven that is grand, and only Yao corresponded to it. How vast was his virtue! The people could find no name for it. 'How majestic was he in the works which he accomplished! How glorious in the elegant regulations which he instituted!'

passages.g: Capable people are genuinely scarce; even the most celebrated eras produced only a handful. The height of virtue is holding most of the power and still choosing to serve rather than seize.
passages.text: Shun had five ministers, and the empire was well-governed. King Wu said, 'I have ten able ministers.' Confucius said, 'Is not the saying that talents are difficult to find, true? Only when the dynasties of T'ang and Yu met, were they more abundant than in this of Chau, yet there was a woman among them. The able ministers were no more than nine men. 'King Wan possessed two of the three parts of the empire, and with those he served the dynasty of Yin. The virtue of the house of Chau may be said to have reached the highest point indeed.'

passages.g: A person who is austere toward himself and lavish toward his duties and his people is beyond criticism.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I can find no flaw in the character of Yu. He used himself coarse food and drink, but displayed the utmost filial piety towards the spirits. His ordinary garments were poor, but he displayed the utmost elegance in his sacrificial cap and apron. He lived in a low mean house, but expended all his strength on the ditches and water-channels. I can find nothing like a flaw in Yu.'

passages.g: Some subjects - profit, fate, and perfected virtue - are best spoken of rarely.
passages.text: The subjects of which the Master seldom spoke were — profitableness, and also the appointments of Heaven, and perfect virtue.

passages.g: Broad learning that never narrows into a single specialty invites the charge of amounting to nothing, and the charge is not worth answering.
passages.text: A man of the village of Ta-hsiang said, 'Great indeed is the philosopher K'ung! His learning is extensive, and yet he does not render his name famous by any particular thing.' The Master heard the observation, and said to his disciples, 'What shall I practise? Shall I practise charioteering, or shall I practise archery? I will practise charioteering.'

passages.g: Follow a change in custom when it costs nothing or saves something, but hold to the old practice when the new one quietly abandons respect.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The linen cap is that prescribed by the rules of ceremony, but now a silk one is worn. It is economical, and I follow the common practice. 'The rules of ceremony prescribe the bowing below the hall, but now the practice is to bow only after ascending it. That is arrogant. I continue to bow below the hall, though I oppose the common practice.'

passages.g: The best mind arrives at nothing in advance: no foregone conclusions, no fixed predeterminations, no obstinacy, no ego.
passages.text: There were four things from which the Master was entirely free. He had no foregone conclusions, no arbitrary predeterminations, no obstinacy, and no egoism.

passages.g: If what you carry is genuinely worth carrying, no threat against you can put an end to it.
passages.text: The Master was put in fear in K'wang. He said, 'After the death of King Wan, was not the cause of truth lodged here in me? 'If Heaven had wished to let this cause of truth perish, then I, a future mortal, should not have got such a relation to that cause. While Heaven does not let the cause of truth perish, what can the people of K'wang do to me?'

passages.g: Versatility usually comes from having been poor and needing many skills, not from superiority, and it is not what makes a person great.
passages.text: A high officer asked Tsze-kung, saying, 'May we not say that your Master is a sage? How various is his ability!' Tsze-kung said, 'Certainly Heaven has endowed him unlimitedly. He is about a sage. And, moreover, his ability is various.' The Master heard of the conversation and said, 'Does the high officer know me? When I was young, my condition was low, and therefore I acquired my ability in many things, but they were mean matters. Must the superior man have such variety of ability? He does not need variety of ability.' Lao said, 'The Master said, "Having no official employment, I acquired many arts."'

passages.g: Real knowledge is not a store of answers but a willingness to take any question, even from the most ignorant asker, and work it through from one end to the other.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Am I indeed possessed of knowledge? I am not knowing. But if a mean person, who appears quite empty-like, ask anything of me, I set it forth from one end to the other, and exhaust it.'

passages.g: When the signs that a better age is coming never arrive, a life's work may simply go unfulfilled.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The FANG bird does not come; the river sends forth no map: — it is all over with me!'

passages.g: Show immediate respect to the grieving, the ceremonially dignified, and the blind, regardless of whether they rank below you.
passages.text: When the Master saw a person in a mourning dress, or any one with the cap and upper and lower garments of full dress, or a blind person, on observing them approaching, though they were younger than himself, he would rise up, and if he had to pass by them, he would do so hastily.

passages.g: A deep body of teaching recedes as you approach it: it seems higher the more you look up, harder the more you press in, and it pulls you forward so strongly that you cannot stop even when you can never fully grasp it.
passages.text: Yen Yuan, in admiration of the Master's doctrines, sighed and said, 'I looked up to them, and they seemed to become more high; I tried to penetrate them, and they seemed to become more firm; I looked at them before me, and suddenly they seemed to be behind. 'The Master, by orderly method, skilfully leads men on. He enlarged my mind with learning, and taught me the restraints of propriety. 'When I wish to give over the study of his doctrines, I cannot do so, and having exerted all my ability, there seems something to stand right up before me; but though I wish to follow and lay hold of it, I really find no way to do so.'

passages.g: Do not stage a dignity you do not possess; better to die among people who actually care for you than surrounded by borrowed ceremony.
passages.text: The Master being very ill, Tsze-lu wished the disciples to act as ministers to him. During a remission of his illness, he said, 'Long has the conduct of Yu been deceitful! By pretending to have ministers when I have them not, whom should I impose upon? Should I impose upon Heaven? 'Moreover, than that I should die in the hands of ministers, is it not better that I should die in the hands of you, my disciples? And though I may not get a great burial, shall I die upon the road?'

passages.g: A talent should be sold rather than hoarded, but you wait for someone to offer its price instead of hawking it.
passages.text: Tsze-kung said, 'There is a beautiful gem here. Should I lay it up in a case and keep it? or should I seek for a good price and sell it?' The Master said, 'Sell it! Sell it! But I would wait for one to offer the price.'

passages.g: No place is barbarous once a person of real character lives in it.
passages.text: The Master was wishing to go and live among the nine wild tribes of the east. Some one said, 'They are rude. How can you do such a thing?' The Master said, 'If a superior man dwelt among them, what rudeness would there be?'

passages.g: Returning to your own ground is often what lets you finally put your work in its proper order.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I returned from Wei to Lu, and then the music was reformed, and the pieces in the Royal songs and Praise songs all found their proper places.'

passages.g: The ordinary duties - serving those above you, serving your family, honoring the dead, staying sober - are harder to fully meet than they look.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Abroad, to serve the high ministers and nobles; at home, to serve one's father and elder brothers; in all duties to the dead, not to dare not to exert one's self; and not to be overcome of wine: — which one of these things do I attain to?'

passages.g: Everything passes on like this, never stopping, day or night.
passages.text: The Master standing by a stream, said, 'It passes on just like this, not ceasing day or night!'

passages.g: People are drawn to goodness far less readily than they are drawn to beauty.
passages.text: The Master said, 'I have not seen one who loves virtue as he loves beauty.'

passages.g: Progress is entirely your own doing: stopping one basketful short of the finished mound is your stopping, and every basketful tipped onto flat ground is your advance.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The prosecution of learning may be compared to what may happen in raising a mound. If there want but one basket of earth to complete the work, and I stop, the stopping is my own work. It may be compared to throwing down the earth on the level ground. Though but one basketful is thrown at a time, the advancing with it is my own going forward.'

passages.g: The finest student is the one who never flags at anything he is taught.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Never flagging when I set forth anything to him; — ah! that is Hui.'

passages.g: What distinguishes an exceptional person is constant advance - never once seen to stop.
passages.text: The Master said of Yen Yuan, 'Alas! I saw his constant advance. I never saw him stop in his progress.'

passages.g: Some things sprout but never flower, and some flower but never fruit; a promising beginning guarantees nothing.
passages.text: The Master said, 'There are cases in which the blade springs, but the plant does not go on to flower! There are cases where it flowers, but no fruit is subsequently produced!'

passages.g: The young deserve respect because their future may surpass your present, but someone who has made no mark by middle age no longer commands it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'A youth is to be regarded with respect. How do we know that his future will not be equal to our present? If he reach the age of forty or fifty, and has not made himself heard of, then indeed he will not be worth being regarded with respect.'

passages.g: Agreeing with hard advice is worthless unless you change your conduct, and enjoying gentle advice is worthless unless you work out what it was actually asking of you.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Can men refuse to assent to the words of strict admonition? But it is reforming the conduct because of them which is valuable. Can men refuse to be pleased with words of gentle advice? But it is unfolding their aim which is valuable. If a man be pleased with these words, but does not unfold their aim, and assents to those, but does not reform his conduct, I can really do nothing with him.'

passages.g: Put faithfulness and sincerity first, keep company only with those who are at least your equal, and never be afraid to abandon a fault once you see it.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles. Have no friends not equal to yourself. When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them.'

passages.g: The general of a great army can be captured, but no one can take the will out of an ordinary person.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The commander of the forces of a large state may be carried off, but the will of even a common man cannot be taken from him.'

passages.g: Standing unashamed in rags among people dressed in furs is admirable, but freedom from envy and greed is still not the whole of excellence.
passages.text: The Master said, 'Dressed himself in a tattered robe quilted with hemp, yet standing by the side of men dressed in furs, and not ashamed; — ah! it is Yu who is equal to this! '"He dislikes none, he covets nothing; — what can he do but what is good!"' Tsze-lu kept continually repeating these words of the ode, when the Master said, 'Those things are by no means sufficient to constitute (perfect) excellence.'

passages.g: Only when conditions turn harsh do you find out which characters hold their ground.
passages.text: The Master said, 'When the year becomes cold, then we know how the pine and the cypress are the last to lose their leaves.'

passages.g: Wisdom frees a person from confusion, goodness from anxiety, and courage from fear.
passages.text: The Master said, 'The wise are free from perplexities; the virtuous from anxiety; and the bold from fear.'

passages.g: Some people can study alongside you but cannot follow you to principles; some reach principles but cannot stand firm in them; some stand firm but still cannot weigh a real situation with you.
passages.text: The Master said, 'There are some with whom we may study in common, but we shall find them unable to go along with us to principles. Perhaps we may go on with them to principles, but we shall find them unable to get established in those along with us. Or if we may get so established along with them, we shall find them unable to weigh occurring events along with us.'

passages.g: When you say someone is too far away to reach, the real distance is that you have not truly been thinking of them.
passages.text: How the flowers of the aspen-plum flutter and turn! Do I not think of you? But your house is distant. The Master said, 'It is the want of thought about it. How is it distant?'

passages.g: Manner should shift with setting: plain and almost inarticulate among your own people, exact and careful in formal or public places, and pitched differently for each person you address.
passages.text: Confucius, in his village, looked simple and sincere, and as if he were not able to speak. When he was in the prince's ancestorial temple, or in the court, he spoke minutely on every point, but cautiously. CHAP II. When he was waiting at court, in speaking with the great officers of the lower grade, he spake freely, but in a straightforward manner; in speaking with those of the higher grade, he did so blandly, but precisely. When the ruler was present, his manner displayed respectful uneasiness; it was grave, but self-possessed.

passages.g: A duty performed on someone else's behalf is done with visible care, exact attention to those around you, and a report back when it is finished.
passages.text: When the prince called him to employ him in the reception of a visitor, his countenance appeared to change, and his legs to move forward with difficulty. He inclined himself to the other officers among whom he stood, moving his left or right arm, as their position required, but keeping the skirts of his robe before and behind evenly adjusted. He hastened forward, with his arms like the wings of a bird. When the guest had retired, he would report to the prince, 'The visitor is not turning round any more.'

passages.g: Bearing carries meaning: entering a place of authority, one contracts and holds back, and only relaxes once the audience is over.
passages.text: When he entered the palace gate, he seemed to bend his body, as if it were not sufficient to admit him. When he was standing, he did not occupy the middle of the gate-way; when he passed in or out, he did not tread upon the threshold. When he was passing the vacant place of the prince, his countenance appeared to change, and his legs to bend under him, and his words came as if he hardly had breath to utter them. He ascended the reception hall, holding up his robe with both his hands, and his body bent; holding in his breath also, as if he dared not breathe. When he came out from the audience, as soon as he had descended one step, he began to relax his countenance, and had a satisfied look.

passages.g: The uneasiness proper to a formal occasion does not stop when the hardest part is over.
passages.text: When he had got to the bottom of the steps, he advanced rapidly to his place, with his arms like wings, and on occupying it, his manner still showed respectful uneasiness.

passages.g: Handling what belongs to someone else with visible strain shows the weight you place on the trust, and the strain lifts once the charge is discharged.
passages.text: When he was carrying the scepter of his ruler, he seemed to bend his body, as if he were not able to bear its weight. He did not hold it higher than the position of the hands in making a bow, nor lower than their position in giving anything to another. His countenance seemed to change, and look apprehensive, and he dragged his feet along as if they were held by something to the ground. In presenting the presents with which he was charged, he wore a placid appearance. At his private audience, he looked highly pleased.

passages.g: Dress is a language: colors, weights, and layers are chosen for occasion and season rather than for display.
passages.text: The superior man did not use a deep purple, or a puce colour, in the ornaments of his dress. Even in his undress, he did not wear anything of a red or reddish colour. In warm weather, he had a single garment either of coarse or fine texture, but he wore it displayed over an inner garment. Over lamb's fur he wore a garment of black; over fawn's fur one of white; and over fox's fur one of yellow. The fur robe of his undress was long, with the right sleeve short. He required his sleeping dress to be half as long again as his body. When staying at home, he used thick furs of the fox or the badger. When he put off mourning, he wore all the appendages of the girdle.

passages.g: What you wear should answer to the occasion, never wearing to a house of mourning what belongs to an ordinary day.
passages.text: His under-garment, except when it was required to be of the curtain shape, was made of silk cut narrow above and wide below. He did not wear lamb's fur or a black cap, on a visit of condolence. On the first day of the month he put on his court robes, and presented himself at court.

passages.g: Preparing for something sacred means deliberately changing your clothes, your food, and even where you sit.
passages.text: When fasting, he thought it necessary to have his clothes brightly clean and made of linen cloth. When fasting, he thought it necessary to change his food, and also to change the place where he commonly sat in the apartment.

passages.g: Attend to how food is prepared, refuse what is spoiled, ill-cooked, or out of season, and keep meat in proportion to grain. Set no arbitrary limit on wine, but never let it confuse you.
passages.text: He did not dislike to have his rice finely cleaned, nor to have his minced meat cut quite small. He did not eat rice which had been injured by heat or damp and turned sour, nor fish or flesh which was gone. He did not eat what was discoloured, or what was of a bad flavour, nor anything which was ill-cooked, or was not in season. He did not eat meat which was not cut properly, nor what was served without its proper sauce. Though there might be a large quantity of meat, he would not allow what he took to exceed the due proportion for the rice. It was only in wine that he laid down no limit for himself, but he did not allow himself to be confused by it.

passages.g: Eat sparingly and attentively, keep silent at meals and in bed, and treat even coarse rice and vegetable soup with reverence.
passages.text: He did not partake of wine and dried meat bought in the market. He was never without ginger when he ate. He did not eat much. When he had been assisting at the prince's sacrifice, he did not keep the flesh which he received overnight. The flesh of his family sacrifice he did not keep over three days. If kept over three days, people could not eat it. When eating, he did not converse. When in bed, he did not speak. Although his food might be coarse rice and vegetable soup, he would offer a little of it in sacrifice with a grave, respectful air.

passages.g: Let the elders leave before you do, and take part in your community's rites in full formal dress.
passages.text: When the villagers were drinking together, on those who carried staffs going out, he went out immediately after. When the villagers were going through their ceremonies to drive away pestilential influences, he put on his court robes and stood on the eastern steps.

passages.g: Receive a gift with full courtesy but refuse to use what you do not understand, rather than pretending you do.
passages.text: When he was sending complimentary inquiries to any one in another State, he bowed twice as he escorted the messenger away. Chi K'ang having sent him a present of physic, he bowed and received it, saying, 'I do not know it. I dare not taste it.'

passages.g: When something is destroyed, ask first whether anyone was hurt, and not about the property.
passages.text: The stable being burned down, when he was at court, on his return he said, 'Has any man been hurt?' He did not ask about the horses.

passages.g: What comes to you from someone you serve is handled according to what it is - shared, consecrated, or kept alive - and never treated as ordinary.
passages.text: When the prince sent him a gift of cooked meat, he would adjust his mat, first taste it, and then give it away to others. When the prince sent him a gift of undressed meat, he would have it cooked, and offer it to the spirits of his ancestors. When the prince sent him a gift of a living animal, he would keep it alive. When he was in attendance on the prince and joining in the entertainment, the prince only sacrificed. He first tasted everything. When he was ill and the prince came to visit him, he had his head to the east, made his court robes be spread over him, and drew his girdle across them.

passages.g: When duty calls, set out at once rather than waiting for conveyance to be arranged.
passages.text: When the prince's order called him, without waiting for his carriage to be yoked, he went at once.

passages.g: Ask about everything, even in the place where you are expected to already know.
passages.text: When he entered the ancestral temple of the State, he asked about everything.

passages.g: Take responsibility for burying a friend who has no one else, and reserve your bow not for costly gifts but for what is sacred.
passages.text: When any of his friends died, if he had no relations who could be depended on for the necessary offices, he would say, 'I will bury him.' When a friend sent him a present, though it might be a carriage and horses, he did not bow. The only present for which he bowed was that of the flesh of sacrifice.

passages.g: At home, drop all formal deportment. But let genuine feeling show without effort: change countenance before the mourner, the blind, the ceremonially dressed, and before thunder and violent wind.
passages.text: In bed, he did not lie like a corpse. At home, he did not put on any formal deportment. When he saw any one in a mourning dress, though it might be an acquaintance, he would change countenance; when he saw any one wearing the cap of full dress, or a blind person, though he might be in his undress, he would salute them in a ceremonious manner. To any person in mourning he bowed forward to the crossbar of his carriage; he bowed in the same way to any one bearing the tables of population. When he was at an entertainment where there was an abundance of provisions set before him, he would change countenance and rise up.
