passages.g: People who are ignorant but believe themselves wise wander in circles, like the blind following the blind.
passages.text: Fools dwelling in ignorance, yet imagining themselves wise and learned, go round and round in crooked ways, like the blind led by the blind.

passages.g: Someone dazzled by wealth concludes that this world is all there is, and so remains trapped in the same cycle again and again.
passages.text: The Hereafter never rises before the thoughtless child (the ignorant), deluded by the glamour of wealth. "This world alone is, there is none other": thinking thus, he falls under my sway again and again.

passages.g: Some truths few even get to hear and fewer understand on hearing; both a capable teacher and a capable receiver are rare.
passages.text: He about whom many are not even able to hear, whom many cannot comprehend even after hearing: wonderful is the teacher, wonderful is he who can receive when taught by an able teacher.

passages.g: What is subtler than the subtle cannot be reached by argument or by repeated thinking, but only through someone who has realized it.
passages.text: When taught by a man of inferior understanding, this Atman cannot be truly known, even though frequently thought upon. There is no way (to know It) unless it is taught by another (an illumined teacher), for it is subtler than the subtle and beyond argument.

passages.g: The deepest truth is not won by reasoning but received through teaching, and a good questioner is as rare and valuable as a good teacher.
passages.text: O Dearest, this Atman cannot be attained by argument; It is truly known only when taught by another (a wise teacher). O Nachiketas, thou hast attained It. Thou art fixed in Truth. May we ever, find a questioner like thee.

passages.g: What is eternal cannot be obtained by means of what is impermanent, though impermanent means may still be the path by which one arrives.
passages.text: I know that (earthly) treasure is transitory, for the eternal can never be attained by things which are non-eternal. Hence the Nachiketa fire (sacrifice) has been performed by me with perishable things and yet I have attained the eternal.

passages.g: Wisdom is seeing everything that could be desired, understanding its full worth, and still setting it aside.
passages.text: O Nachiketas, thou hast seen the fulfillment of all desires, the basis of the universe, the endless fruit of sacrificial rites, the other shore where there is no fear, that which is praiseworthy, the great and wide support; yet, being wise, thou hast rejected all with firm resolve.

passages.g: Whoever knows the ancient self hidden in the depths of their own being is freed from being tossed between joy and sorrow.
passages.text: The wise, who by means of the highest meditation on the Self knows the Ancient One, difficult to perceive, seated in the innermost recess, hidden in the cave of the heart, dwelling in the depth of inner being, (he who knows that One) as God, is liberated from the fetters of joy and sorrow.

passages.g: Grasping the subtle self brings joy, because it is the source from which all joy comes.
passages.text: A mortal, having heard and fully grasped this, and having realized through discrimination the subtle Self, rejoices, because he has obtained that which is the source of all joy. I think the abode (of Truth) is open to Nachiketas.

passages.g: The thing worth knowing lies beyond good and evil, beyond cause and effect, and beyond time.
passages.text: Nachiketas said: That which thou seest, which is neither virtue nor vice, neither cause nor effect, neither past nor future (but beyond these), tell me That.

passages.g: The goal that all scriptures point toward and all disciplines aim at can be named in a single syllable.
passages.text: Yama replied: That goal which all the Vedas glorify, which all austerities proclaim, desiring which (people) practice Brahmacharya (a life of continence and service), that goal I tell thee briefly — it is Aum.

passages.g: That name is itself the ultimate reality, and knowing it grants whatever one truly wants.
passages.text: This Word is indeed Brahman. This Word is indeed the Supreme. He who knows this Word obtains whatever he desires.

passages.g: It is the ultimate support of everything, and whoever knows it is honored at the highest level.
passages.text: This is the best Support, This is the highest Support; he who knows this Support is glorified in the world of Brahman.

passages.g: The self is never born and never dies; it did not come from anything and is not destroyed when the body is destroyed.
passages.text: This Self is never born, nor does It die. It did not spring from anything, nor did anything spring from It. This Ancient One is unborn, eternal, everlasting. It is not slain even though the body is slain.

passages.g: Both the killer who thinks he kills and the victim who thinks he is killed are mistaken, because what is essential in a person can neither kill nor be killed.
passages.text: If the slayer thinks that he slays, or if the slain thinks that he is slain, both of these know not. For It neither slays nor is It slain.

passages.g: The self is both smaller than the smallest and larger than the largest, and it is seen only by someone whose mind and senses are quiet and who is free from craving and grief.
passages.text: The Self is subtler than the subtle, greater than the great; It dwells in the heart of each living being. He who is free from desire and free from grief, with mind and senses tranquil, beholds the glory of the Atman.

passages.g: The self is present everywhere without moving, and it encompasses both joy and its absence.
passages.text: Though sitting, It travels far; though lying, It goes everywhere. Who else save me is fit to know that God, who is (both) joyful and joyless?

passages.g: Those who recognize the vast, bodiless self living inside their perishable body do not grieve.
passages.text: The wise who know the Self, bodiless, seated within perishable bodies, great and all- pervading, grieve not.

passages.g: The self cannot be reached by scholarship, cleverness, or repeated listening; it reveals itself to the one it chooses.
passages.text: This Self cannot be attained by study of the Scriptures, nor by intellectual perception, nor by frequent hearing (of It); He whom the Self chooses, by him alone is It attained. To him the Self reveals Its true nature.

passages.g: No amount of knowledge grants realization to someone who has not stopped doing wrong, calmed their mind, and mastered their senses.
passages.text: He who has not turned away from evil conduct, whose senses are uncontrolled, who is not tranquil, whose mind is not at rest, he can never attain this Atman even by knowledge.

passages.g: Reality is so vast that even the mightiest people, and death itself, are consumed within it.
passages.text: Who then can know where is this mighty Self? He (that Self) to whom the Brahmanas and Kshatriyas are but food and death itself a condiment.

passages.g: Two dwell within the heart, one the shadow and one the light, and both partake of what a person's actions produce.
passages.text: There are two who enjoy the fruits of their good deeds in the world, having entered into the cave of the heart, seated (there) on the highest summit. The knowers of Brahman call them shadow and light. So also (they are called) by householders who perform five fire- sacrifices or three Nachiketa fire-sacrifices.

passages.g: May we come to know both the practice that carries us across and the imperishable reality that awaits those who cross beyond fear.
passages.text: May we be able to learn that Nachiketa fire-sacrifice, which is a bridge for those who perform sacrifice. May we also know the One, who is the highest imperishable Brahman for those who desire to cross over to the other shore which is beyond fear.

passages.g: A person is like a chariot: the self is the rider, the body the chariot, the intellect the driver, and the mind the reins.
passages.text: Know the Atman (Self) as the lord of the chariot, and the body as the chariot. Know also the intellect to be the driver and mind the reins.

passages.g: The senses are the horses and the objects of sense are the roads they run on; a person experiences the world only where self, body, and mind are joined.
passages.text: The senses are called the horses; the sense objects are the roads; when the Atman is united with body, senses and mind, then the wise call Him the enjoyer.

passages.g: Someone without judgment and with an unruly mind cannot control their senses, just as a driver cannot control vicious horses.
passages.text: He who is without discrimination and whose mind is always uncontrolled, his senses are unmanageable, like the vicious horses of a driver.

passages.g: Someone with clear judgment and a disciplined mind finds their senses obedient, as a driver does with well-trained horses.
passages.text: But he who is full of discrimination and whose mind is always controlled, his senses are manageable, like the good horses of a driver.

passages.g: Without discernment and self-control, a person never reaches the goal and remains caught in the cycle of striving and dying.
passages.text: He who does not possess discrimination, whose mind is uncontrolled and always impure, he does not reach that goal, but falls again into Samsara (realm of birth and death).

passages.g: With discernment and a disciplined mind, a person reaches the goal and does not fall back into the cycle.
passages.text: But he who possesses right discrimination, whose mind is under control and always pure, he reaches that goal, from which he is not born again.

passages.g: Discernment as driver and a controlled mind as reins carry a person all the way to the end of the journey.
passages.text: The man who has a discriminative intellect for the driver, and a controlled mind for the reins, reaches the end of the journey, the highest place of Vishnu (the All-pervading and Unchangeable One).

passages.g: The objects of the senses are more fundamental than the senses, the mind more than the objects, the intellect more than the mind, and the self more than the intellect.
passages.text: Beyond the senses are the objects, beyond the objects is the mind, beyond the mind is the intellect, beyond the intellect is the great Atman.

passages.g: Above the individual self lies the unmanifest, and above that the cosmic person, beyond which there is nothing; that is the final destination.
passages.text: Beyond the great Atman is the Unmanifested; beyond the Unmanifested is the Purusha (the Cosmic Soul); beyond the Purusha there is nothing. That is the end, that is the final goal.

passages.g: The self is hidden inside every being and does not announce itself; only those with keen and refined attention perceive it.
passages.text: This Atman (Self), hidden in all beings, does not shine forth; but It is seen by subtle seers through keen and subtle understanding.

passages.g: Self-mastery is layered: govern speech with the mind, the mind with reason, and reason with the deepest self.
passages.text: A wise man should control speech by mind, mind by intellect, intellect by the great Atman, and that by the Peaceful One (the Paramatman or Supreme Self).

passages.g: Wake up and seek understanding from those who have it; the path to insight is narrow and very hard to walk.
passages.text: Arise! Awake! Having reached the Great Ones (illumined Teachers), gain understanding. The path is as sharp as a razor, impassable and difficult to travel, so the wise declare.

passages.g: Recognizing the reality that has no sensory qualities and no beginning or end releases a person from death.
passages.text: Knowing That which is soundless, touchless, formless, undecaying; also tasteless, odorless, and eternal; beginningless, endless and immutable; beyond the Unmanifested: (knowing That) man escapes from the mouth of death.

passages.g: Whoever learns and retells the teaching about death and the self is honored.
passages.text: The intelligent man, who has heard and repeated the ancient story of Nachiketas, told by the Ruler of Death, is glorified in the world of Brahman.

passages.g: Reciting this secret of immortality with devotion brings lasting reward.
passages.text: He who with devotion recites this highest secret of immortality before an assembly of Brahmanas (pious men) or at the time of Shraddha (funeral ceremonies), gains everlasting reward, he gains everlasting reward.

passages.g: The senses point outward, so people perceive the world but not themselves; only someone who deliberately turns attention inward discovers their own essential nature.
passages.text: The Self-existent created the senses out-going; for this reason man sees the external, but not the inner Atman (Self). Some wise man, however, desiring immortality, with eyes turned away (from the external) sees the Atman within.

passages.g: The immature chase fleeting pleasures and are caught by death; the wise do not look for permanence among things that pass.
passages.text: Children (the ignorant) pursue external pleasures; (thus) they fall into the wide- spread snare of death. But the wise, knowing the nature of immortality, do not seek the permanent among fleeting things.

passages.g: The same awareness by which you know sight, taste, smell, sound and touch is what lets you know anything at all, and that awareness is the ultimate reality.
passages.text: That by which one knows form, taste, smell, sound, touch and sense enjoyments, by That also one knows whatever remains (to be known). This verily is That (which thou hast asked to know).

passages.g: The awareness present in both dreaming and waking is one all-pervading self, and knowing it ends grief.
passages.text: That by which a mortal perceives, both in dream and in waking, by knowing that great all-pervading Atman the wise man grieves no more.

passages.g: Whoever recognizes the self within as the experiencer and master of past and future stops being afraid.
passages.text: He who knows this Atman, the honey-eater (perceiver and enjoyer of objects), ever near, as the lord of the past and future, fears no more. This verily is That.

passages.g: The same reality concealed within the elements also dwells within the human heart.
passages.text: He who sees Him seated in the five elements, born of Tapas (fire of Brahman), born before water; who, having entered the cave of the heart, abides therein — this verily is That.

passages.g: The power that sustains life and the gods resides inside the human heart.
passages.text: He who knows Aditi, who rises with Prana (the Life Principle), existent in all the Devas; who, having entered into the heart, abides there; and who was born from the elements — this verily is That.

passages.g: The divine lies latent in ordinary things, as fire lies hidden in kindling, and must be tended daily by those who stay alert.
passages.text: The all-seeing fire which exists hidden in the two sticks, as the foetus is well-guarded in the womb by the mother, (that fire) is to be worshipped day after day by wakeful seekers (after wisdom), as well as by sacrificers. This verily is That.

passages.g: Everything, including the powers people worship, depends on one reality beyond which nothing goes.
passages.text: From whence the sun rises, and whither it goes at setting, upon That all the Devas depend. No one goes beyond That. This verily is That.

passages.g: The visible and the invisible are one and the same; whoever insists they differ passes from death to death.
passages.text: What is here (in the visible world), that is there (in the invisible); he who sees difference (between visible and invisible) goes from death to death.

passages.g: This unity can be grasped only by the mind; seeing division where there is none keeps a person bound to death.
passages.text: By mind alone this is to be realized. There is no difference whatever (between visible and invisible). He who sees difference here (between these) goes from death to death.

passages.g: The self dwelling in the body is master of past and future, and knowing it ends fear.
passages.text: The Purusha (Self), of the size of a thumb, resides in the middle of the body as the lord of the past and the future, (he who knows Him) fears no more. This verily is That.

passages.g: The self within is like a smokeless flame, the same today as tomorrow.
passages.text: That Purusha, of the size of a thumb, is like a light without smoke, lord of the past and the future. He is the same today and tomorrow. This verily is That.

passages.g: Whoever sees reality as fragmented ends up chasing the fragments in every direction.
passages.text: As rain water, (falling) on the mountain top, runs down over the rocks on all sides; similarly, he who sees difference (between visible forms) runs after them in various directions.

passages.g: One who truly knows merges with the ultimate, as pure water poured into pure water becomes indistinguishable.
passages.text: O Gautama (Nachiketas), as pure water poured into pure water becomes one, so also is it with the Self of an illumined Knower (he becomes one with the Supreme).

passages.g: The body is a city with eleven gates inhabited by an unborn, unchanging knower; contemplating it ends grief and brings freedom.
passages.text: The city of the Unborn, whose knowledge is unchanging, has eleven gates. Thinking on Him, man grieves no more; and being freed (from ignorance), he attains liberation. This verily is That.

passages.g: One reality is present everywhere at once: in the sun, the air, the fire, the guest at the door, in beings great and small, in water, earth, and mountain.
passages.text: He is the sun dwelling in the bright heaven; He is the air dwelling in space; He is the fire burning on the altar; He is the guest dwelling in the house. He dwells in man. He dwells in those greater than man. He dwells in sacrifice. He dwells in the ether. He is (all that is) born in water, (all that) is born in earth, (all that) is born in sacrifice, (all that) is born on mountains. He is the True and the Great.

passages.g: The self at the center drives the breath in and out, and every sense serves it.
passages.text: He it is who sends the (in-coming) Prana (life-breath) upward and throws the (out-going) breath downward. Him all the senses worship, the adorable Atman, seated in the centre (the heart).

passages.g: When the self departs the body, nothing remains.
passages.text: When this Atman, which is seated in the body, goes out (from the body), what remains then? This verily is That.

passages.g: We do not live by breathing; we live by something on which breathing itself depends.
passages.text: No mortal lives by the in-coming breath (Prana) or by the out-going breath (Apana), but he lives by another on which these two depend.

passages.g: There is a teaching about what is eternal and about what becomes of the self after death.
passages.text: O Gautama (Nachiketas), I shall declare unto thee the secret of the eternal Brahman and what happens to the Self after death.

passages.g: What a person becomes after death follows from what they have done and what they have understood.
passages.text: Some Jivas (individual Souls) enter wombs to be embodied; others go into immovable forms, according to their deeds and knowledge.

passages.g: The reality that stays awake while everything else sleeps is pure, deathless, and the ground on which all worlds rest.
passages.text: The Being who remains awake while all sleep, who grants all desires, That is pure, That is Brahman, That alone is said to be immortal. On That all the worlds rest. None goes beyond That. This verily is That.

passages.g: One reality takes the shape of whatever it enters, as a single fire takes the form of whatever it burns, and yet it also exists beyond all of it.
passages.text: As fire, though one, having entered the world, becomes various according to what it burns, so does the Atman (Self) within all living beings, though one, become various according to what it enters. It also exists outside.

passages.g: One reality assumes the form of everything it inhabits, as air does, while still remaining outside them.
passages.text: As air, though one, having entered the world, becomes various according to what it enters, so does the Atman within all living beings, though one, become various according to what it enters. It also exists outside.

passages.g: Just as the sun is not stained by the ugliness it illuminates, the self within all beings is untouched by the world's suffering.
passages.text: As the sun, the eye of the whole world, is not defiled by external impurities seen by the eyes, thus the one inner Self of all living beings is not defiled by the misery of the world, being outside it.

passages.g: A single reality makes itself into many, and those who find it within themselves gain lasting joy, which others never do.
passages.text: There is one ruler, the Self of all living beings, who makes the one form manifold; the wise who perceive Him seated within their Self, to them belongs eternal bliss, not to others.

passages.g: The permanent underlies the changing and awareness underlies all awareness; those who find it within themselves have lasting peace.
passages.text: Eternal among the changing, consciousness of the conscious, who, though one, fulfils the desires of many: the wise who perceive Him seated within their Self, to them belongs eternal peace, not to others.

passages.g: Those who reach the highest happiness can point to it but cannot say how it is known or whether it shines by its own light.
passages.text: They (the wise) perceive that indescribable highest bliss, saying, This is That. How am I to know It? Does It shine (by Its own light) or does It shine (by reflected light)?

passages.g: The ultimate is what makes everything else visible; sun, moon, stars and fire shine only by borrowing its light.
passages.text: The sun does not shine there, nor the moon, nor the stars; nor do these lightnings shine there, much less this fire. When He shines, everything shines after Him; by His light all is lighted.

passages.g: Reality is rooted above and branches downward, the deathless ground in which all worlds rest and beyond which nothing lies.
passages.text: This ancient Aswattha tree has its root above and branches below. That is pure, That is Brahman, That alone is called the Immortal. All the worlds rest in That. None goes beyond That. This verily is That.

passages.g: Everything in the universe arises from and moves within one vital power; it is terrifying in its magnitude, and those who know it become deathless.
passages.text: Whatever there is in the universe is evolved from Prana and vibrates in Prana. That is a mighty terror, like an upraised thunderbolt. They who know That become immortal.

passages.g: The whole order of nature runs on obedience to a single power.
passages.text: From fear of Him the fire burns, from fear of Him the sun shines. From fear of Him Indra and Vayu and Death, the fifth, speed forth.

passages.g: Failing to realize this before death means returning to embodied existence again.
passages.text: If a man is not able to know Him before the dissolution of the body, then he becomes embodied again in the created worlds.

passages.g: How clearly you perceive the ultimate depends on where you look for it: sharp as a mirror when you look within, dim as a dream or a reflection in water anywhere else.
passages.text: As in a mirror, so is He seen within oneself; as in a dream, so (is He seen) in the world of the fathers (departed spirits); as in water, so (is He seen) in the world of Gandharvas (the angelic realm). As light and shadow, so (is He seen) in the world of Brahma (the Creator).

passages.g: The senses arise and pass away separately from the self, and knowing this ends grief.
passages.text: Knowing that the senses are distinct (from the Atman) and their rising and setting separate (from the Atman), a wise man grieves no more.

passages.g: Reality is layered: above the senses is the mind, above the mind reason, above reason the great self, and above that the unmanifest.
passages.text: Higher than the senses is the mind, higher than the mind is the intellect, higher than the intellect is the great Atman, higher than the Atman is the Unmanifested.

passages.g: Beyond even the unmanifest is a pervading, imperceptible being, and knowing it frees a person from death.
passages.text: Beyond the Unmanifested is the all-pervading and imperceptible Being (Purusha). By knowing Him, the mortal is liberated and attains immortality.

passages.g: The ultimate cannot be seen with the eye but is apprehended by heart, intellect, and mind, and those who apprehend it become deathless.
passages.text: His form is not to be seen. No one can see Him with the eye. He is perceived by the heart, by the intellect and by the mind. They who know this become immortal.

passages.g: The highest state is reached when the five senses and the mind grow still and the intellect stops acting.
passages.text: When the five organs of perception become still, together with the mind, and the intellect ceases to be active: that is called the highest state.

passages.g: Yoga is the steady restraint of the senses, and it demands vigilance because the state it produces comes and goes.
passages.text: This firm holding back of the senses is what is known as Yoga. Then one should become watchful, for Yoga comes and goes.

passages.g: The ultimate cannot be reached by speech, thought, or sight; it is realized only by one who simply affirms that it exists.
passages.text: He cannot be attained by speech, by mind, or by the eye. How can That be realized except by him who says "He is"?

passages.g: Reality must first be affirmed as existing before its true nature can reveal itself.
passages.text: He should be realized as "He is" and also as the reality of both (visible and invisible). He who knows Him as "He is," to him alone His real nature is revealed.

passages.g: When every desire lodged in the heart falls away, a mortal becomes deathless here and now.
passages.text: When all desires dwelling in the heart cease, then the mortal becomes immortal and attains Brahman here.

passages.g: When all the heart's attachments are cut, a mortal becomes deathless.
passages.text: When all the ties of the heart are cut asunder here, then the mortal becomes immortal. Such is the teaching.

passages.g: Of the many channels leading out from the heart, only the one running up through the head leads to deathlessness; the rest lead elsewhere.
passages.text: There are a hundred and one nerves of the heart. One of them penetrates the centre of the head. Going upward through it, one attains immortality. The other (hundred nerve-courses) lead, in departing, to different worlds.

passages.g: A deathless self sits in the heart of every living being, and with persistence it can be drawn out from the body as a stalk is drawn from its sheath.
passages.text: The Purusha, the inner Self, of the size of a thumb, is ever seated in the heart of all living beings. With perseverance man should draw Him out from his body as one draws the inner stalk from a blade of grass. One should know Him as pure and deathless, as pure and deathless.

passages.g: Anyone who comes to understand the nature of the self becomes free of impurity and death.
passages.text: Thus Nachiketas, having acquired this wisdom taught by the Ruler of Death, together with all the rules of Yoga, became free from impurity and death and attained Brahman (the Supreme). So also will it be with another who likewise knows the nature of the Self.

passages.g: What is it that directs the mind, the breath, speech, the eye, and the ear toward their objects?
passages.text: By whom commanded and directed does the mind go towards its objects? Commanded by whom does the life-force, the first (cause), move? At whose will do men utter speech? What power directs the eye and the ear?

passages.g: Behind ear, mind, speech, life, and eye stands that which enables each of them; those who realize it and let go of desire become deathless.
passages.text: It is the ear of the ear, the mind of the mind, the speech of the speech, the life of the life, the eye of the eye. The wise, freed (from the senses and from mortal desires), after leaving this world, become immortal.

passages.g: The ultimate cannot be reached by sight, speech, or mind; it is other than what we know and also other than what we do not know.
passages.text: There the eye does not go, nor speech, nor mind. We do not know That; we do not understand how It can be taught. It is distinct from the known and also It is beyond the unknown. Thus we have heard from the ancient (teachers) who told us about It.

passages.g: What enables speech but is never itself spoken is the ultimate, not the objects people worship.
passages.text: That which speech does not illumine, but which illumines speech: know that alone to be the Brahman (the Supreme Being), not this which people worship here.

passages.g: What enables thought but cannot itself be thought is the ultimate, not the objects people worship.
passages.text: That which cannot be thought by mind, but by which, they say, mind is able to think: know that alone to be the Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: What enables sight but cannot itself be seen is the ultimate, not the objects people worship.
passages.text: That which is not seen by the eye, but by which the eye is able to see: know that alone to be the Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: What enables hearing but cannot itself be heard is the ultimate, not the objects people worship.
passages.text: That which cannot be heard by the ear, but by which the ear is able to hear: know that alone to be Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: The ultimate reality is the power that makes breathing happen rather than any object people can point to and worship.
passages.text: That which none breathes with the breath, but by which breath is in-breathed: know that alone to be the Brahman, not this which people worship here.

passages.g: Anyone confident they fully understand ultimate reality understands very little of it, and what you take yourself to already know is exactly what still needs seeking.
passages.text: If thou thinkest "I know It well," then it is certain that thou knowest but little of the Brahman (Absolute Truth), or in what form He (resideth) in the Devas (minor aspects of Deity). Therefore I think that what thou thinkest to be known is still to be sought after.

passages.g: Genuine understanding is neither the claim to know nor the claim not to know; whoever truly understands grasps what both claims actually mean.
passages.text: The disciple said: I do not think I know It well, nor do I think that I do not know It. He among us who knows It truly, knows (what is meant by) "I know" and also what is meant by "I know It not."

passages.g: Those who think they do not know it are the ones who know it, and those who think they know it do not, because the wise recognize its infinitude while the ignorant mistake their grasp for the thing.
passages.text: He who thinks he knows It not, knows It. He who thinks he knows It, knows It not. The true knowers think they can never know It (because of Its infinitude), while the ignorant think they know It.

passages.g: Ultimate reality is known only when it is recognized in every state of consciousness, and that self-knowledge is itself the source of strength and deathlessness.
passages.text: It (Brahman) is known, when It is known in every state of consciousness. (Through such knowledge) one attains immortality. By attaining this Self, man gains strength; and by Self-knowledge immortality is attained.

passages.g: To realize this truth in the present life is everything and to miss it is total loss, because seeing the same self in all beings is what liberates a person.
passages.text: If one knows It here, that is Truth; if one knows It not here, then great is his loss. The wise seeing the same Self in all beings, being liberated from this world, become immortal.

passages.g: Success that actually comes from a greater power is easily mistaken for one's own achievement, and that mistake breeds pride.
passages.text: The Brahman once won a victory for the Devas. Through that victory of the Brahman, the Devas became elated. They thought, "This victory is ours. This glory is ours."

passages.g: The power responsible for our success can stand directly in front of us and go unrecognized.
passages.text: The Brahman perceived this and appeared before them. They did not know what mysterious form it was.

passages.g: When something genuinely unknown appears, those reputed to know everything are the first sent to explain it.
passages.text: They said to Fire: "O Jataveda (All-knowing)! Find out what mysterious spirit this is." He said: "Yes."

passages.g: Confronted by what exceeds us and asked what we are, we answer by reciting our names and titles.
passages.text: He ran towards it and He (Brahman) said to him: "Who art thou?" "I am Agni, I am Jataveda," he (the Fire-god) replied.

passages.g: Asked what we are actually capable of, we describe our powers in their most absolute and unlimited form.
passages.text: Brahman asked: "What power resides in thee?" Agni replied: "I can burn up all whatsoever exists on earth."

passages.g: Boasted powers collapse at the simplest test, and the boaster comes away still ignorant of what he was facing.
passages.text: Brahman placed a straw before him and said: "Burn this." He (Agni) rushed towards it with all speed, but was not able to burn it. So he returned from there and said (to the Devas): "I was not able to find out what this great mystery is."

passages.g: When the first authority fails, the task of explaining the unknown passes to the next one.
passages.text: Then they said to Vayu (the Air-god): "Vayu! Find out what this mystery is." He said: "Yes."

passages.g: Approached by what exceeds us, we again identify ourselves by name and reputation.
passages.text: He ran towards it and He (Brahman) said to him: "Who art thou?" "I am Vayu, I am Matarisva (traveller of Heaven)," he (Vayu) said.

passages.g: Asked what we can do, we again claim power over everything that exists.
passages.text: Then the Brahman said: "What power is in thee?" Vayu replied: "I can blow away all whatsoever exists on earth."

passages.g: The second boast fails at the same trivial obstacle, and its owner returns as ignorant as the first.
passages.text: Brahman placed a straw before him and said: "Blow this away." He (Vayu) rushed towards it with all speed, but was not able to blow it away. So he returned from there and said (to the Devas): "I was not able to find out what this great mystery is."

passages.g: When the mystery is charged at directly, it simply disappears.
passages.text: Then they said to Indra: "O Maghavan (Worshipful One)! Find out what this mystery is." He said: "Yes"; and ran towards it, but it disappeared before him.

passages.g: Understanding arrives not through force but in the empty space the mystery leaves behind, and through something encountered there.
passages.text: Then he saw in that very space a woman beautifully adorned, Uma of golden hue, daughter of Haimavat (Himalaya). He asked: "What is this great mystery?"

passages.g: Our victories are never our own; they belong to the reality working through us, and recognizing that is the whole of the knowledge.
passages.text: She (Uma) said: "It is Brahman. It is through the victory of Brahman that ye are victorious." Then from her words, he (Indra) knew that it (that mysterious form) was Brahman.

passages.g: Those who come closest to ultimate reality, and who recognize it first, surpass everyone else.
passages.text: Therefore these Devas, — Agni, Vayu and Indra — excel other Devas, because they came nearer to Brahman. It was they who first knew this spirit as Brahman.

passages.g: Whoever comes nearest and knows it first stands above all the rest.
passages.text: Therefore Indra excels all other Devas, because he came nearest to Brahman, and because he first (before all others) knew this spirit as Brahman.

passages.g: Insight into ultimate reality arrives as a sudden flash, present and gone in the time it takes to blink.
passages.text: Thus the teaching of Brahman is here illustrated in regard to the Devas. He dashed like lightning, and appeared and disappeared just as the eye winks.

passages.g: Within a person, the approach to ultimate reality is made by the mind, through repeated remembering and contemplation.
passages.text: Next (the teaching) is regarding Adhyatman (the embodied Soul). The mind seems to approach Him (Brahman). By this mind (the seeker) again and again remembers and thinks about Brahman.

passages.g: Ultimate reality is what all things ultimately long for, and whoever knows it becomes beloved by every being.
passages.text: That Brahman is called Tadvanam (object of adoration). He is to be worshipped by the name Tadvanam. He who knows Brahman thus, is loved by all beings.

passages.g: The teaching has already been given in full; asking again for it misses that nothing remains to add.
passages.text: The disciple asked: O Master, teach me the Upanishad. (The teacher replied:) The Upanishad has been taught thee. We have certainly taught thee the Upanishad about Brahman.

passages.g: This wisdom rests on self-discipline, mastery of the senses, and right action, and truth is what supports the whole structure.
passages.text: The Upanishad is based on tapas (practice of the control of body, mind and senses), dama (subjugation of the senses), karma (right performance of prescribed actions). The Vedas are its limbs. Truth is its support.

passages.g: Whoever understands this is freed of all wrongdoing and comes to rest in the highest and most blissful state.
passages.text: He who knows this (wisdom of the Upanishad), having been cleansed of all sin, becomes established in the blissful, eternal and highest abode of Brahman, in the highest abode of Brahman.

passages.g: Philosophy is never premature and never too late, because caring for the health of one's soul has no proper age; the young gain freedom from fear of the future, and the old gain the pleasure of remembering a life well spent.
passages.text: Let no one delay to study philosophy while he is young, and when he is old let him not become weary of the study; for no man can ever find the time unsuitable or too late to study the health of his soul. And he who asserts either that it is not yet time to philosophize, or that the hour is passed, is like a man who should say that the time is not yet come to be happy, or that it is too late. So that both young and old should study philosophy, the one in order that, when he is old, he may be young in good things through the pleasing recollection of the past, and the other in order that he may be at the same time both young and old, in consequence of his absence of fear for the future.

passages.g: Since happiness is what all our striving aims at, it is worth studying what actually produces it, beginning with a conception of the divine as untroubled and imperishable and free of anything inconsistent with that.
passages.text: It is right then for a man to consider the things which produce happiness, since, if happiness is present, we have everything, and when it is absent, we do everything with a view to possess it. Now, what I have constantly recommended to you, these things I would have you do and practise, considering them to be the elements of living well. First of all, believe that God is a being incorruptible and happy, as the common opinion of the world about God dictates; and attach to your idea of him nothing which is inconsistent with incorruptibility or with happiness; and think that he is invested with everything which is able to preserve to him this happiness, in conjunction with incorruptibility.

passages.g: The divine exists but is not what popular opinion makes it, and real impiety is not rejecting the crowd's gods but attributing the crowd's opinions to them.
passages.text: For there are Gods; for our knowledge of them is indistinct. But they are not of the character which people in general attribute to them; for they do not pay a respect to them which accords with the ideas that they entertain of them. And that man is not impious who discards the Gods believed in by the many, but he who applies to the Gods the opinions entertained of them by the many. For the assertions of the many about the Gods are not anticipations (προλήψεις), but false opinions (ὑπολήψεις). And in consequence of these, the greatest evils which befall wicked men, and the benefits which are conferred on the good, are all attributed to the Gods;

passages.g: People imagine the divine by analogy with human virtues, and dismiss as incompatible with divinity anything unlike human qualities.
passages.text: for they connect all their ideas of them with a comparison of human virtues, and everything which is different from human qualities, they regard as incompatible with the divine nature.

passages.g: Death is nothing to us because all good and evil lie in sensation and death is simply the end of sensation, and grasping this makes a mortal life pleasant by removing the craving for endless time.
passages.text: Accustom yourself also to think death a matter with which we are not at all concerned, since all good and all evil is in sensation, and since death is only the privation of sensation. On which account, the correct knowledge of the fact that death is no concern of ours, makes the mortality of life pleasant to us, inasmuch as it sets forth no illimitable time, but relieves us for the longing for immortality. For there is nothing terrible in living to a man who rightly comprehends that there is nothing terrible in ceasing to live; so that he was a silly man who said that he feared death, not because it would grieve him when it was present, but because it did grieve him while it was future.

passages.g: What causes no distress when present cannot rationally be feared when merely anticipated, and death never coexists with us at all, so it concerns neither the living nor the dead.
passages.text: For it is very absurd that that which does not distress a man when it is present, should afflict him when only expected. Therefore, the most formidable of all evils, death, is nothing to us, since, when we exist, death is not present to us; and when death is present, then we have no existence. It is no concern then either of the living or of the dead; since to the one it has no existence, and the other class has no existence itself. But people in general, at times flee from death as the greatest of evils, and at times wish for it as a rest from the evils in life. Nor is the not living a thing feared, since living is not connected with it: nor does the wise man think not living an evil;

passages.g: The wise value life the way they value food, by quality rather than quantity, and learning to live well is the same task as learning to die well.
passages.text: but, just as he chooses food, not preferring that which is most abundant, but that which is nicest; so too, he enjoys time, not measuring it as to whether it is of the greatest length, but as to whether it is most agreeable. And he who enjoins a young man to live well, and an old man to die well, is a simpleton, not only because of the constantly delightful nature of life, but also because the care to live well is identical with the care to die well. And he was still more wrong who said:—

passages.g: Some claim it is best never to be born, and having been born, to leave life as quickly as possible.
passages.text: ’Tis well to taste of life, and then when born To pass with quickness to the shades below.

passages.g: Anyone who sincerely believed life not worth living would simply leave it, so such a claim is either insincere or a joke made where joking is out of place. The future is neither wholly ours nor wholly beyond us, and our desires divide into natural and empty ones, with the natural ones dividing further into necessary and merely natural.
passages.text: For if this really was his opinion why did he not quit life? for it was easily in his power to do so, if it really was his belief. But if he was joking, then he was talking foolishly in a case where it ought not to be allowed; and, we must recollect, that the future is not our own, nor, on the other hand, is it wholly not our own, I mean so that we can never altogether await it with a feeling of certainty that it will be, nor altogether despair of it as what will never be. And we must consider that some of the passions are natural, and some empty; and of the natural ones some are necessary, and some merely natural.

passages.g: The necessary desires serve happiness, bodily ease, or life itself, and a correct understanding of them refers every choice and avoidance to the body's health and the mind's freedom from disturbance, which is the whole aim of living.
passages.text: And of the necessary ones some are necessary to happiness, and others, with regard to the exemption of the body, from trouble; and others with respect to living itself; for a correct theory, with regard to these things, can refer all choice and avoidance to the health of the body and the freedom from disquietude of the soul. Since this is the end of living happily; for it is for the sake of this that we do everything, wishing to avoid grief and fear; and when once this is the case, with respect to us, then the storm of the soul is, as I may say, put an end to;

passages.g: Once that aim is reached there is nothing left to reach for, since nothing further could perfect body and mind.
passages.text: since the animal is unable to go as if to something deficient, and to seek something different from that by which the good of the soul and body will be perfected.

passages.g: We need pleasure only when the lack of it pains us, and though pleasure is the origin and the goal of a happy life, we deliberately pass over pleasures that bring greater trouble in their wake.
passages.text: For then we have need of pleasure when we grieve, because pleasure is not present; but when we do not grieve, then we have no need of pleasure; and on this account, we affirm, that pleasure is the beginning and end of living happily; for we have recognized this as the first good, being connate with us; and with reference to it, it is that we begin every choice and avoidance; and to this we come as if we judged of all good by passion as the standard; and, since this is the first good and connate with us, on this account we do not choose every pleasure, but at times we pass over many pleasures when any difficulty is likely to ensue from them;

passages.g: We accept pain willingly when enduring it for a time yields a greater pleasure afterward.
passages.text: and we think many pains better than pleasures, when a greater pleasure follows them, if we endure the pain for a time.

passages.g: Every pleasure is good by nature and every pain evil, but not every pleasure is worth choosing nor every pain worth avoiding, and the right measure is a comparison of what each brings with it. Contentment matters not so that we may always live on little, but so that we can live well on little when little is all we have, since those who need luxury least enjoy it most.
passages.text: Every pleasure is therefore a good on account of its own nature, but it does not follow that every pleasure is worthy of being chosen; just as every pain is an evil, and yet every pain must not be avoided. But it is right to estimate all these things by the measurement and view of what is suitable and unsuitable; for at times we may feel the good as an evil, and at times, on the contrary, we may feel the evil as good. And, we think, contentment a great good, not in order that we may never have but a little, but in order that, if we have not much, we may make use of a little, being genuinely persuaded that those men enjoy luxury most completely who are the best able to do without it;

passages.g: What is natural is easy to obtain and what is useless is hard, and simple inexpensive habits produce health, freedom from anxiety, and readiness to meet whatever fortune brings.
passages.text: and that everything which is natural is easily provided, and what is useless is not easily procured. And simple flavours give as much pleasure as costly fare, when everything that can give pain, and every feeling of want, is removed; and corn and water give the most extreme pleasure when any one in need eats them. To accustom one’s self, therefore, to simple and inexpensive habits is a great ingredient in the perfecting of health, and makes a man free from hesitation with respect to the necessary uses of life. And when we, on certain occasions, fall in with more sumptuous fare, it makes us in a better disposition towards it, and renders us fearless with respect to fortune.

passages.g: Calling pleasure the highest good means freedom of the body from pain and of the mind from confusion, not the indulgence of appetite.
passages.text: When, therefore, we say that pleasure is a chief good, we are not speaking of the pleasures of the debauched man, or those which lie in sensual enjoyment, as some think who are ignorant, and who do not entertain our opinions, or else interpret them perversely; but we mean the freedom of the body from pain, and of the soul from confusion.

passages.g: A pleasant life comes not from feasting and indulgence but from sober reasoning that examines the grounds of every choice and clears away the false beliefs that disturb the mind.
passages.text: For it is not continued drinkings and revels, or the enjoyment of female society, or feasts of fish and other such things, as a costly table supplies, that make life pleasant, but sober contemplation, which examines into the reasons for all choice and avoidance, and which puts to flight the vain opinions from which the greater part of the confusion arises which troubles the soul.

passages.g: Practical wisdom is the root of every other virtue and worth more even than philosophy, because one cannot live pleasantly without living wisely, honorably, and justly, nor the reverse.
passages.text: Now, the beginning and the greatest good of all these things is prudence, on which account prudence is something more valuable than even philosophy, inasmuch as all the other virtues spring from it, teaching us that it is not possible to live pleasantly unless one also lives prudently, and honourably, and justly; and that one cannot live prudently, and honestly, and justly, without living pleasantly; for the virtues are connate with living agreeably, and living agreeably is inseparable from the virtues.

passages.g: The best life belongs to whoever holds sound views about the divine, fears death not at all, understands the limits nature sets, sees that the greatest good is easily obtained and the greatest evil short-lived, and rejects fate by recognizing that fortune is unstable while our own will is free.
passages.text: Since, who can you think better than that man who has holy opinions respecting the Gods, and who is utterly fearless with respect to death, and who has properly contemplated the end of nature, and who comprehends that the chief good is easily perfected and easily provided; and the greatest evil lasts but a short period, and causes but brief pain. And who has no belief in necessity, which is set up by some as the mistress of all things, but he refers some things to fortune, some to ourselves, because necessity is an irresponsible power, and because he sees that fortune is unstable, while our own will is free;

passages.g: Freedom is what makes us responsible and open to praise and blame, and believing in gods who might be appeased is less enslaving than submitting to an inexorable determinism.
passages.text: and this freedom constitutes, in our case, a responsibility which makes us encounter blame and praise. Since it would be better to follow the fables about the Gods than to be a slave to the fate of the natural philosopher; for the fables which are told give us a sketch, as if we could avert the wrath of God by paying him honour; but the other presents us with necessity who is inexorable.

passages.g: Fortune is neither a goddess nor a dependable cause but merely supplies the raw materials of great goods and evils, and it is better to fail while acting reasonably than to succeed by accident.
passages.text: And he, not thinking fortune a goddess, as the generality esteem her (for nothing is done at random by a God), nor a cause which no man can rely on, for he thinks that good or evil is not given by her to men so as to make them live happily, but that the principles of great goods or great evils are supplied by her; thinking it better to be unfortunate in accordance with reason, than to be fortunate irrationally; for that those actions which are judged to be the best, are rightly done in consequence of reason.

passages.g: Practicing these principles constantly, alone and in conversation with like-minded people, frees the mind from disturbance waking and sleeping and lets a person live among mortals like an immortal.
passages.text: Do you then study these precepts, and those which are akin to them, by all means day and night, pondering on them by yourself, and discussing them with any one like yourself, and then you will never be disturbed by either sleeping or waking fancies, but you will live like a God among men; for a man living amid immortal Gods, is in no respect like a mortal being.

passages.g: Divination has no reality, and even if it did, whatever it foretold would be nothing to us.
passages.text: In other works, he discards divination; and also in his Little Epitome. And he says divination has no existence; but, if it has any, still we should think that what happens according to it is nothing to us.

passages.g: These conclusions about how to live are set out at greater length elsewhere.
passages.text: These are his sentiments about the things which concern the life of man, and he has discussed them at greater length elsewhere.

passages.g: Whatever is perfectly happy and imperishable neither suffers trouble nor inflicts it, and therefore feels neither anger nor gratitude, since both of those belong to weakness.
passages.text: That which is happy and imperishable, neither has trouble itself, nor does it cause it to anything; so that it is not subject to the feelings of either anger or gratitude; for these feelings only exist in what is weak.

passages.g: Death is nothing to us, because what has been dissolved has no sensation, and what has no sensation is nothing to us.
passages.text: Death is nothing to us; for that which is dissolved is devoid of sensation, and that which is devoid of sensation is nothing to us.

passages.g: Pleasure reaches its limit at the removal of everything that causes pain, and wherever pleasure is present pain is absent for as long as it lasts.
passages.text: The limit of the greatness of the pleasures is the removal of everything which can give pain. And where pleasure is, as long as it lasts, that which gives pain, or that which feels pain, or both of them, are absent.

passages.g: Intense bodily pain is always brief, pain that only slightly outweighs pleasure does not last long, and chronic illness holds more pleasure in it than pain.
passages.text: Pain does not abide continuously in the flesh, but in its extremity it is present only a very short time. That pain which only just exceeds the pleasure in the flesh, does not last many days. But long diseases have in them more that is pleasant than painful to the flesh.

passages.g: It is impossible to live pleasantly without living wisely, honorably, and justly, and impossible to live wisely, honorably, and justly without living pleasantly.
passages.text: It is not possible to live pleasantly without living prudently, and honourably, and justly; nor to live prudently, and honourably, and justly, without living pleasantly. But he to whom it does not happen to live prudently, honourably, and justly, cannot possibly live pleasantly.

passages.g: People pursue power and eminence not because ruling is good in itself but to feel safe from other people, so such a life succeeds only if it actually delivers that safety and otherwise fails at the very thing it was sought for.
passages.text: For the sake of feeling confidence and security with regard to men, and not with reference to the nature of government and kingly power being a good, some men have wished to be eminent and powerful, in order that others might attain this feeling by their means; thinking that so they would secure safety as far as men are concerned. So that, if the life of such men is safe, they have attained to the nature of good; but if it is not safe, then they have failed in obtaining that for the sake of which they originally desired power according to the order of nature.

passages.g: No pleasure is bad in itself, but the things that produce certain pleasures bring disturbances that outweigh them.
passages.text: No pleasure is intrinsically bad: but the efficient causes of some pleasures bring with them a great many perturbations of pleasure.

passages.g: If every pleasure were equally intense, equally lasting, and felt throughout the whole body, no pleasure would be any better or worse than another.
passages.text: If every pleasure were condensed, if one may so say, and if each lasted long, and affected the whole body, or the essential parts of it, then there would be no difference between one pleasure and another.

passages.g: If indulgence actually freed people from fear of the heavens, of death, and of pain, and taught them where desire should stop, there would be no ground for criticizing those who devote themselves entirely to pleasure.
passages.text: If those things which make the pleasures of debauched men, put an end to the fears of the mind, and to those which arise about the heavenly bodies, and death, and pain; and if they taught us what ought to be the limit of our desires, we should have no pretence for blaming those who wholly devote themselves to pleasure, and who never feel any pain or grief (which is the chief evil) from any quarter.

passages.g: The study of nature would be unnecessary if we were not disturbed by fear of the heavens, fear of death, and ignorance about the limits of pain and desire.
passages.text: If apprehensions relating to the heavenly bodies did not disturb us, and if the terrors of death have no concern with us, and if we had the courage to contemplate the boundaries of pain and of the desires, we should have no need of physiological studies.

passages.g: No one can be free of fear about ultimate things without understanding how the universe actually works, so untroubled pleasure depends on knowledge of nature.
passages.text: It would not be possible for a person to banish all fear about those things which are called most essential, unless he knew what is the nature of the universe, or if he had any idea that the fables told about it could be true; and therefore, it is, that a person cannot enjoy unmixed pleasure without physiological knowledge.

passages.g: Safety from other people is worth nothing to someone still terrified of the cosmos.
passages.text: It would be no good for a man to secure himself safety as far as men are concerned, while in a state of apprehension as to all the heavenly bodies, and those under the earth, and in short, all those in the infinite.

passages.g: Power and wealth can secure a degree of protection from other people, but lasting security comes from a tranquil mind and freedom from ambition.
passages.text: Irresistible power and great wealth may, up to a certain point, give us security as far as men are concerned; but the security of men in general depends upon the tranquillity of their souls, and their freedom from ambition.

passages.g: What nature requires is limited and easy to obtain, while imagined wants can never be satisfied.
passages.text: The riches of nature are defined and easily procurable; but vain desires are insatiable.

passages.g: The wise person owes little to luck, because reason secures the greatest goods and keeps them available for a lifetime.
passages.text: The wise man is but little favoured by fortune; but his reason procures him the greatest and most valuable goods, and these he does enjoy, and will enjoy the whole of his life.

passages.g: The just person is the least troubled of all, while the unjust one lives in constant anxiety.
passages.text: The just man is the freest of all men from disquietude; but the unjust man is a perpetual prey to it.

passages.g: Once the pain of lack has been removed, bodily pleasure cannot be increased, only varied.
passages.text: Pleasure in the flesh is not increased, when once the pain arising from want is removed; it is only diversified.

passages.g: The deepest peace of mind comes from thinking clearly about the things that frighten us most.
passages.text: The most perfect happiness of the soul depends on these reflections, and on opinions of a similar character on all those questions which cause the greatest alarm to the mind.

passages.g: Measured by reason rather than duration, a finite life holds as much pleasure as an endless one.
passages.text: Infinite and finite time both have equal pleasure, if any one measures its limits by reason.

passages.g: Only if the body could feel unlimited pleasure would eternity be worth wanting.
passages.text: If the flesh could experience boundless pleasure, it would want to dispose of eternity.

passages.g: Understanding that the body ends releases us from fear of eternity and makes a finite life complete, so that a person in this state can leave life without loss.
passages.text: But reason, enabling us to conceive the end and dissolution of the body, and liberating us from the fears relative to eternity, procures for us all the happiness of which life is capable, so completely that we have no further occasion to include eternity in our desires. In this disposition of mind, man is happy even when his troubles engage him to quit life; and to die thus, is for him only to interrupt a life of happiness.

passages.g: Whoever knows the limits of life sees that what ends want and completes life is easily obtained, so nothing hard-won is needed.
passages.text: He who is acquainted with the limits of life knows, that that which removes the pain which arises from want, and which makes the whole of life perfect, is easily procurable; so that he has no need of those things which can only be attained with trouble.

passages.g: We must hold our conception of life's ultimate goal to the same standard of evidence we use for everything else, or all judgment dissolves into confusion.
passages.text: But as to the subsisting end, we ought to consider it with all the clearness and evidence which we refer to whatever we think and believe; otherwise, all things will be full of confusion and uncertainty of judgment.

passages.g: Reject the senses altogether and you destroy the only standard by which you could have judged them false.
passages.text: If you resist all the senses, you will not even have anything left to which you can refer, or by which you may be able to judge of the falsehood of the senses which you condemn.

passages.g: Dismissing one sense without distinguishing raw sensation from the inferences built on it corrupts the others and leaves no criterion of truth at all.
passages.text: If you simply discard one sense, and do not distinguish between the different elements of the judgment, so as to know on the one hand, the induction which goes beyond the actual sensation, or, on the other, the actual and immediate notion; the affections, and all the conceptions of the mind which lean directly on the sensible representation, you will be imputing trouble into the other sense, and destroying in that quarter every species of criterion.

passages.g: Treating unverified inferences as equal in authority to immediate certainties guarantees error, because it mixes doubtful opinion with reliable knowledge.
passages.text: If you allow equal authority to the ideas, which, being only inductive, require to be verified, and to those which bear about them an immediate certainty, you will not escape error; for you will be confounding doubtful opinions with those which are not doubtful, and true judgments with those of a different character.

passages.g: If we do not measure each action against life's ultimate aim, our conduct will contradict what we claim to believe.
passages.text: If, on every occasion, we do not refer every one of our actions to the chief end of nature, if we turn aside from that to seek or avoid some other object, there will be a want of agreement between our words and our actions.

passages.g: Of everything wisdom provides for a happy life, friendship is by far the most important.
passages.text: Of all the things which wisdom provides for the happiness of the whole life, by far the most important is the acquisition of friendship.

passages.g: Understanding life's limits shows that no evil lasts long and that friendship is the surest protection available within a lifetime.
passages.text: The same opinion encourages man to trust that no evil will be everlasting, or even of long duration; as it sees that, in the space of life allotted to us, the protection of friendship is most sure and trustworthy.

passages.g: Desires divide into those that are natural and necessary, those natural but unnecessary, and those rooted in nothing but vain opinion.
passages.text: Of the desires, some are natural and necessary, some natural, but not necessary, and some are neither natural nor necessary, but owe their existence to vain opinions.” (Epicurus thinks that those are natural and necessary which put an end to pains, as drink when one is thirsty; and that those are natural but not necessary which only diversify pleasure, but do not remove pain, such as expensive food; and that these are neither natural nor necessary, which are such as crowns, or the erection of statues.)

passages.g: A desire whose frustration causes no pain is unnecessary, and can be silenced whenever satisfying it would be difficult or harmful.
passages.text: Those desires which do not lead to pain, if they are not satisfied, are not necessary. It is easy to impose silence on them when they appear difficult to gratify, or likely to produce injury.

passages.g: When a desire is violent even though frustrating it would cause no pain, its intensity comes from illusion rather than from nature.
passages.text: When the natural desires, the failing to satisfy which is, nevertheless, not painful, are violent and obstinate, it is a proof that there is an admixture of vain opinion in them; for then energy does not arise from their own nature, but from the vain opinions of men.

passages.g: Justice is a mutual agreement not to harm and not to be harmed.
passages.text: Natural justice is a covenant of what is suitable, leading men to avoid injuring one another, and being injured.

passages.g: Beings and groups incapable of making such an agreement have neither justice nor injustice.
passages.text: Those animals which are unable to enter into an argument of this nature, or to guard against doing or sustaining mutual injury, have no such thing as justice or injustice. And the case is the same with those nations, the members of which are either unwilling or unable to enter into a covenant to respect their mutual interests.

passages.g: Justice has no independent existence; it arises wherever people actually agree not to harm one another.
passages.text: Justice has no independent existence; it results from mutual contracts, and establishes itself wherever there is a mutual engagement to guard against doing or sustaining mutual injury.

passages.g: Wrongdoing is not bad in itself, but only because of the fear of being caught and punished.
passages.text: Injustice is not intrinsically bad; it has this character only because there is joined with it a fear of not escaping those who are appointed to punish actions marked with that character.

passages.g: Someone who secretly breaks the social agreement can never be confident of escaping detection, no matter how often he has escaped before.
passages.text: It is not possible for a man who secretly does anything in contravention of the agreement which men have made with one another, to guard against doing, or sustaining mutual injury, to believe that he shall always escape notice, even if he have escaped notice already ten thousand times; for, till his death, it is uncertain whether he will not be detected.

passages.g: Justice is everywhere the same in principle, since mutual society is advantageous, but its particular content varies with circumstances.
passages.text: In a general point of view, justice is the same thing to every one; for there is something advantageous in mutual society. Nevertheless, the difference of place, and divers other circumstances, make justice vary.

passages.g: A law becomes genuinely just the moment it proves useful to people's dealings with one another, whether or not everyone recognizes this.
passages.text: From the moment that a thing declared just by the law is generally recognized as useful for the mutual relations of men, it becomes really just, whether it is universally regarded as such or not.

passages.g: A law that does not serve mutual advantage is not just, and one that stops being useful stops being just, though it was truly just while it served.
passages.text: But if, on the contrary, a thing established by law is not really useful for the social relations, then it is not just; and if that which was just, inasmuch as it was useful, loses this character, after having been for some time considered so, it is not less true that, during that time, it was really just, at least for those who do not perplex themselves about vain words, but who prefer, in every case, examining and judging for themselves.

passages.g: A rule that conflicts with reason was never really just, and a rule that circumstances render useless ceases to be just at that moment.
passages.text: When, without any fresh circumstances arising, a thing which has been declared just in practice does not agree with the impressions of reason, that is a proof that the thing was not really just. In the same way, when in consequence of new circumstances, a thing which has been pronounced just does not any longer appear to agree with utility, the thing which was just, inasmuch as it was useful to the social relations and intercourse of mankind, ceases to be just the moment when it ceases to be useful.

passages.g: To live free of fear of others, make friends where you can, avoid making enemies where you cannot, and keep your distance from those you can do neither with.
passages.text: He who desires to live tranquilly without having any thing to fear from other men, ought to make himself friends; those whom he cannot make friends of, he should, at least, avoid rendering enemies; and if that is not in his power, he should, as far as possible, avoid all intercourse with them, and keep them aloof, as far as it is for his interest to do so.

passages.g: The happiest people are those with nothing to fear from anyone near them; they live in mutual trust and full friendship, and do not grieve a friend's early death as a calamity.
passages.text: The happiest men are they who have arrived at the point of having nothing to fear from those who surround them. Such men live with one another most agreeably, having the firmest grounds of confidence in one another, enjoying the advantages of friendship in all their fulness, and not lamenting, as a pitiable circumstance, the premature death of their friends.

passages.g: Some things operate on a scale so vast that ordinary experience provides no measure for them.
passages.text: In the northern ocean there is a fish, called the Leviathan, many thousand li in size. This leviathan changes into a bird, called the Rukh, whose back is many thousand li in breadth. With a mighty effort it rises, and its wings obscure the sky like clouds. At the equinox, this bird prepares to start for the southern ocean, the Celestial Lake. And in the Record of Marvels we read that when the rukh flies southwards, the water is smitten for a space of three thousand li around, while the bird itself mounts upon a typhoon to a height of ninety thousand li, for a flight of six months' duration.

passages.g: Perspective is relative: what looks immense from below looks trivial from far enough away, and nothing can be supported by a medium too shallow for it.
passages.text: Just so are the motes in a sunbeam blown aloft by God. For whether the blue of the sky is its real colour, or only the result of distance without end, the effect to the bird looking down would be just the same as to the motes. Distance being relative. The rukh at an altitude of 90,000 li (three li to a mile) is no more than a mote in a sunbeam a few feet from the ground. If there is not sufficient depth, water will not float large ships. Upset a cupful into a small hole, and a mustard-seed will be your boat. Try to float the cup, and it will stick, from the disproportion between water and vessel.

passages.g: Great undertakings require correspondingly great conditions, and small creatures, judging by their own capacities, find such undertakings absurd.
passages.text: So with air. If there is not a sufficient depth, it cannot support large birds. And for this bird a depth of ninety thousand li is necessary; and then, with nothing save the clear sky above, and no obstacle in the way, it starts upon its journey to the south. A cicada laughed, and said to a young dove, "Now, when I fly with all my might, 'tis as much as I can do to get from tree to tree. And sometimes I do not reach, but fall to the ground midway. What then can be the use of going up ninety thousand li in order to start for the south?"

passages.g: Preparation must match the scale of the journey, and small knowledge cannot encompass great knowledge any more than a short life can imagine a long one.
passages.text: He who goes to Mang-ts'ang, A short distance into the country. taking three meals with him, comes back with his stomach as full as when he started. But he who travels a hundred li must grind flour enough for a night's halt. And he who travels a thousand li must supply himself with provisions for three months. Those two little creatures, — what should they know? Small knowledge has not the compass of great knowledge any more than a short year has the length of a long year. How can we tell that this is so? The mushroom of a morning knows not the alternation of day and night. The chrysalis knows not the alternation of spring and autumn. Theirs are short years.

passages.g: Every span of life seems long or short only relative to the observer, and even the longest is envied by those with less.
passages.text: But in the State of Ch'u there is a tortoise whose spring and autumn are each of five hundred years' duration. And in former days there was a large tree which had a spring and autumn each of eight thousand years' duration. Yet, P'êng Tsu The Methusaleh of China. His age has not been agreed upon by Chinese writers, but the lowest computation gives him a life of eight hundred years. is still, alas! an object of envy to all. It was on this very subject that the Emperor T'ang spoke to Chi, as follows: — "At the barren north there is a great sea, the Celestial Lake. In it there is a fish, several thousand li in breadth, and I know not how many in length. It is called the Leviathan.

passages.g: Some beings exist on a scale to which ordinary comparisons simply do not apply.
passages.text: There is also a bird, called the Rukh, with a back like Mount T'ai,

passages.g: The small mock what they cannot conceive, judging the purposes of others by the limits of their own reach.
passages.text: China's most famous mountain, situated in the province of Shantung. and wings like clouds across the sky. Upon a typhoon it soars up to a height of ninety thousand li, beyond the clouds and atmosphere, with only the clear sky above it. And then it directs its flight towards the south pole. "A quail laughed, and said: Pray, what may that creature be going to do? I rise but a few yards in the air, and settle again after flying around among the reeds. That is the most I can manage. Now, where ever can this creature be going to?"

passages.g: Someone who fills a small role well takes his own narrow scale as the measure of everything, just as the quail measures the great bird by its own short flight.
passages.text: The repetition of this story, coupled with its quotation from the Record of Marvels, is considered to give an air of authenticity to Chuang Tzŭ's illustration, which the reader might otherwise suppose to be of his own invention. Such, indeed, is the difference between small and great. Take, for instance, a man who creditably fills some small office, or who is a pattern of virtue in his neighbourhood, or who influences his prince to right government of the State, — his opinion of himself will be much the same as that quail's. The philosopher Yung laughs at such a one.

passages.g: A person is admirable when the world's praise cannot inflate him nor its blame shake him, because he can tell what is intrinsically his from what is merely conferred; but even this falls short of true freedom.
passages.text: He, if the whole world flattered him, would not be affected thereby, nor if the whole world blamed him would he lose his faith in himself. For Yung can distinguish between the intrinsic and the extrinsic, between honour and shame, — and such men are rare in their generation. But even he has not established himself.

passages.g: Even extraordinary powers leave a person dependent on something outside himself.
passages.text: Beyond the limits of an external world. His achievements are after all only of the earth, earthy. There was Lieh Tzŭ again. A personage of whom nothing is really known. He is considered by the best authorities to have been of Chuang Tzŭ's own creation. This, however, did not prevent some enterprising scholar, probably of the Han dynasty, from discovering a treatise which still passes under Lieh Tzŭ's name. He could ride upon the wind, and travel whithersoever he wished, staying away as long as fifteen days. Among mortals who attain happiness, such a man is rare. Yet although Lieh Tzŭ was able to dispense with walking, he was still dependent upon something.

passages.g: Complete freedom would depend on nothing at all, and belongs to the person who has let go of self, of striving, and of reputation.
passages.text: Sc. the wind. But had he been charioted upon the eternal fitness of Heaven and Earth, driving before him the elements as his team while roaming through the realms of For-Ever, — upon what, then, would he have had to depend? That is, nourished upon the doctrines of inaction, the continuity of life and death, etc., which will be dealt with in later chapters. Thus it has been said, "The perfect man ignores self; the divine man ignores action; the true Sage ignores reputation." His — for the three are one — is a bliss "beyond all that the minstrel has told." Material existences melt into thin air;
